File.Name: d422h-d421-046.jpg
Notes: 8673923204 This is looking out over the foggy Santa Barbara channel towards Anacapa Island in the background, with a lone paraglider hanging over the native fire-scarred Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family along a rocky ridge near the top of the Tunnel Trail. This is a regular launch place for the paragliders. Looks exhilarating, and maybe quiet and peaceful - but too exciting for me! (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d423a-d422_043.jpg
Notes:
8675941599 This is a new flower of our native Stream Orchid (Epipactis gigantea) in the Orchidaceae plant family. It's just starting to flower in a place along the river that always has water. The flowers point down, but I gently tucked the flower stem under another plant to get this photo. It's not rare in our mountains, but I'm always happy and surprised to find them in flower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

It was a gloomy morning and a windy afternoon, so I took the day off from taking photos. I needed a catch-up day anyway after our telephone/DSL outage on Sunday.

File.Name: d423b-d422_057.jpg
Notes: 8677018570 These are dreamy new flowers of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, just starting to flower on a dry flat near the river. It's a stand-up shrub, but it does have climbing tendencies given the opportunity. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

File.Name: d423c-d422_009.jpg
Notes: 8675870835 These are new flowers of the Elegant Clarkia (Clarkia unguiculata) in the Onagraceae plant family. The petals look like they've been cut with scissors. It's another Farewell-to-Spring - a sign that our dry summer is approaching, too fast! The flowers look a bit bedraggled by the dry weather we've been having all this calendar year. These are the first I've seen this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

File.Name: d423d-d422_014.jpg
Notes: 8676946410 This is a different Grasshopper in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera on the dry flood plain of the Santa Ynez River. It looks to me like a Band-winged Grasshopper (subfamily Oedipodinae), but I don't really know. It has interesting yellow tibia - the lower legs. As it flew off, I had the impression of pale greenish wings. Alice reports that it's a Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis), so I was on the right track. Thanks again Alice! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

File.Name: d423e-d422_017.jpg
Notes:
8676898862 I found this striking little Grasshopper in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera on a leaf growing on the dry flood plain of the Santa Ynez River. It's rather small, not much more than an inch long. It seems to be an adult with wings, but it might be a late-stage instar. It got away from me before I could see it clearly.

Alice reports that it's a 5th instar Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis). Thanks again Alice! I'm struck by how different it looks from the critter in my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

File.Name: d423f-d422_018.jpg
Notes:
8675769063 Here are brand new flowers of native Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) in the old Scrophulariaceae plant family, flowering near the river. Now they are moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. Those darned botanists! They are beautiful plants, but they might be scarce in this dry year. I figure I better get photos while I can! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

You can see the curled up stamens inside the top-right flower.

File.Name: d423g-d422_030.jpg
Notes: 8676852762 Here are new flowers of native Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing on the dry flood plain of the Santa Ynez River. The aromatic leaves of this fine shrub make healing tea, incense, and a smoke that lives up to its common name. I really like this plant! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

File.Name: d423h-d422_046.jpg
Notes: 8676817058 This is a rather large tumor-like gall growing on a stem of the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family. The plant has long pinnate leaves. I can find no information about this gall in my books or on the web. This one plant had several of them, but I haven't noticed them on any other plants. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

File.Name: d424a_d424_003.jpg
Notes:
8679856666 These are drizzle-drops on a cultivar of native Douglas iris (Iris douglasiana) in the Iridaceae plant family growing on the dry edge of our garden. Rain knocks the water drops off, but drizzle is just right. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2013)

It was drizzly and gloomy all day today. We actually ended up with 0.08 inches of rain in our gauge! But it was breezy as well as drippy, so there was not much opportunity for photos and I had to kick up the ISO for this one. I'll be camping this weekend on the Porter Ranch in the Huasna Valley, so I hope the weather clears up some!

File.Name: d424b_d422_002.jpg
Notes: 8679769774 This is looking close at the head of a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis), showing the "third eye" that these lizards have. It's also called a "parietal eye" or "pineal eye". It appears to have a lens. As I understand, this third eye distinguishes light and dark, and has something to do with controlling body temperature and daily rhythms. It's associated with the pineal gland, which we have too, buried deep in our heads. It's so interesting that Descartes picked the pineal gland (our third eye, somewhat buried) as the seat of the soul. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2013)

File.Name: d424c_d421_041.jpg
Notes: 8678629741 This Grasshopper in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera sure looks like the Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis) that I posted yesterday, but I'm willing to be wrong! I'm sure it's a Band-winged Grasshopper in the subfamily Oedipodinae. I got this photo in a very different place than the [Previous] one - high in the mountains, but a similar sandy habitat. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d424d_d422-052.jpg
Notes: 8679708596 I guess these are yellow flowers of the beautiful Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. I got this photo near the river, but the flowers are usually more red in the mountains near home. Regional variation? Or just variation plain and simple? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

File.Name: d424e_d421-029.jpg
Notes: 8679695710 I guess this is native Bishop's Lotus (Lotus strigosus) in the Fabaceae plant family. "Strigose" is one of those botanical words that means "with stiff, straight, sharp, appressed hairs". You can see strigose hairs on both the leaves and pea-like seed pods. This plant has been scarce in this dry year. (Santa Ynez Mountains, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d424f_d421-055.jpg
Notes: 8679657940 I believe this is a White-flowering Current (Ribes indecorum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It looks very similar to the more common Chaparral Currant (R. malvaceum) - but it seems more compact, it grows higher in the mountains on mostly north-facing slopes, and it does have pale almost white flowers which open later in the season. I've seen this before and never thought of it as a different species, but here it is. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d424g_d422_006.jpg
Notes:
8678499547 On the left is a blade of a native grass, probably California Brome (Bromus carinatus) in the Poaceae plant family, which we've been encouraging in this place. On the right is a not-quite-flowering native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's surprising how similar the stems look! The grass has some pubescence and the leaves are slightly different at the base, but you have to look close to see any difference at all. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2013)

That's a non-native Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family that flowering on the right.
CommonNames: California Brome; Fairy Lantern; Common Bedstraw
ScientificName: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae
associatedTaxa: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae

File.Name: d424h_d421_003.jpg
Notes: 8679588880 This is new foliage of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family - one of our most fabulous summer flowers. It won't flower until June, but I'm happy to see the plants looking so good. It's such a dry year, I've been worried about our summer flowers. See my photo of the fantastic flowers from last year. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 April 2013)

File.Name: d425a_d425_002.jpg
Notes:
8681456575 Here are raindrops on new flowers of Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family - reflecting the woods. Usually the wind after a storm blows off the drops, but the wind held off for a moment this morning - before the clouds and wind came back! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2013)

I've been preoccupied today getting ready for my camping trip this weekend on the Porter Ranch in the Huasna Valley - but flowers, slime molds, mushrooms and insects isn't bad! I'll be back on Sunday afternoon, but probably wont post any photos until Monday.

File.Name: d425b_d425_016.jpg
Notes:
8681400999 Looks like a torn-open vacuum cleaner sack!

This is the same "False Puffball" (Reticularia lycoperdon) in the fascinating Myxomycetes or Myxogastria group of Slime Molds that I showed a week ago. Now it's releasing a dust storm of spores. The spores will hatch into single-celled organisms, not fungi. The filamentous material inside the spore mass is the capillatum of the slime mold. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2013)

I give thanks to Myxonz for setting me straight about what critter this is. His time lapse movies of a slime mold running a maze here and here are astounding!

File.Name: d425c_d425_018.jpg
Notes: 8681372365 This is a little dark-spored Inky Cap (genus Coprinus) mushroom growing in our parking area. It was slender enough to wiggle in the breeze today. Mushrooms in this genus "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime. These mushrooms are ephemeral - in with a drizzle and gone with the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2013)

File.Name: d425d_d425_008.jpg
Notes: 8682469290 This is a pretty golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) preparing for take off on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's grooming itself. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2013)

File.Name: d425e_d425_010.jpg
Notes: 8681316985 This is just a fly... I don't know what kind of Fly (order Diptera) it is, probably family Muscidae, but it's bristly enough to suggest family Tachinidae. I love photographing flies even when I don't know what they are - most of the time. They have so much structural detail! (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2013)

File.Name: d425f_d422_033.jpg
Notes: 8681292815 These are tiny staminate (male) flowers of native Narrowleaf Bedstraw (Galium angustifolium) in the Rubiaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see the widely-spaced whorled leaves on the stems. Galium plants are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. This one is a proper sub-shrub, not a climber like my [Previous] photo. The staminate plants look yellow, but the female pistillate plants look white. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 April 2013)

File.Name: d425g_d413_003.jpg
Notes:
8681261595 These are tiny new flowers of Galium nuttallii in the Rubiacae plant family. Everything comes in fours with these tiny flowers on the whorled plant stems. Galium plants are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. This one has slightly woody stems and climbs up into other plants. I really like this genus. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2013)

I've been sitting on this photo for a while, but it seems right to post it along with my [Next] photo of a different Galium species.

File.Name: d425h_d422_007.jpg
Notes:
8681208129 I learned something today...

This little weed growing above our retaining wall always reminded me of Henbit aka Giraffe's Head (Lamium amplexicaule) in the Lamiaceae plant family, without the pretty flowers. I did some research today, and discovered it IS Lamium amplexicaule, but these plants have cleistogamous flowers that never open. They do produce seeds. If you do a web search on "Lamium amplexicaule cleistogamous" you'll get lots of hits. This plant is well known for cleistogamy. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2013)

It's curious that these cleistogamous plants grow above the wall, but we have normally flowering plants below the wall. I don't know why.
ScientificName: Lamium amplexicaule; Lamiaceae; Lamium amplexicaule; cleistogamous
associatedTaxa: Lamium amplexicaule; Lamiaceae; Lamium amplexicaule; cleistogamous

File.Name: d429a_d427_016.jpg
Notes:
8693974199 This is a grand old Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family growing in our canyon last weekend. A brother asked me how old these tree are, and I had to say I don't know. But judging from the slow growth of saplings, I have to guess they are hundreds of years old. That means that Chumash kids and grizzly bears might have once sheltered here. (Porter Ranch, Huasna Rancho, San Luis Obispo County, 27 April 2013)

The grasses under the tree look good from a distance, but they are mostly weedy non-native grasses up close. That's the way all this ranch land is. Combine this with the drought year, and the wildflowers are scarce. But I found a few!

I was camped on the Huasna Rancho this weekend, on the edge of the Huasna Valley. The original Huasna Ranch was founded as a Mexican land grant by Isaac Sparks in 1843. The property been split since then, but much of it is still in the family. It's a fabulous place.
CommonNames: Valley Oak; Huasna Rancho; Huasna Valley

File.Name: d429b_d427_027.jpg
Notes:
8695043924 You may have to squint to see this, but here are seedheads of (I'm pretty sure) native Purple Needlegrass (Nassella pulchra) in the Poaceae plant family. I found a colony of these hiding in the non-native grasses near the top of our canyon last weekend. It's good to know that the native grasses are making a stand against the non-native grasses. (Porter Ranch, Huasna Rancho, San Luis Obispo County, 27 April 2013)

The native grass is hard to make out in my photo, but they are even harder to notice in the grassland!

File.Name: d429c_d428_155.jpg
Notes:
8693819421 These damn non-native grasses have taken over the valleys. The brown seedhead left of center is Foxtail Barley (Hordeum jubatum); on the right of that is Italian Rye-grass (Lolium multiflorum); and all the rest is Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) or some other Bromus. All are in the Poaceae plant family. Missing from this photo is Soft Chess or Cheat Grass (Bromus horedeaceus). It's hard to photograph these non-native grasses when they grow everywhere! (Porter Ranch, Huasna Rancho, San Luis Obispo County, 27 April 2013)

Fortunately the stickery seeds that collect in socks and shoes were not quite ripe, so I didn't need my weed-gaiters. But give it a week or two...
CommonNames: Foxtail Barley; Italian Rye-grass; Ripgut Grass; Soft Chess; Cheat Grass
ScientificName: Hordeum jubatum; Lolium multiflorum; Bromus diandrus; Bromus; Poaceae; Bromus horedeaceus
associatedTaxa: Hordeum jubatum; Lolium multiflorum; Bromus diandrus; Bromus; Poaceae; Bromus horedeaceus

File.Name: d429d_d427_019.jpg
Notes: 8693758275 I believe these are pretty Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus) in the Fabaceae plant family. They are making a stand with the non-native grasses in this valley, probably Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) or some other Bromus in the Poaceae plant family. The non-native grasses control this valley, and the wildflowers have to struggle to find a place. (Porter Ranch, Huasna Rancho, San Luis Obispo County, 27 April 2013)
ScientificName: Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae; Bromus diandrus; Bromus; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae; Bromus diandrus; Bromus; Poaceae

File.Name: d429e_d427_035.jpg
Notes: 8694847630 I believe this is the species Winecup Clarkia (Clarkia purpurea ssp. purpurea) in the Onagraceaeplant family. We have the ssp. quadrivulnera at home - see this recent photo. This is another of the wildflowers hiding in the non-native grasses. (Porter Ranch, Huasna Rancho, San Luis Obispo County, 27 April 2013)

File.Name: d429e_d428_154.jpg
Notes:
8693648135 This is native Soap Plant (Chenopodium californicum) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. The Chumash dug and scrapped the roots for use in washing hair, skin, and clothing. This plant is hiding in the weedy grasses along with Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia intermedia) and a Popcorn Flower (probably Plagiobothrys nothofulvus) in the Boraginaceae plant family. There were other wildflowers hiding in the non-native range grasses, but I had to hunt for them! (Porter Ranch, Huasna Rancho, San Luis Obispo County, 27 April 2013)

The other native Soap Plant is the monocot Chlorogalum pomeridium in the Liliaceae plant family. I'll have photos soon, I hope!
CommonNames: Soap Plant; Common Fiddleneck; Popcorn Flower; Soap Plant
ScientificName: Chenopodium californicum; Chenopodiaceae; Amsinkia intermedia; Plagiobothrys nothofulvus; Boraginaceae; Chlorogalum pomeridium; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Chenopodium californicum; Chenopodiaceae; Amsinkia intermedia; Plagiobothrys nothofulvus; Boraginaceae; Chlorogalum pomeridium; Liliaceae

File.Name: d429f_d427_039.jpg
Notes:
8694704032 I'm not sure, but I believe this is a flower of Indian Tobacco (Nicotiana quadrivalvis) in the Solanaceae plant family, with sessile leaves. The flowers close during the day, but I got this photo just as the morning sun was upon it. A number of these plants were growing by the drain of our camp kitchen, where the water goes. (Porter Ranch, Huasna Rancho, San Luis Obispo County, 27 April 2013)

Yes, it's real native tobacco. I wish I'd remembered to collect some before I left!

File.Name: d429g_d427_022.jpg
Notes:
8694630920 This sure looks like the black fungus encrusted "oak apple" gall of a California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera) - but it's growing on a flowering twig of native Redberry (Rhamnus crocea) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family rather than an oak! There's also a bit of Lace Lichen (aka California Spanish Moss and Treebeard Lichen, my namesake!) (Ramalina menziesii) in the photo. Did a gall wasp get confused? It seems to have taken, whatever happened. (Porter Ranch, Huasna Rancho, San Luis Obispo County, 27 April 2013)

We have the native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub at home, but not this shrub with smaller and less oak-like leaves.
CommonNames: California Gall Wasp; Redberry; Lace Lichen; California Spanish Moss; Treebeard Lichen; Holly-Leaved Redberry
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Rhamnus crocea; Rhamnaceae; Ramalina menziesii; Rhamnus ilicifolia
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Rhamnus crocea; Rhamnaceae; Ramalina menziesii; Rhamnus ilicifolia

File.Name: d430a_d430-016.jpg
Notes:
8697816884 This is the next stage of a beautiful caterpillar of the Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin - with drizzle drops. Compare my [Previous] photo. This one is perched on the lip of my plexiglass rain gauge on the shaky upstairs porch. It can't munch plastic, so I moved it back to an oak tree and I noticed it an hour later happily munching an oak leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2013)

It was a crummy day for photos - gloomy and breezy, with a bit of drizzle. But I tried and got a few. It was a nice respite from the heat that's forecast for the next few days - in the 90s by Friday!

File.Name: d430b_d430-007.jpg
Notes: 8697796264 I believe this is a tiny caterpillar of the Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin - with drizzle drops. It's on new growth of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. These early-stage caterpillars look quite different from the later stage. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2013)
ScientificName: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d430c_d430-014.jpg
Notes:
8696627477 I found this beautiful little Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. I have no idea what kind of wasp it is. It seems to have little yellow horns, and look at its brilliant green tibia, like a glow stick! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2013)

It was a crummy day for photos - gloomy and breezy, with a bit of drizzle. But I tried.

File.Name: d430d_d429-052.jpg
Notes:
8696600139 Here is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working a new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2013)

I'm a little worried about these butterflies because I haven't seen a single caterpillar on the Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - see this photo. A drought year is tough on everyone.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Canyon Sunflower; Climbing Penstemon
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: d430e_d429-001.jpg
Notes: 8696571383 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is striking a pose on a petal of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has swollen pedipalps - those little "legs" below its head - so I reckon this is a male spider with mating on its mind. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2013)

File.Name: d430f_d429-008.jpg
Notes: 8696533995 This is a Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. This bee appears to collect pollen on its abdomen, so I guess it's in the family Megachilidae. It also seems to have pollen-collecting hairs on its forehead. It's interesting to watch the flowers unfold from outside into the center. At first the flowers have a column-like stamen, but in time it replaced by a forked pistil which has a definite female shape. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2013)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: d430g_d429-032.jpg
Notes: 8697600078 These are pretty new flowers of Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla) in the old Scrophulariaceae plant family, growing in the woods. Now they are moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. Those darned botanists! The whorled flowers look like lupines at first, but they are really quite different. The flowers are highly variable - the color varies from deep violet (like these) to pure white. Some plants are quite pale, almost another species. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2013)

File.Name: d430h_d429-050.jpg
Notes:
8696453413 This is the spring bisexual gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. These galls are "monothalamous" which means there's only one critter inside. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2013)

In a month or two, male and female wasps will emerge from different galls, mate, and form the large polythalamous "oak apple" summer galls, which contain many females. These females will emerge next spring to parthenogenetically produce a new generation of these bisexual galls. It's complicated, but life is like that!
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d501a_d501-035.jpg
Notes:
8699885327 I believe this is a beautiful Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly. It's perched on a Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family, but I scared it away from the new flowers of Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family that it was working. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)

It was breezy this morning as the fog cleared out, but then it quickly warmed up and the wind died down.
CommonNames: Gabb's Checkerspot; Bigberry Manzanita; Wedge-leaved Horkelia
ScientificName: Chlosyne gabbii; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Chlosyne gabbii; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae

File.Name: d501b_d501-048.jpg
Notes: 8700983874 This is the same Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly as my [Previous] photo, with wings open. I guess this is a female - like ducks, the males are more brightly colored. This one is perched on a weedy Cutleaf Geranium (Geranium dissectum) in the Geraniaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)

File.Name: d501c_d501-046.jpg
Notes: 8699813805 So that's why we call these Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterflies! This butterfly looks quite silvery and also a bit blue on it's body and the base of its wings. It was quite attached to these new flowers of non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra) in the Brassicaceae plant family. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)

File.Name: d501d_d501-028.jpg
Notes: 8700910040 This is the first Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly that I've managed to capture this year. I guess this is a large Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). It's perched on leaves of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family down by the creek today. You can see the hook-like tips on its club-like antennae. This is the classic "jet plane" pose. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: d501e_d501-020.jpg
Notes: 8699735769 I'm sure this is a caterpillar of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly. It's an odd pose, but it's more odd that it's on a leaf stalk of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It seems to have munched all the leaflets on this stem. Usually, these caterpillars prefer "scrophs" in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. Maybe it's confused because the botanists have reclassified most scrophs to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae). I'm confused too! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Wild Sweetpea; Plantain Family
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: d501f_d501-033.jpg
Notes: 8699697263 Look close! This is a fantastic copper-colored Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family in the canyon. This is an unusual plant in our canyon, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia grandiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia grandiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: d501g_d501-042.jpg
Notes: 8700776698 This is a pretty little "True Bug" in the insect order Hemiptera, perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. My first thought was that it's a Lygaeid Bug in the family Lygaeidae - and maybe it is, though I can't seem to find it at BugGuide. It's a pretty little critter with those raised orange lines on its wing covers. You can see its sucking proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)
CommonNames: True Bug; Coast Live Oak; Lygaeid Bug

File.Name: d501h_d501-014.jpg
Notes:
8699614107 This is a another squat little Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) in the woods perched on a dead grass stem. I first thought it was a Western Black-Legged Tick (Ixodes pacificus), but Alice corrected me. It's really a female Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis). Damn, even tick IDs are difficult! I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens, but it was breezy this morning as the fog blew out. If you stand back about 10 feet, it looks great! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)

It's just waiting (and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me. Ticks can spend weeks just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that. If you get ticked, just pull the damn thing out! Use tweezers if you have to. The first sign of a tick bite is a persistent irritation. The bites usually swell up and itch for a day after you remove the tick. No big deal.
Locality: and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me.  Ticks can spend weeks just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that.  If you get ticked
CommonNames: Tick; Western Black-Legged Tick; Pacific Coast Tick
ScientificName: Ixodoidea; Arachnida; Ixodes pacificus; Dermacentor occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Ixodoidea; Arachnida; Ixodes pacificus; Dermacentor occidentalis

File.Name: d502a_d502-026.jpg
Notes:
8702574355 This is a beautiful Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) butterfly perched for a moment on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. I followed it here, and a moment later it was gone. This is a tight crop and a bit over-exposed, but good enough! I found them last February (see this photo), so I guess this is their second brood. I love the peaches 'n cream colors of these butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)

I was warm today - in the 80s, it feels like summer. It was breezy, but not quite windy. We don't get those nasty Santa Ana Winds in the Santa Barbara mountains like they do further south, because there is no direct connection to the desert. I'm glad!
ScientificName: Anthocharis sara; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthocharis sara; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: d502b_d502-002.jpg
Notes: 8703675264 This is a male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. The hooked antennae and chevrons on the white wings are unmistakable. They are not common in our mountains, but I find them now and then. This one was going from flower to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)

File.Name: d502c_d502-037.jpg
Notes:
8703643272 This is a handsome California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly showing her pretty blue colors on her front wings. This one is "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil on a garden path. This is about the only chance I have to photograph these butterflies who seem to fly endlessly among the oak trees and rarely visit flowers. This one seems to have a damaged wing on the right, but it doesn't seem to be slowing it down. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)

Note how the orange spots on it's forewings are bordered by brown, and compare my photo of a Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly, which looks similar but lacks the border.

File.Name: d502d_d502-031.jpg
Notes: 8702495831 This is the first elegant new flower of Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family that I've found in our canyon at home. It's kind of an upside-down Mariposa Lily. You can see the nectar gland at the base of the petal that attracts insects, and the hairs on the overlapping petals that makes it hard for them to leave until they are thoroughly dusted with pollen. I love these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)

File.Name: d502e_d502-021.jpg
Notes: 8702474643 These are pretty new flowers of native Tomcat Clover (Trifolium willdenovii) in the Fabaceae plant family. It does have three-parted leaves like all clovers - except for the lucky 4 (or 5-9) leaf clover. They are not common in our mountains. I got this photo on the hillside across from our mailbox today. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)

File.Name: d502f_d502-011.jpg
Notes: 8702452719 This little Crab Spider (family Thomisidae) looks like it's trying to outrun a mighty wave! The "wave" is the backside of a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. Yeah, a leaf was in the way of my lens, but I like the soft effect. Not every mistake is ruinous! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)

File.Name: d502g_d502-027.jpg
Notes: 8703541558 This is the perfect nymph of a Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera. It's perched on a glandular flower stem of unusual native California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) in the Fabaceae plant family. This plant isn't common in our mountains, but I find it here and there. The flowers aren't quite open yet, but I'm keeping an eye on them. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Rupertia physodes; Psoralea physodes; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Rupertia physodes; Psoralea physodes; Fabaceae

File.Name: d502h_d502-008.jpg
Notes: 8703520676 This is the speckled nymph of a Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) - probably a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae. This one is resting "butts up" on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. They've gotten larger since my last photo on March 10. Its antennae look distinctly lobster-like. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; California Angle-winged Katydid; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d503a_d503-017.jpg
Notes:
8705367401 This is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly working a new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. These are our earliest butterflies, so this must be a new brood. The butterflies are very plain below, which distinguishes them. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2013)

It's been breezy enough that I haven't wanted to leave the canyon for a few days. And it's been warm - in the 80s, like summer. Of course *wildfire* is on my mind. It's only the beginning of May, but the woods are already very dry. I hope we make it through the summer.

File.Name: d503b_d503-010.jpg
Notes: 8706472856 This is another Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly. I guess it's a large Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). It's perched on leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. These butterflies are kind of moth-like, though they do have clubbed antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2013)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Poanes melane; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: d503c_d503-007.jpg
Notes:
8705319447 Here are a mating pair of Tephritid Fruit Flies (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) on a flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden this morning. This is after there was some confusion about who the lucky male would be - see my [Previous] photo. The larger female on the left seems to ovipositing - laying eggs - while she's "doing it". (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2013)

Yeah, these are tiny flies, and there was a breeze. I took lots of photos, and selected this one as the best.
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; lots
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; lots

File.Name: d503d_d503-002.jpg
Notes:
8706410494 Just count the (compound) eyes and divide by two. I guess they didn't read the manual!

This is a dogpile of Tephritid Fruit Flies (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) on a flower bud of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2013)

File.Name: d503e_d503-027.jpg
Notes: 8706387088 This is a line of beautiful new flowers of native "Checker Bloom" (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae plant family. Look at that pretty little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) in the middle flower. Maybe it's a Sweat Bee in the family Halictidae? (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2013)
CommonNames: Checker Bloom; Wild Bee; Sweat Bee
ScientificName: Sidalcea malviflora; Malvaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Halictidae
associatedTaxa: Sidalcea malviflora; Malvaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Halictidae

File.Name: d503f_d501-002.jpg
Notes: 8705228163 These are brand new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. They are beautiful. The stamens look like little hot dogs! The flowers come from a "connate leaf" that surrounds the stem like Miners Lettuce. You can see a bit of it in the upper-right. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)

File.Name: d503g_d501-030.jpg
Notes: 8706318850 I believe this is a pretty new flower of Sticky False Gilyflower - a terrible name! - (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. It seems early for them to be flowering, but here they are. I love the blue pollen! I don't know what that bit of fluff is to the lower left of the flower, but I wish it wasn't there. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)

File.Name: d503h_d502-024.jpg
Notes: 8705184555 These are new florets of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the Poaceae plant family growing on the dry edge of our garden. This grass has short awns, which distinguishes it from weedy grasses like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and Red Brome (Bromus rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. We've been encouraging it around the house. You have to know something about grasses to recognize it. The leaf blades all look the same. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)
CommonNames: California Brome; Ripgut Grass; Red Brome
ScientificName: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens
associatedTaxa: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens

File.Name: d504a_d504-014.jpg
Notes:
8708044511 This is another Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly in a classic "jet plane" pose. It's perched for a moment on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess it's another large Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). The blue colors on the thorax of this plain moth-like butterfly are interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2013)

There was a big shift in the weather today, from sunny and hot to mostly cloudy and cool. There's a chance of (a little) rain coming up!
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Poanes melane

File.Name: d504b_d504-021.jpg
Notes: 8708003123 Here is a beautiful caterpillar of the Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin. It's climbing up a stem of native grass that is just starting to flower - probably Western Rye (Elymus glaucus) in the Poaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2013)
ScientificName: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Elymus glaucus; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Elymus glaucus; Poaceae

File.Name: d504c_d504-011.jpg
Notes:
8709097058 Here is a small female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) - that blur on the left - going into a flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. You can see how pollination happens for these flowers. The stamens of the flowers rub against the hummer's forehead to deposit pollen. In time, the pistil drops down to receive the pollen on another visit. They don't call these "hummingbird flowers" for nothing! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2013)

This isn't the perfectly sharp hummingbird photo I've been trying to get, but it will do until I get it!

File.Name: d504d_d504-018.jpg
Notes: 8709041456 This is a different Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm not sure what kind of bee this is. It seems to have pollen collecting hairs mostly on its face! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2013)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: d504e_d504-006.jpg
Notes:
8707810333 These are tiny Wild Bees (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) - maybe family Andrenidae? They are on flowers of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. The bee in the upper-left is easy to see; the one in the lower flower is mostly hidden. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2013)

I'm more interested in the flowers. These are male staminate flowers. The spent flower on the left has lost the circle of stamens around the "hypanthium" disk, but you can see the little cone of what I guess are female pistils in the center - and the Jepson manual says the flowers are mostly unisexual. Compare my [Previous] photo of a fully female pistillate flower.

The Wild Blackberries are having a tough time in this drought year. The flowers are almost done for the year. What will the bees do?
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Andrenidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Andrenidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d504f_d419-037.jpg
Notes: 8707781909 This the illusive female pistillate flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Almost all the plants of this most common understory plant in our canyon are male staminate plants, though they seem to have a core of pistils in the center - see my [Next] photo. I found this pistillate plant by our mailbox. I really wonder if all the plants in our canyon are a clone? (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2013)

File.Name: d504g_d429-028.jpg
Notes: 8707758619 This is native Bowlesia incana in the Apiaceae plant family. The Apiaceae were once known as the Umbelliferae - because the flowers usually come in umbrella-like "umbels" with lots of flowers, but this is an umbel of one. I think of it as a limit thing - like a circle is a limit of an ellipse, so this flower is the limit of an umbel. It has attractive foliage. I took me years to figure out what this is. It would be a good final exam plant for a plant taxonomy class! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2013)

File.Name: d504h_d504-002.jpg
Notes: 8708839886 This is a surprisingly beautiful grape-sized gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It is growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These galls have both red and green forms. This one seems in between. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2013)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: d505a_d505-004.jpg
Notes:
8711591747 I'm pretty sure this is a little Treehopper (family Membracidae of the old insect order Homoptera). Looking at BugGuide, it sure looks like genus Cyrtolobus. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen. They have remarkable eyes, like all jumpers that have to see where they're jumping. It's about 1/4 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2013)

It was a crummy day for photos - cold (in the low 40s), gloomy, and windy. But I happened to notice this little creature on a grass stem that was shaking in the wind. I moved it to a plant stem on the ground. I'm surprised it didn't hop away, but maybe it was to cold? I took many photos to get this one keeper. I had to crank up the ISO another notch. This was my only keeper photo today.

We actually had a bit of rain/drizzle last night - half of 1/10 inch, and there's been more drizzle today. More rain is expected tonight and tomorrow, but it probably won't amount to much. (I don't want to jinx it!)

File.Name: d505b_d502-015.jpg
Notes: 8712700340 Here is a small Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched for a moment on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. Yeah, it's a bit underexposed. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Western Bracken Fern
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: d505c_d502-013.jpg
Notes: 8711523463 This little white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is on a shrub of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's the same spider I showed before in this photo. A leaf was blocking it before, so I nudged it along the stem. I like the earlier photo better, despite the leaf - but I do like how the spider's eyes are pointing in all different directions. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2013)

File.Name: d505d_d503-022.jpg
Notes: 8712618976 Here you can see new peas in pods of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family - thanks to some backlight, my favorite light. Beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2013)

File.Name: d505e_d503-024.jpg
Notes:
8712596078 These are elegant new flowers of Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in the canyon. We have quite a few plants on the dry hillside above our retaining wall, but the deer have developed a taste for the buds. I figure I better show then while I can! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2013)

They are kind of upside-down Mariposa Lilies. You can see the nectar glands at the base of the petals that attracts insects, and the hairs on the overlapping petals that makes it hard for them to leave until they are thoroughly dusted with pollen.

File.Name: d505f_d504-003.jpg
Notes:
8711386177 I believe that the caterpillar-like roll on the leaf on the right is an insect gall on a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Maybe it's the gall of the Snowberry Gall Midge (genus Dasineura, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) that roll onto the upper surface of the leaf. I don't notice these galls very often. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2013)

There's some confusion about what species our plants really are. The real S. mollis is said to only grow a foot or two tall, and our plants are more like S. albus at 2-4 feet tall. It definitely some kind of Snowberry!
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Dasineura; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; S. mollis; S. albus
associatedTaxa: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Dasineura; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; S. mollis; S. albus

File.Name: d505g_d429-035.jpg
Notes:
8712476194 This is a closer photo of the deformed leaves of a native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family, which has been invaded by a powdery Orange Rust fungus (Uredinales, Basidiomycotina). See my [Previous] photo. This rust fungus sure looks like photos of Gymnoconia nitens. Another candidate is Arthuriomyces peckianus. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2013)

The plants completely change when they are infected - the leaflets are thin and widely spaced, and the stems have few thorns. I find this every year on a few plants in the canyon. It doesn't seem to be getting worse over the years.

File.Name: d505h_d501-018.jpg
Notes:
8711231009 These are deformed leaves of a native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family, which has been invaded by a rust fungus (Uredinales, Basidiomycotina). This rust fungus sure looks like photos of Gymnoconia nitens. Another candidate is Arthuriomyces peckianus.

The plants completely change when they are infected - the leaflets are thin and widely spaced, and the stems have no thorns. See my [Next] photo. I find this every year on a few plants in the canyon. It doesn't seem to be getting worse over the years. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2013)

File.Name: d506a_d506-037.jpg
Notes:
8715360537 This is the fantastic nymph of a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. It's perched on a bristly flower head of annual Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family, now moved to the Boraginaceae. This is the first one I've seen this year. It's tiny, only about 1/4 inch long without the antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)

We had an hour of good rain last night around midnight! We ended up with 4/10 inch in the rain gauge. We had a 70% chance of more rain today, but it didn't happen. We're now up to 52% of normal. It was still a pretty good soak that will do the late-season wildflowers some good in this drought year.
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: d506b_d506-015.jpg
Notes: 8716460394 I guess this is a Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae, order Diptera) - maybe genus Rhagio. It's perched on a leaf of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella or R. californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Their abdomens remind me of those collapsible cups I used to carry when backpacking. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)
ScientificName: Rhagionidae; Diptera; Rhagio; Rhamnus tomentella; R. californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Rhagionidae; Diptera; Rhagio; Rhamnus tomentella; R. californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: d506c_d506-032.jpg
Notes: 8716421846 I guess this is the nest of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. It has a separate orb web nearby. Like a pirate, these spiders decorate their nests with the bodies of their prey. Pretty grisly! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)

File.Name: d506d_d506-013.jpg
Notes:
8716383448 This is another elegant new flower of Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in the canyon - with raindrops. They are kind of upside-down Mariposa Lilies. You can see the nectar glands at the base of the petals that attracts insects, and the hairs on the overlapping petals that makes it hard for them to leave until they are thoroughly dusted with pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)

"When You Got It, Flaunt It!

File.Name: d506e_d506-022.jpg
Notes:
8715239077 This is a new flower of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in our canyon. It's quite uncommon in our mountains behind Santa Barbara. I only find it in a few shady places where the sun rarely shines, and I've never found the edible (but thin) fruit here. It looks like a blackberry, but different. The leaves are in one piece, and the flowers have both stamens and pistils. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)

They are common and almost a roadside weed in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are uncommon here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I guess it's a relict plant from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on so far against climate change.

File.Name: d506f_d506-008.jpg
Notes:
8715209949 Here are the first new flowers I've seen this year of the unusual native California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) in the Fabaceae plant family. This plant isn't common in our mountains, but I find it here and there. This plant has "leaves of three" like Poison Oak. It's one of those natives that could disappear from the woods without leaving a ripple, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)

It has an interesting common name. I haven't tried it for tea, but I should.

File.Name: d506g_d506-035.jpg
Notes: 8715175589 These are new flowers of native Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) in the old Scrophulariaceae plant family, now they are moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. Those darned botanists! This one is flowering in our canyon on San Marcos Pass. They are scarce in the mountains and much more common along the river. (Where else doe it grow in the mountains?) They are beautiful plants, with classic hummingbird flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)

File.Name: d506h_d506-027.jpg
Notes: 8715158279 These are glandular new flowers of native Slender Tarweed (Madia gracilis) in the Asteraceae plant family . The pretty composite flowers usually close early in the morning, so I rarely see them - but they stay open on gloomy days like today. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)

File.Name: d507a_d507-006.jpg
Notes:
8718443461 I wonder if stink bugs like to get their bellies scratched, like our cats?

This is a "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Green Stink Bug in the genus Chinavia of the tribe Nezarini. You can see its proboscis folded under its belly. I found this bug on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family, but I moved it to the ground for a better photo. Here it is on a grass stem with the "hairy balls" of non-native Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family, next to a hairy stem of Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Labiatae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2013)

It was mostly gloomy and breezy today, with moments of clarity. We had a few bouts of drizzle, but not enough to add to the rain gauge. It was another day to crank up the ISO a notch or two! Maybe tomorrow will finally be a nice day?
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Green Stink Bug; Canyon Sunflower; Common Bedstraw; Wood Mint
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Nezarini; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae; Stachys bullata; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Nezarini; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae; Stachys bullata; Labiatae

File.Name: d507b_d507-009.jpg
Notes:
8718367451 Here is another photo of the beautiful little Wasp in the order Hymenoptera that I showed a few days ago perched on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. This one is perched on a leaf of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2013)

I can't see any sign of a "wasp-waist" on this critter, so I wonder if it's a "Sawfly" in the "Symphyta" group - maybe genus Tenthredo of the family Tenthredinidae? (Yes, I've been browsing at BugGuide!)
CommonNames: Wasp; Western Bracken Fern; California Honeysuckle
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Symphyta; Tenthredo; Tenthredinidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Symphyta; Tenthredo; Tenthredinidae

File.Name: d507c_d507-014.jpg
Notes: 8718345167 Here is a pollen-dusted Bumble Bee (genus Bombus, family Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on flower petals of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It was cold and gloomy this morning, and I reckon the bee got stuck here, out of gas. The white balance is a little funky too. Compare my [Previous] photo of a tiny fly on a similar flower petal. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2013)
ScientificName: Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: d507d_d507-022.jpg
Notes:
8718304733 This is a tiny Fly (order Diptera) on a single petal of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I was bored, so I tried to take the photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. It came out surprisingly well for a breezy day! I'm not sure what kind of fly it is - maybe a Dance Fly in the family Empididae, or an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae)? Compare my [Next] photo for a sense of scale. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2013)

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The critter is about 530 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.4x530/4800 = 2.6 mm, less than 1/8 inch. Like I said, this fly is tiny!
CommonNames: Fly; Canyon Sunflower; Dance Fly; Bee Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Empididae; Bombyliidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Empididae; Bombyliidae

File.Name: d507e_d507-020.jpg
Notes: 8718276951 Here are a pair of native woodland grasses in the Poaceae plant family in one photo. That's the short-awned California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass arching over the top, and the spikier Western Rye aka Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus) grass lower down. Julie has been encouraging these native grasses on the dry edge of our garden. They are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2013)
CommonNames: California Brome; Western Rye; Blue Wildrye

File.Name: d507f_d507-015.jpg
Notes:
8718222889 These are minimalist male pollen-producing staminate flowers of native Meadow Rue (Thalictrum fendleri var. polycarpum) in the Ranunculaceae plant family - with Aphids in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera. I believe those are female pistillate flowers above the left-most aphid. The flowers lack petals but not beauty. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2013)

This is a garden photo of a plant Julie bought at the SB Botanic Garden. They are native to coastal creeks, but not up here in the mountains. We have them in a shady place. It remains to be seen if they'll survive.
ScientificName: Thalictrum fendleri; polycarpum; Ranunculaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Thalictrum fendleri; polycarpum; Ranunculaceae; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: d507g_d506-039.jpg
Notes: 8719327128 These are small but pretty flowers of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the Rosaceae plant family growing near the creek. There's a tiny Fly (order Diptera) in the lower-right. The stamens look like little lollipops. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)

File.Name: d507h_d506-010.jpg
Notes:
8719282252 This is a still life near the bottom of our driveway. I'm pretty sure the plant in the center is a flowering native Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a native perennial in our mountains with large dandelion-like seed heads. I usually don't notice it until it gets seeds - see this photo. This is a nice photo, but busy. It's a hard plant to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2013)

There's lots more in this photo - Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla, Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae), Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica, Onagraceae), Wood Mint (Stachys bullata, Labiatae), and Small-flowered Melic Grass (Mellica imperfecta, Poaceae). There's no doubt more!
CommonNames: Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion; Chinese Houses; Speckled Clarkia; Wood Mint; Small-flowered Melic Grass
ScientificName: Agoseris grandiflora; Asteraceae; Collinsia heterophylla; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Clarkia cylindrica; Onagraceae; Stachys bullata; Labiatae; Mellica imperfecta; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Agoseris grandiflora; Asteraceae; Collinsia heterophylla; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Clarkia cylindrica; Onagraceae; Stachys bullata; Labiatae; Mellica imperfecta; Poaceae

File.Name: d508a_d508-039.jpg
Notes:
8721348231 The real Toad Hall!

I guess this is a California Toad (Anaxyrus boreas halophilus), partly because of the stripe down its back. It has this perfect "toad hole" in the bank above a perennial water source. It put up with me for a minute or two, then retreated into its hole. Can toads really dig holes or is this an old gopher hole? (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

Clouds were moving in and out today, and it was breezy - but I got some good photo anyway!

File.Name: d508b_d508-035.jpg
Notes: 8721294679 This is a fantastic native Yellow Mariposa Lilyaka Clubhair Mariposa Lily (Calochortus clavatus ssp. pallidus) in the Liliaceae plant family - with beetles. I found it growing up through a shrub of Purple Sage (Salvia leucophylla) in the Labiatae plant family on crumbly soil of Monterey Shale. This was the only one I found today. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)
CommonNames: Yellow Mariposa Lily; Clubhair Mariposa Lily; Purple Sage
ScientificName: Calochortus clavatus; pallidus; Liliaceae; Salvia leucophylla; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus clavatus; pallidus; Liliaceae; Salvia leucophylla; Labiatae

File.Name: d508c_d508-044.jpg
Notes: 8721268123 This is a beautiful Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family - with many insects! Compare my [Previous] photo of (C. catalinae). This one has extra maroon spots above the nectary glands. Insects love these flowers - mostly beetles, but I also see a couple of little diurnal (daytime) moths. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

File.Name: d508d_d508-029.jpg
Notes:
8722357496 I believe this is a native Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Liliaceae plant family. It looks similar to the Butterfly Mariposa Lily (C. venustus), but it has ovate nectaries and lacks the purple spots on the petals. Very beautiful. Compare my [Next] photo. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

I was expecting to find lots of these today, but this was the only one I found. The dry weather has been taking a toll on wildflowers. They are finishing fast!
CommonNames: Catalina Mariposa Lily; Butterfly Mariposa Lily

File.Name: d508e_d508-012.jpg
Notes: 8722346136 This is a perfect Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly trying to warm up on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. Just beautiful! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

File.Name: d508f_d508-014.jpg
Notes:
8722306226 I came across this is a beautiful California Kingsnake (Lampropeltis getula californiae) on my trail today. Of course, it headed away as I fumbled with my tripod. By reputation, they sometimes feed on rattlesnakes. They are described as black and white, but they are really a rich chocolate-brown and white. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

Don't miss this fabulous photo of a kingsnake in action by RandomTruth!

File.Name: d508g_d508-010.jpg
Notes: 8721171211 Here are new flowers of native Stream Orchid (Epipactis gigantea) in the Orchidaceae plant family. It's not rare in our mountains, but I'm always happy and surprised to find them in flower. These are growing in a perennial wet place along my trail today. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

File.Name: d508h_d508-047.jpg
Notes:
8722252630 As I understand, this is native Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua or Stipa cernua) in the Poacea plant family, with twisted awns. I might be wrong about this, but you can see that the base of the awns is twisted. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

It's interesting to look for native perennial grasses where the weedy annual grasses are rampant. If you look close, you will often find them. They are kind of shocking to notice!

File.Name: d509a_d509-020.jpg
Notes:
8725050762 This is a "rare" - as in "rarely common" - flower of native Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum) in the Papaveraceae plant family. The flower is falling apart, but there are more coming. I only found this one. It's the other "California Poppy" that only grows as a fire-follower after recent wildfire burns. I found this one in a place that was cleared but didn't burn in the wildfire - but maybe there was a "slash pile" burn in this place. It really looks like a little Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum). See my [Previous] photo for a look at the the whole plant. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2013)

It was another gloomy morning, but it cleared up for a while and the winds were moderate. Then the "sundowner winds" kicked up in the afternoon. It sure has been a windy spring!

File.Name: d509b_d509-017.jpg
Notes: 8725013470 This is the plant and flower of a "rare" native Fire Poppy (Papaver californicum) in the Papaveraceae plant family. That's "rare" as in "rarely common"! It's the other "California Poppy" that only grows as a fire-follower after recent wildfire burns. I found this one in a place that was cleared but didn't burn in the wildfire - but maybe there was a "slash pile" burn in this place. It really looks like a little Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum). See my [Next] photo for a closer look at the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2013)

File.Name: d509c_d509-034.jpg
Notes: 8724970682 This is a little native Common Monkey Flower (Mimulus guttatus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. Now it's been moved to the Lopseed Family (Phrymaceae). Those darn botanists! I found this plant growing in a wet place near the start of the trail to the "Playground" in our mountains. The flowers are much smaller than the usual monkey flowers. They might be Mimulus guttatus ssp. micranthus. This subspecies isn't recognized in the 1993 Jepson Manual or the new eFlora version, but it is in the old 1959 Munz flora. It really seems distinct. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2013)
ScientificName: Mimulus guttatus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Mimulus guttatus; micranthus
associatedTaxa: Mimulus guttatus; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Mimulus guttatus; micranthus

File.Name: d509d_d509-039.jpg
Notes: 8723822555 This is a beautiful plant of the other miner's lettuce - the Small-flowered Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia parviflora) in the Portulacaceae plant family. The way that the leaves are clustered in the center with the flower stalk around the outside is typical. The red color seems unusual, but I found a number of these plants today. I think of it as a fire follower. This place is close to the Zaca Fire a few years ago, but I don't think it actually burned here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2013)

File.Name: d509e_d508-020.jpg
Notes: 8723812785 These are new flowers of the Elegant Clarkia (Clarkia unguiculata) in the Onagraceae plant family. The petals look like they've been cut with scissors, but that's how they are. It's another Farewell-to-Spring - a sign that our dry summer is approaching, too fast! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

File.Name: d509f_d508-005.jpg
Notes: 8724918574 This is a beautiful flower of native Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica) in the Onagraceae plant family. They also go by the name Farewell-to-Spring because of when they flower - and this year, it's true. This is one of those wildflowers that form solid patches of color along the highways. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

File.Name: d509g_d509-010.jpg
Notes: 8724887194 I believe this is native Small-flowered Melic Grass (Melica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family. It does especially well after wildfires and brush clearing like this, but it's also growing in the woodland around home. I found this in a place that was cleared but not burned a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2013)

File.Name: d509h_d508-053.jpg
Notes: 8723751063 These are native perennial "bunch grasses" growing in a place where the weedy annual grasses are rampant. I believe these are native Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua or Stipa cernua) in the Poacea plant family, with twisted awns. See my photo yesterday. If you look close, you will often find natives mixed in the weeds. They are hanging on. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)

File.Name: d510a_d510-017.jpg
Notes:
8727772764 I believe this is the Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly perched on a branch of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. This is not the best focus, but I was hunched over and a moment later it was gone. This butterfly doesn't have the white scaling of the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra), and it's darker and more interesting than the Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum). I know the photo isn't quite sharp, but it's a record photo! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2013)

It was a nice day today, but the persistent wind is starting to get me down. It sure has been a windy spring!
ScientificName: Satyrium saepium; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Satyrium tetra; Satyrium auretorum
associatedTaxa: Satyrium saepium; Prunus ilicifolia; Rosaceae; Satyrium tetra; Satyrium auretorum

File.Name: d510b_d510-003.jpg
Notes:
8727743044 This is a mighty Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Hemiptera. It's perched under a new white flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. A moment later, it flew away before I could fiddle with my exposure. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2013)

It was a good day for "almost great" photos!
CommonNames: Bee Assassin; Assassin Bug; California Thistle
ScientificName: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Hemiptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Hemiptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d510c_d510-029.jpg
Notes:
8726599017 *Sigh* - this isn't a great photo, but it's my one photo of these insects. Here are a mating pair of unusual native "Timema" insects - chunky members of the Walkingstick clan in the family Timematidae, of the suborder Timematodea, in the order Phasmatodea. They are fooling around in a shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I reckon that's the large female above (in this photo) and the much smaller male below. They didn't like being watched and dropped away into the void just after I got this photo. I can't blame them! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2013)

Well, you can't get them all!
ScientificName: Timematidae; Timematodea; Phasmatodea; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Timematidae; Timematodea; Phasmatodea; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d510d_d510-026.jpg
Notes: 8726566977 This is a beautiful new flower of native Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Onagraceae plant family - with beetles in the order Coleoptera. The nodding ("reflexed") buds are diagnostic of the species. This seems to be an uncommon plant in our mountains, at least I only find it in this one canyon - where it is quite common. Go figure! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2013)

File.Name: d510e_d508-043.jpg
Notes: 8726553287 This is a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. It's interesting that the flowers are yellow near the river, but the flowers are usually red in the mountains near home. Sure, it's a fairly tight crop. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 May 2013)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Southern California Dudleya; Lanceleaf Liveforever
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Dudleya lanceolata; Crassulaceae

File.Name: d510f_d510-014.jpg
Notes: 8727651826 These are tiny new flowers of native Turkish Rigging (Chorizantha staticoides) in the Polygonaceae plant family. Look how shriveled the leaves at the base of the stems are. I think of this as a summer flower, but here it is. I wonder what August and September will bring in this second very dry year? (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2013)

File.Name: d510g_d509-032.jpg
Notes: 8727636558 I believe these are new flowers of Hollyleaf Navarretia (Navarretia atractyloides) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. I like these little flowers on spiky plants with blue pollen! (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2013)

File.Name: d510h_d510-006.jpg
Notes: 8726502835 I almost hate to make these non-native Wild Oats (Avena fatua) look so beautiful, but here they are. It's a too-common weedy grass in the family Poaceae growing along my trail today. I suspect it was seeded many years ago. They look "buggy" and remind me of trout flies. When you "sow your wild oats" this is what your distributing - so watch out! (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2013)

File.Name: d511a_d511-007.jpg
Notes:
8730572078 This is a tiny Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera. I guess it's an Issid Planthopper (Issidae) - very likely Danepteryx robusta with those narrow wings. ( See BugGuide.) Those skinny wings can't be useful for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? They have amazing eyes. I guess they need them to see where they're hopping. This is a 1:1 800x1066 crop from my original 4800x3200 photo (in DX mode). My D800 camera has good pixels! I wasn't quite at 1:1 magnification so I can't measure it by counting pixels, but I reckon this little critter is only about 1/8 inch long. It's resting on a stem of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2013)

It was a beautiful day today - not too windy, and warm - 93∞ according to a neighbor's weather station. These first really hot days of the season are hard to take.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Danepteryx robusta; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Danepteryx robusta; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: d511b_d511-018.jpg
Notes: 8730544040 These are caterpillars of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly where they should be, on foliage of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. These caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some webbing in the photo in the upper-right. Sometimes they shroud the penstemon leaves like "tent caterpillars", but they are not the bad kind. I've been finding the new butterflies, and I hope this means more are coming.
Locality: Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. These caterpillars are web-spinners
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Plantain Family
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: d511c_d511-029.jpg
Notes: 8730503740 This is a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native California Figwort aka California Bee Plant(Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family. (This one is still a scroph!) This might be the hardest flower in the woods to photograph. The flowers are widely spaced on long stems that seem to sway in the slightest breeze. Nothing to do except crank up the ISO another notch! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2013)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; California Figwort; California Bee Plant
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: d511d_d511-012.jpg
Notes: 8730479242 This is another photo of new flowers on the unusual native California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) in the Fabaceae plant family. This plant isn't common in our mountains, but I find it here and there. This plant has clover-like "leaves of three" like Poison Oak. The flowers remind me of a locoweed (Astragalus). It's one of those natives that could disappear from the woods without leaving a ripple, but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2013)
ScientificName: Rupertia physodes; Psoralea physodes; Fabaceae; Astragalus
associatedTaxa: Rupertia physodes; Psoralea physodes; Fabaceae; Astragalus

File.Name: d511e_d511-015.jpg
Notes: 8730461904 This is the seed head and spent flower of native Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers never open much and close in the morning, but the large dandelion-like seed heads are wonderful. See this photo of the whole plant with flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2013)

File.Name: d511f_d510-012.jpg
Notes: 8729300227 These are Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family, with Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family behind. What's missing are the actual long flowers of the Woolly Blue Curls - compare this photo. These small signs of drought are very interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2013)
ScientificName: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d511g_d510-040.jpg
Notes:
8729247731 Here are lots of insects on a brand new flower head of local Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family, which is just starting to flower in the mountains. That large beetle on the left is a Metallic Wood-boring Beetle(Buprestidae) in the order Coleoptera. The flowers are like those fancy easter eggs with treasures inside - look close at that open flower below the large beetle's abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2013)

This shrub is named for the naturalist Blanche Trask who studied the botany of the channel islands 100 years ago. She's one of my heros. There's a collection of her writing, well worth reading, on the web at: http://naturespeace.org/blanchetraskanthology.htm
CommonNames: Trask's Yerba Santa; Metallic Wood-boring Beetle

File.Name: d511h_d510-019.jpg
Notes: 8729212631 Here are (finally!) new flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plants in the Fabaceae plant family - now renamed Acmispon glaber . (Those darn botanists!) What's odd is that I'm just starting to see the flowers in May instead of February or March. Compare this photo from last year, also a dry year.
