I think most plants will skip flowering in the very dry year. Many Blue and Hairstreak butterflies depend on these flowers. I wonder what they will do? These small signs of drought are very interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2013)

File.Name: d512a_d512-016.jpg
Notes:
8733055659 This is another beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, on a spent flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's sticking its tongue out in a perfect loop! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2013)

Maybe it's a secret among local butterfly photographers, but these are the easiest large and beautiful butterflies to photograph! They are a bit touchy when they're newly emerged, but they become quite docile. Still, they are very beautiful.

It was another *hot* day today, in the 90s. The wind was manageable - dead calm in the morning, though it kicked up later in the day.
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; easiest
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; easiest

File.Name: d512b_d512-040.jpg
Notes: 8734146704 This is a beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working a new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. If you look close, you can see a tiny Weevil beetle (Curculionidae, Coleoptera) above the butterfly's head. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2013)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Curculionidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: d512c_d512-013.jpg
Notes: 8732988025 Here is another little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native California Figwort aka California Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family. It seems appropriate to find a bee on Bee Plant! This might be the hardest flower in the woods to photograph. The flowers are widely spaced on long stems that seem to sway in the slightest breeze. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2013)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; California Figwort; California Bee Plant
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Scrophularia californica; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: d512d_d512-025.jpg
Notes: 8732965759 This is another beautiful new flower of native Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Onagraceae plant family - with a beetle in the order Coleoptera. The erect stems and nodding ("reflexed") buds are diagnostic of the species. They are not as uncommon as

File.Name: d512e_d512-032.jpg
Notes: 8734025362 This is a foliose gall on a stem of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe this is the gall of a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera - maybe the species Ceanothus Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) or kin? I also find them on other Ceanothus shrubs in the mountains, sometimes much larger and leafier. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2013)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Gall Midge; Ceanothus Gall Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi; Ceanothus
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi; Ceanothus

File.Name: d512f_d512-027.jpg
Notes:
8732858283 I believe this is a Pacific Woodrush (Luzula comosa) in the Juncaceae plant family. Rushes usually grow in wet places, but this one prefers drier places. They have broad grass-like leaves and flower heads that are different. This one is growing on the shady hillside along East Camino Cielo. Once I noticed it, I found quite a few more. It might be more common than I thought. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2013)

I know, it's not quite sharp - but it's good enough for the ID. I was distracted. A friend and a fan stopped by the road at the same time that I was trying to get this photo. By the time they left, the sun was gone. You don't realize how fast the sun moves until you are trying to capture a grass in a beam of sunlight!

File.Name: d512g_d512-005.jpg
Notes:
8732799709 This is the beautiful whole plant of a flowering native grass Purple Needlegrass (Nassella pulchra aka Stipa pulchra) in the Poaceae plant family. This one is growing in a dry place in the garden, from local seed. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2013)

Hah - the genus Nassella has gone back to genus Stipa! That's why I don't worry too much about scientific names. As long as they index synonyms in the floras, I figure I'm good.
ScientificName: Nassella pulchra; Stipa pulchra; Poaceae; Nassella; Stipa
associatedTaxa: Nassella pulchra; Stipa pulchra; Poaceae; Nassella; Stipa

File.Name: d512h_d512-011.jpg
Notes: 8732772937 Here's another photo of two native grasses in one shot. That's short-awned California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass arching across the photo, and the spikier Western Rye aka Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus) grass in the middle and the very right. Julie has been encouraging these native grasses on the dry edge of our garden. They are beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2013)
CommonNames: California Brome; Western Rye; Blue Wildrye

File.Name: d513a_d513-021.jpg
Notes: 8736401533 This is a Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on non-native flowers of Saltcedar or Tamarisk, maybe Tamarix ramosissima in the Tamaricaceae plant family growing by the river. This is the first Buckeye I've managed to photograph this year. A swarm of other insects are working the flowers - mostly bees, but also flies and beetles. The flowers are pretty enough, but this is a horrible weed along western rivers. See my [

File.Name: d513b_d513-018.jpg
Notes:
8737473586 This is non-native Saltcedar or Tamarisk, maybe Tamarix ramosissima in the Tamaricaceae plant family, though there are several different species. If you look close, you can see a cloud of little insects in the air. That's what's left of the Santa Ynez River in the background - and these wet places are getting hard to find in this very dry year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2013)

Tamarisk is a horrible weed along western rivers. It literally takes over the banks of rivers like the Green River, making it impossible to find a place to land on a float trip. The small plants on the Santa Ynez River are hopefully ephemeral - they will disappear the next time the river floods. But there are larger plants upstream above Gibraltor Dam that will keep replenishing them.

File.Name: d513c_d513-010.jpg
Notes: 8736314455 This is a Grasshopper in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera - I guess it's another Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis). It does seem to have pale green wings when it takes off. I like those tiny pinhead Lichens on the rock. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2013)

File.Name: d513d_d513-015.jpg
Notes: 8737413266 These are tiny pistillate (female) flowers of native Narrowleaf Bedstraw (Galium angustifolium) in the Rubiaceae plant family. You can see the widely-spaced whorled leaves on the stems. The staminate plants have a distinct yellow look, but these pistillate plants have a white look. I guess the brown "things" on some of the flowers are galls, though they are not listed in my book. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2013)

File.Name: d513e_d513-030.jpg
Notes:
8736255665 This is an aging flower of magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family on a dry flat by the river - with little ants. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2013)

Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!

File.Name: d513f_d513-005.jpg
Notes:
8737334378 These are brand new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. We rescued the plant from a slide along Paradise Road years ago, and it's done very well. The leaves have a strong camphor smell. They make good smudge sticks and have a reputation for quieting the dead. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2013)

The flowers are interesting. The open flower on the left is still "closed" - they remain folded shut like this until an insect comes along that is big enough to force its way in. Then the flower opens flat like the one on the right. Honeybees can't open these flowers. It takes a large insect like a Carpenter Bee to unlock them. See this photo. The blue-tinged pistil of these flowers also does a little dance - from the side (closed) to the center (open). They are fun to watch.

File.Name: d513g_d513-039.jpg
Notes: 8736201437 I believe this is native Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua or Stipa cernua) in the Poacea plant family, with twisted awns. It's a very beautiful native grass in flower like this, though it looks quite dead most of the year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2013)

File.Name: d513h_d513-035.jpg
Notes: 8736145961 I believe the native grasses on the left are Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua or Stipa cernua) in the Poacea plant family, with twisted awns. See my [Next] photo. But the large clump on the right is non-native Smilograss (Piptatherum miliaceum aka Stipa miliacea), which is a truly bad invasive weed in our area. One difference is that the smilo has leafy stems - but they really are similar. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 13 May 2013)
ScientificName: Nasella cernua; Stipa cernua; Poacea; Piptatherum miliaceum; Stipa miliacea
associatedTaxa: Nasella cernua; Stipa cernua; Poacea; Piptatherum miliaceum; Stipa miliacea

File.Name: d514a_d514-007.jpg
Notes:
8739280429 This is a native ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending a slug-like caterpillar on flower buds of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. The ant has a single nodal bump. This ant is tending the caterpillar, not attacking it. Maybe it's the caterpillar of a Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly? (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2013)

It was 10 degrees cooler today, but still quite windy. Cooler and windy is better that hot and windy!
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; tending; Leptotes marina
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; tending; Leptotes marina

File.Name: d514b_d514-022.jpg
Notes: 8740342222 Here are a pair of native ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending a slug-like caterpillar on flower buds of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. The ants are tending the caterpillar, not attacking it. The caterpillar itself looks like something that belongs in the ocean! Maybe it's the caterpillar of a Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly? (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2013)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; tending; Leptotes marina
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; tending; Leptotes marina

File.Name: d514c_d514-039.jpg
Notes: 8740322596 This is a beautiful Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on a flower head of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a hairstreak butterfly without tails, though it still rubs its hind wings together like any other hairstreak. It looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches (including violet) combine to make an eye-catching brown. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2013)
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; pointillist
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; pointillist

File.Name: d514d_d514-034.jpg
Notes: 8740292666 This is a tattered Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly working new flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plants in the Fabaceae plant family - now renamed Acmispon glaber. The flowers have been uncommonly scarce in this very dry spring. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2013)
ScientificName: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber
associatedTaxa: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber

File.Name: d514e_d514-037.jpg
Notes: 8740276994 This is the cute nymph of a Grasshopper in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera. I won't try to guess what kind of grasshopper it is. It's perched on a sad-looking stem of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2013)

File.Name: d514f_d514-036.jpg
Notes: 8739140451 Here is a mass of strange and beautiful leaf galls on a Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. They are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp - the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera). You can see the a few of the aphids on the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2013)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: d514g_d514-032.jpg
Notes:
8740214040 This looks like a flower bud, but it's a gall on a new plant of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus var. foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. The gall will not keep this plant from flowering soon! This gall is not listed in my book, but I suspect it is caused by a Gall Midge (family Cecidomyiidae) in the order Diptera. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2013)

I reckon this is Erigeron foliosus var. foliosus and not the var. Stenophyllus with very thin "filiform" leaves that is more common in our mountains. It's growing in a place that was cleared a few years ago.
ScientificName: Erigeron foliosus; foliosus; Asteraceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Erigeron foliosus; foliosus; Stenophyllus
associatedTaxa: Erigeron foliosus; foliosus; Asteraceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Erigeron foliosus; foliosus; Stenophyllus

File.Name: d514h_d514-041.jpg
Notes: 8739072535 This is non-native Hedge Parsley (Torilis arvensis) in the Apiaceae plant family. You may not recognize the plant, but I'll bet you recognize the sticky bur-like seeds that get stuck in your socks and shoe laces! It's a non-native weed that has become a nasty invader of the woodlands in our mountains. We try to pull it in our canyon before it goes to fruit, but it has a remarkable way of coming back a few days later. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2013)

File.Name: d515a_d515-043.jpg
Notes:
8742306605 I found this remarkable Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the insect order Diptera on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family today. Maybe it's Physocephala burgessi in the subfamily Conopinae? It's a remarkable wasp mimic, but it really is a fly - just count the wings and notice the halteres. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2013)

Not the best photo - it was breezy today, and this is a tight crop... but it's still a very cool insect that looks more like a wasp than most real wasps. Ain't nature grand!

Tomorrow around noon the AMGEN Bike Tour is coming over San Marcos Pass from Santa Barbara. The persistent wind has been towards the ocean which won't help the riders. The highway will be closed. I'll try to be there.
ScientificName: Conopidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Physocephala burgessi; Conopinae
associatedTaxa: Conopidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Physocephala burgessi; Conopinae

File.Name: d515b_d515-046.jpg
Notes:
8742222461 Me and my shadow...

I found this remarkable Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the insect order Diptera on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family today. Maybe it's Physocephala burgessi in the subfamily Conopinae? It's a remarkable wasp mimic, but it really is a fly - just count the wings and notice the halteres. That little red bump in it's left rear leg-pit is a small hitchhiking Mite (Acari) that I can see more clearly in other photos. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2013)
ScientificName: Conopidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Physocephala burgessi; Conopinae; Acari
associatedTaxa: Conopidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Physocephala burgessi; Conopinae; Acari

File.Name: d515c_d515-039.jpg
Notes: 8743278910 This is another beautiful Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's working the same flowers as its more common cousins, the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies in my [Previous] two photos, but they are much less common. I never saw them last year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2013)
CommonNames: Gabb's Checkerspot; Canyon Sunflower; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Chlosyne gabbii; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Chlosyne gabbii; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: d515d_d515-037.jpg
Notes: 8743259498 Here is a two-fer of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies, on a native flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. If you compare the wings of them both, you can see why they are called "variable"! They are by far the most common butterflies in our mountains right now. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2013)

File.Name: d515e_d515-041.jpg
Notes: 8743234432 This is another beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, on a native flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. This butterfly is starting to show some scars from its travels in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2013)

File.Name: d515f_d515-026.jpg
Notes: 8743199524 This is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly perched on foliage of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. I don't often find these handsome butterflies on a perch - usually they just fly constantly through the oak trees. I dig the soccer ball eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2013)
CommonNames: California Sister; Climbing Penstemon; Plantain Family
ScientificName: Adelpha bredowii; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Adelpha bredowii; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: d515g_d515-012.jpg
Notes: 8742053947 Here is a handsome Bumble Bee (genus Bombus, family Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's wings are starting to look pretty worn. These bees will keep flying until they can't fly any longer - like we all do. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2013)
ScientificName: Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: d515h_d515-020.jpg
Notes: 8742033541 This is a new flower cluster of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family - with a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on the open flower. You can see the "connate leaf" that surrounds the stem like Miners Lettuce below the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2013)
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: d516a_d516-018.jpg
Notes:
8745334841 "King of the Mountain!"

The is the breakaway group of stage 5 of the AMGEN Bike Tour coming over San Marcos Pass today! I believe that rider in the front is Carter Jones, who won the "KOM" points today. He's smiling because he knows he has it. He looks so young and thin, but what a slog peddling over the pass! This breakaway group had a five minute lead over the rest of the riders. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2013)

Does anyone else fondly remember the old movie Breaking Away?

File.Name: d516b_d516-023.jpg
Notes: 8746420772 The big excitement today was the AMGEN Bike Tour coming over San Marcos Pass! This is the start of the "peloton" - the mass of riders - coming over the top of San Marcos Pass. The highway was closed, and there were 100+ people here to cheer them on. It was quite festive, but the show was over in a few minutes. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2013)

File.Name: d516c_d516-025.jpg
Notes: 8745161853 The big excitement today was the AMGEN Bike Tour coming over San Marcos Pass! This is the "peloton" - the mass of riders - coming over the top of San Marcos Pass. The highway was closed, and there were 100+ people here to cheer them on. It was quite festive, but the show was over in a few minutes. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2013)

File.Name: d516d_d516-039.jpg
Notes: 8746203402 This is not a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) - but it sure has a cat face on it's abdomen! I found this little Spider tending its nest in a web-trapped fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's in a shrub of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It must be an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2013)
CommonNames: Cat-Faced Spider; Spider; Coast Live Oak; California Buckwheat; Orb-Weaver Spider
ScientificName: Araneus gemmoides; Quercus agrifolia; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Araneidae
associatedTaxa: Araneus gemmoides; Quercus agrifolia; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Araneidae

File.Name: d516e_d516-002.jpg
Notes: 8746187292 I found this interesting furry Fly (order Diptera) trying to warm up on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. I first thought it might be a Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae), but I learned from Alice that it's a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae. It has beautiful patterned wings. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2013)
CommonNames: Fly; Wild Blackberry; Bee Fly; Stiletto Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Bombyliidae; Therevidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Bombyliidae; Therevidae

File.Name: d516f_d516-041.jpg
Notes: 8746142632 These are the first new flowers of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family - now moved to the Boraginaceae. The individual flowers are pale and not very interesting, but the caterpillar-like clusters that unwind as they keep producing flowers make up for it. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2013)

File.Name: d516g_d516-005.jpg
Notes: 8745010669 These are pretty new flowers of native "Checkerbloom" (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae plant family. I have no idea why it has this common name, as the flowers aren't checkered at all. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2013)

File.Name: d516h_d516-044.jpg
Notes:
8746078604 This is a new flower of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in the mountains. This photo shows the maple-like palmate leaves which are quite different from our common Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) - visible in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2013)

Thimbleberries are common and almost a roadside weed in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are quite uncommon here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I guess it's a relict plant from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on so far against climate change. I've never found the berries here.

File.Name: d517a_d517-006.jpg
Notes:
8748545165 Here is another Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on a faded flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. This butterfly is committing the crime of "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the sepals instead of through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2013)

This is a hairstreak butterfly without tails, though it still rubs its hind wings together like any other hairstreak. It looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches - including violet and gold - combine to make an eye-catching brown.

It was warm enough for short sleeves today, but cool enough for long pants. The wind held off until the afternoon, though it did finally kick up. It was a very nice day for taking a few photos!
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; pointillist
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; pointillist

File.Name: d517b_d517-012.jpg
Notes: 8749655818 This is the beautiful caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin. It has clown colors! This one is munching a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's tiny, only about 1/4 inch long without the long hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2013)
ScientificName: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d517c_d517-019.jpg
Notes: 8749637374 This is a brand new flower of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus var. foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. If you look close, you can spot a tiny diurnal moth (Lepidoptera) - "microlepidoptera" for sure - on the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2013)

File.Name: d517d_d517-018.jpg
Notes: 8748483511 This is a sinuous strand of parasitic Dodder having it way with seed pods of the real California Mustard (Thelypodium lasiophyllum aka Guillenia lasiophylla) in the Brassicaceae plant family. This dodder is probably California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae plant family. The mustard is more interesting. It's not one of the showy but non-native "wild mustards" that take over the foothills. This is a truly native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2013)
ScientificName: Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla; Brassicaceae; Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae
associatedTaxa: Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla; Brassicaceae; Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae

File.Name: d517e_d517-026.jpg
Notes:
8748444977 Here are new flowers of the Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. This variety with narrow leaves is endemic to the south-facing coastal side of our mountains - and mostly west of San Marcos Pass. The inland variety (L. subspicata var. denudata) has broader leaves and is much more common throughout the area. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2013)

Botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen with narrow leaves from the coast in 1831. The inland variety with wide leaves is more common, but the first named collection remains official.
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; L. subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; L. subspicata; denudata

File.Name: d517f_d517-036.jpg
Notes: 8748414747 These are brilliant new flowers of the native shrub Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana) in the Fabaceae plant family. The pretty flowers look fertile, but the shrubs mostly spread by runners and rarely if ever make seeds. They have gnarly spines, and it's painful to bushwack past them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2013)

File.Name: d517g_d517-041.jpg
Notes: 8748407209 This is looking closer at new flowers of native Peak Rush-rose (Helianthemum scoparium) in the Cistaceae) plant family. It's an interesting family that also includes several non-native species in the genus Cistus that become weedy in the mountains. See my [Previous] photo of the whole plant. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2013)

File.Name: d517h_d517-037.jpg
Notes: 8749513386 These are new plants of native Peak Rush-rose (Helianthemum scoparium) in the Cistaceae) plant family. It's an interesting family that also includes several non-native species in the genus Cistus that become weedy in the mountains. This is a bushy perennial with the stature of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) that grows in dry places, often on burns. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2013)
ScientificName: Helianthemum scoparium; Cistaceae; Cistus; Lotus scoparius
associatedTaxa: Helianthemum scoparium; Cistaceae; Cistus; Lotus scoparius

File.Name: d518a_d518-022.jpg
Notes:
8752610590 This is the same doublet of mating Carpenter Bees (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) as my [Previous] photo, but now they are on a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The double weight is more than enough to open the fulcrum of this flower! At least they're in a position to mate, but I don't see any intimate connection between them. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)

It was warm enough for shorts today, but quite breezy. I got most of my photos early on the dry edge of our garden.

The woods are so dry, and it's not even summer yet. There was a disturbing article in Science Daily about this that (in part) blames the winds. (This is a great resource. You can subscribe for daily updates.)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d518b_d518-025.jpg
Notes: 8752566684 This is a doublet of mating Carpenter Bees (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) on a flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. At least they're in a position to mate. I don't see any intimate connection between them. I assume that's the larger all-black female above, and the smaller white-maned male below. There are other differences, like the eye color. the female seems unconcerned, even visiting this sweet pea flower that they aren't usually interested in! (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: d518c_d518-008.jpg
Notes: 8752542116 Only a large Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) like this is heavy enough to open the fulcrum of these flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family! The over-exposed flower above is still closed. The stamens of the flower really should be pulled down over the bee's back, but I reckon it's leg is in the way. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d518d_d518-012.jpg
Notes: 8751375923 Try as it might, this Bumble Bee (genus Bombus, family Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) just isn't heavy enough to pry open this new flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family! Only the larger Carpenter Bees (Xylocopa) seem to be heavy enough to open them. See my [Next] few photos. Once the flowers are "opened," other bees can get in. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; White Sage; Carpenter Bees
ScientificName: Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Xylocopa

File.Name: d518e_d518-016.jpg
Notes: 8751323813 This is a Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) sticking it's head into a flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. Maybe the bee is a Small Carpenter Bee in the genus Ceratina? You can see how pollination happens with these flowers. The bee is about to back up over the pistil of the flowers, transferring pollen from other flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Wild Sweetpea; Small Carpenter Bee
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Ceratina
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae; Ceratina

File.Name: d518f_d518-055.jpg
Notes: 8751288787 These are new seeds of Woolly-fruited Lomatium (Lomatium dasycarpum) in the Apiaceae plant family. See this photo of the flowers. The plants are sometimes known as "Biscuit Root" because of their edible roots. I like to nibble on the seeds - they have a spicy taste that stays with you! (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)

File.Name: d518g_d518-053.jpg
Notes: 8752354636 These are yesterday's new flowers of native Soap Plant (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. See my [Previous] photo. The new flowers open in the late afternoon. I'll have to make a special trip to photograph these when they are fully open. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)

File.Name: d518h_d518-050.jpg
Notes:
8751171407 This is native Soap Plant (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. It has crinkly leaves and long Asparagus-like flower stalks that are just starting to open. This might be the hardest plant in our woods to photograph well because of the combination of low leaves and high flowers. That's a flowering shrub of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)

There is another native "soap plant" in our mountains, see this photo. And the flowers of all of our native Ceanothus species in the Rhamnaceae also make suds.
ScientificName: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Ceanothus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Ceanothus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: d519a_d519-041.jpg
Notes:
8756255618 This is a beautiful California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working a new flower of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I first thought it was a Western Tailed Blue (Everes amyntula), but the markings are all wrong for that one. I forget how beautiful these hirstreaks are! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2013)

Today was warm - in the 80's - and not as windy as forecast. I stayed in the canyon, and managed to do an all-butterfly day - including one butterfly-like moth, and a couple from yesterday. I keep having that nagging thought "I'll get no photos today" - and they keep appearing. Like they say in AA, one day at a time!
CommonNames: California Hairstreak; Coffeeberry; Western Tailed Blue
ScientificName: Satyrium californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Everes amyntula
associatedTaxa: Satyrium californica; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Everes amyntula

File.Name: d519b_d519-035.jpg
Notes: 8755111985 Here is a beautiful lady Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly thinking about where to deposit her eggs on a stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family - now renamed Acmispon glaber. See my [Previous] photo. The deerweed has few flowers in this drought year, but there are a few. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2013)
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber

File.Name: d519c_d519-038.jpg
Notes: 8755057623 This is a little lady Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly oviposting (laying eggs) on a stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family - now renamed Acmispon glaber. The deerweed has few flowers in this drought year, but there are a few. Yeah, it's not quite in focus, but I like this photo because it captures the iridescent flecks on the eyes of the wings. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2013)
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber

File.Name: d519d_d519-033.jpg
Notes: 8755020587 This is the first Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) that I've seen this year. It's working flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. It's a diurnal (daytime) moth that I almost count as a butterfly, except it lacks the club-like antennae. Look at all the yellow pollen stuck to its long proboscis! This photo is a bit underexposed, but passable. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2013)

File.Name: d519e_d519-016.jpg
Notes: 8756099490 This is another beautiful Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on an almost-flowering shrub of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I suspect it's looking for a place to lay her eggs. See my [Previous] photo. It looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches - including violet and gold - combine to make an eye-catching brown. These butterflies have been having a good year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2013)
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; pointillist
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; pointillist

File.Name: d519f_d519-047.jpg
Notes: 8754917721 This isn't the best photo, but it shows (I think) a Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly oviposting (laying eggs) on new flower buds of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2013)

File.Name: d519g_d518-040.jpg
Notes: 8756001350 This is a beautiful - and alert! - Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of spiny Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family down by the creek. Yep, it's a tight crop. These butterflies seem to spend all their time chasing away any other butterflies. They are wary, and won't let me get closer. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)

File.Name: d519h_d518-044.jpg
Notes:
8755967656 The composite flowers on the edge are always the hardest to reach!

This is (yet another) Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly reaching for that elusive flower on the edge. The composite flower is Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. You an see the butterfly's soccer ball eyes and powdery hindwings. These is surely the easiest large beautiful butterfly to get close to, but I always enjoy the encounter. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2013)

File.Name: d520a_d520-059.jpg
Notes:
8762011016 This is a male Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly in the subfamily Lycaenidae with his wings open to show the copper color. It's perched on a flower head of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. The females look quite different on top. It's not a common butterfly in our mountains, but I find a few every year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)

I don't think I like the "new look" to Flickr that appeared today without warning. I miss the photo dates and page view counts. What happened to the maps and photo info? That tiny window to enter a photo title is really annoying. I'll give it a try, but it worries me because Fotolog did the same thing last year and totally alienated me and a few other old posters.

Well, they say you get used to hanging if you do it long enough...
ScientificName: Lycaena gorgon; Lycaenidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena gorgon; Lycaenidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d520b_d520-062.jpg
Notes: 8760709315 This is a tattered male Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly in the subfamily Lycaenidae perched on a flower head of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's larger than our local Blues in the subfamily Polyommatinae. You can see a bit of his copper-colored upperwings through the tatters. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)
ScientificName: Lycaena gorgon; Lycaenidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena gorgon; Lycaenidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae

File.Name: d520c_d520-052.jpg
Notes: 8761673382 When I got this photo today I thought I had a new checkerspot - but I guess it's another Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly with unusual markings. It's working a flower cluster of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)

File.Name: d520d_d520-029.jpg
Notes: 8760471045 I assumed without looking too close that this is a beautiful lady Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly, but Alice informs me that it's really a Bernardino Blue (Euphilotes bernardino aka Euphilotes battoides bernardino), a kind of "Square-Spotted Blue". It's smaller, with black checks along the wing margins, and no iridescent flecks on the marginal spots. It's perched for a moment on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family, though its host plant native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family was growing nearby. The males are bluer on their upper wings. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Bernardino Blue; Scrub Oak; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: d520e_d520-056.jpg
Notes: 8761388330 This is another beautiful Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly on a flower cluster of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I didn't even notice the tiny diurnal (daytime) micro-moth across the table when I got this photo. The moth has very long antennae! These butterflies are so painterly - like they were painted by Matisse or Seurat - many color swatches combine to make a rich brown. Look close, and then stand back. I've been finding lots of these butterflies this year. I'm glad something is having a good year! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)

File.Name: d520f_d520-025.jpg
Notes: 8761312492 This is a handsome California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture and salts - on damp gravel soil in the parking area in front of our house. They mostly puddle with their wings folded up. You have to wait for just the right moment to snap a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)

File.Name: d520g_d520-039.jpg
Notes: 8761170948 These are Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies on new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now moved to the Boraginaceae - which is just starting to flower in the mountains. That little beetle on the left is a Metallic Wood-boring Beetle(Buprestidae) in the order Coleoptera. There's also some slime of Spittle Bugs aka Froghoppers (family Aphrophoridae of the order Homoptera) in the lower-right. It's a busy moment! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Trask's Yerba Santa; Metallic Wood-boring Beetle; Spittle Bugs; Froghoppers
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Aphrophoridae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Buprestidae; Coleoptera; Aphrophoridae; Homoptera

File.Name: d520h_d520-004.jpg
Notes: 8759977287 This is (yet another) Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly - on my thumb! I rested my hand on a garden table and got the photo with my other hand. Yes, they are that common right now! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)

File.Name: d521a_d521-030.jpg
Notes:
8779324606 This is a stunning flower of the Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Dry as its been this year, I found hundreds of these today on this windy hillside along Stagecoach Road. I had to wait and wait for the wind to die down a bit. I'll have to get back in the morning. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2013)

I've been having DÈj‡ vu from the Fotolog change last year, but maybe the "New Flickr" isn't so bad. All the old functionality seems to be there, once you figure out the menus. But images on the "home page" are still way too large. There is a time lag. Doesn't this waste their bandwidth?

File.Name: d521b_d520-031.jpg
Notes: 8772554225 These are beautiful new flowers of native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. It's interesting that the flowers are usually red in the mountains near home, but more yellow near the river. Compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)

File.Name: d521c_d520-006.jpg
Notes: 8772098863 This is a large male Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera). It's working new flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. This bee has mites (Acari). They can bring these large bees down. For some reason, I've been noticing them on the males but not the females. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Acari
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Acari

File.Name: d521d_d515-053.jpg
Notes: 8778527932 This is a remarkable Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the insect order Diptera on a flower of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family today. Maybe it's Physocephala burgessi in the subfamily Conopinae? That little red bump in it's left rear leg-pit is a small Mite (Acari). Even flies get ticked! (San Marcos Pass, 15 May 2013)
ScientificName: Conopidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Physocephala burgessi; Conopinae; Acari
associatedTaxa: Conopidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Physocephala burgessi; Conopinae; Acari

File.Name: d521e_d519-007.jpg
Notes:
8778319012 This is a beautiful Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) with mating colors - and ticks! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2013)

New research suggests that lizards blood actually kill the bacteria that carry Lyme disease. Our mountain ticks have a complicated life-cycle that moves from wood rat (rodent) to lizard (reptile) to human (me!). I don't quite understand the details, but I'm glad the lizards act as a filter. I want to understand this.
Locality: me!).  I don't quite understand the details

File.Name: d521f_d520-038.jpg
Notes: 8778172588 This is another wonderful little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working a new flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I guess the fly is genus Bombylius. This fly looks different from my [Previous] photo - maybe it's a male/female thing? (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)

File.Name: d521g_d520-036.jpg
Notes: 8778035632 This is a wonderful little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera flying into new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I guess the fly is genus Bombylius. You can see some pollen on its long proboscis from other flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2013)

File.Name: d521h_d521-025.jpg
Notes: 8771269547 This is the nymph of a Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) - probably a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae. It's looking into the throat of a flower of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Lopseed Family (Phrymaceae). Those darn botanists. They are as bad as the Flickr admins, changing what we've always done! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2013)

File.Name: d522a_d522-026.jpg
Notes:
8786097897 I believe this is a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly perched at a weird angle on a flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It was cool today, and the butterfly is posed like a solar collector to catch some rays. It has real tails and eyespots that the Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) in my [Previous] photo lacks. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2013)

I've been trying to make light of the "New Flickr" stuff, but it makes my tummy hurt. Fotolog did a very similar change last year - and I left, to come back to Flickr. I'm trying to make the best of the changes, posting my usual 8 photos a day, but I won't tolerate them putting their ads on my photos. I'm out of here, or out $50 a year. We'll see.
CommonNames: Hedgerow Hairstreak; Golden Yarrow; Brown Elfin

File.Name: d522b_d522-016.jpg
Notes: 8786014713 This is yet another beautiful Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture and salts - on a garden path. These butterflies are so painterly - like they were painted by Matisse or Seurat - many color swatches combine to make a rich brown. They are so common right now that I almost didn't notice the butterfly in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2013)

File.Name: d522c_d521-014.jpg
Notes: 8785920427 I guess this is another Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture and salts - on a garden path. It was so small, I first thought it was a Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta). These little checkerspots are having a good year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2013)

File.Name: d522d_d522-002.jpg
Notes: 8796342826 This is Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) heaven! This one is working a new flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2013)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d522e_d521-022.jpg
Notes: 8796257406 This is a native Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the canyon. The droopy flower buds are a key characteristic. This one isn't speckled at all. They also go by the name Farewell-to-Spring because of when they flower - and in this dry year, it's true. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2013)

File.Name: d522f_d519-029.jpg
Notes: 8796166228 This is a wonderful native flower of Indian Pink aka Cardinal Catchfly (Silene laciniata) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family. It's just starting to flower in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2013)

File.Name: d522g_d522-006.jpg
Notes: 8785362961 This is another new weed in the mountains. I believe it is Moth Mullein (Verbascum blattaria) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. There have been colonies of this plant near Lake Cachuma and along Stagecoach Road under the bridge, but this is the first plant I've found growing on East Camino Cielo near home. It's rather pretty, but it doesn't belong here. I'll remove it before it makes seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2013)

File.Name: d522h_d522-009.jpg
Notes: 8785285825 I went back to that hillside this morning to get another photo of a Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family. The flowers aren't usually this rosy on the outside, but some are. It was early morning, and still windy. Dry as its been this year, there are hundreds of these on this always windy hillside along Stagecoach Road. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2013)

File.Name: d523a_d523-020.jpg
Notes:
8798601027 "Gotta sing, gotta dance..."

This is one of the parent House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) that occupy a bird house on our front porch - with one of her always hungry babies! There are two almost-fledged babies in the nest. This year we have three active nests around our house. These babies will soon be out of the nest, and we're keeping our cats inside until it happens. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2013)

I had a chore in town too early this morning and again in the afternoon, and I had to wait by the house phone in between. But these birds on the porch were great fun to watch. I watched them for an hour or so. The parents brought in caterpillars, crane flies, sow bugs, crickets, even a spider! They are having better luck finding critters that I am!

Well, one of my pet peeves with the "New Flickr" was solved tonight. Now I have more room to enter a caption for my photos. Maybe someone is listening?

File.Name: d523b_d523-008.jpg
Notes:
8809048438 "What, sow bugs again?"

This is one of the parent House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) that occupy a bird house on our front porch delivering sow bugs to her almost-fledged babies. These critters are also known as Wood Lice - suborder Oniscidea of the order Isopoda of the subphylum Crustacea. These are the kind that don't roll up into a ball. It's a good trick for the bird to hold two of them in its beak at one time! Look close - is it also holding a Crane Fly (Tipulidae, Diptera)? (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2013)
CommonNames: House Wrens; sow bugs; Crane Fly
ScientificName: Troglodytes aedon; Wood Lice; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Tipulidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Troglodytes aedon; Wood Lice; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Tipulidae; Diptera

File.Name: d523c_d523-015.jpg
Notes: 8798357841 This is one of the parent House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) that occupy a bird house on our front porch delivering a cricket or grasshopper (order Orthoptera) to her almost-fledged babies in the nest. (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2013)

File.Name: d523d_d523-019.jpg
Notes:
8798299625 Watch out Mama, that baby's hungry!

This is one of the parent House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) that occupy a bird house on our front porch. She stuffing some critter down the mouth of its baby. This gets increasingly hazardous as the babies mature! (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2013)

File.Name: d523e_d523-007.jpg
Notes:
8798209915 Food goes in, poop comes out!

This is one of the parent House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) that occupy a bird house on our front porch. It's bringing out a neat little "poop bag" from the babies inside. This must be an adaptation - I don't think the adult birds deliver poop in such neat packages! (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2013)

File.Name: d523f_d522-034.jpg
Notes: 8798090147 Here are tiny new flowers of native Phlox-leaved Bedstraw (Galium andrewii) in the Rubiacae plant family. The 4-merous flowers are really small, a mm across or less. It's a "suffrutescent perennial" that forms low mounds in the woods. It's one of my personal favorite native plants, as is the whole genus. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2013)

File.Name: d523g_d522-014.jpg
Notes: 8808432296 These are new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I suppose they aren't the prettiest of flowers, but local insects disagree, and there is usually a buzz of bees and flies about them. There's an interesting rolled leaf or gall in the top-center of my photo, that may be the gall of a Midrib Gall Moth (Sorhagenia nimbosa) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the Lepidoptera. See this photo for a closer look. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2013)
ScientificName: Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Sorhagenia nimbosa; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae; Sorhagenia nimbosa; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: d523h_d522-025.jpg
Notes: 8797799001 This is a beautiful new feathery seed head of California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. This plant is about 3 feet tall, but other plants are lower. It's a fire-follower that doesn't seem to be very common in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 22 May 2013)

File.Name: d524a_d524-041.jpg
Notes:
8809806767 This is another male Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly in the subfamily Lycaenidae working new flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Its showing it's remarkable coppery upper wings. The females look quite different, but I haven't seen them yet this year. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2013)

It was cool and downright windy today. I drove up into the mountains, and did the best I could.
ScientificName: Lycaena gorgon; Lycaenidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena gorgon; Lycaenidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d524b_d524-053.jpg
Notes: 8820335228 I believe this is a male Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working a flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's much bluer than the lady in my [Previous] photo. I think I can make out a speckle under the wing in front. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2013)

File.Name: d524c_d524-045.jpg
Notes: 8820195382 I believe this is a lady Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working a flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. With these Blues in the subfamily Polyommatinae, it's hard to know for sure - but see my [Next] photo of a male butterfly from the same plant that does show a little speckle on its wing. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2013)
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae

File.Name: d524d_d524-035.jpg
Notes: 8809450597 This is another tattered Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working new flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are having a good year, but for some reason I didn't find a single one last year. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2013)

File.Name: d524e_d524-037.jpg
Notes: 8809331743 This is a mighty Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Hemiptera. You can see a bit of it's proboscis folded under its head. This one is perched on a flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This insect is pollen-dusted, but it's definitely a predator. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2013)
CommonNames: Bee Assassin; Assassin Bug; Golden Yarrow
ScientificName: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Hemiptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Hemiptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d524f_d524-070.jpg
Notes: 8819783380 These are new almost-summer flowers of native Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. It's growing with weedy grasses along East Camino Cielo. It's a wonderful plant, but the flowers are prettier from a distance. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2013)

File.Name: d524g_d524-013.jpg
Notes: 8819701576 These are new flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. These are classic hummingbird flowers. The plants in the canyon at home aren't doing so well in this very dry year. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2013)

File.Name: d524h_d524-012.jpg
Notes: 8819463194 Along with ordinary weeds and weedy grasses, we also have weedy shrubs. These are non-native Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) plants in the Fabaceae plant family. It has a Cal-IPC rating of high, which means its a bad invasive weed. I suspect it was originally planted by CalTrans along Highway 154 for erosion control, but it's spread into the mountains. I got this photo - with a car - on East Camino Cielo in the mountains, well away from the highway. I admit the shrub is rather pretty, but it's one of those non-native plants you should not let get established. Kill it while you can! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2013)

File.Name: d525a_d525-015.jpg
Notes:
8835298766 I'm not sure - but I'm willing to bet on it! - that this is the uncommon Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly. It's very plain. This one is angled towards the sun, trying to warm up on a cool day. It's perched on a leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2013)

I wasn't expecting an all-butterfly photo day, but here it is! This one is something new, but I've found all the others before. It's odd that butterflies are the most noticeable insects right now. The woods are really drying out, so I figure I better get them while I can!

File.Name: d525b_d525-009.jpg
Notes: 8835041832 This is yet another beautiful Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly, but it's as much a photo of the new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. These butterflies are so painterly - like they were painted by Matisse or Seurat - many color swatches combine to make a rich brown. They are very common right now , but I keep looking close at them hoping they will turn out to be a more interesting hairstreak. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2013)

File.Name: d525c_d525-005.jpg
Notes: 8834925056 This is the same Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly as my [Previous] photo, showing its lower-wings. It's perched on a fading leaflet of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. I've been trying to get photos showing upper and lower wings of these butterflies, so I can recognize them. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2013)

File.Name: d525d_d525-006.jpg
Notes: 8834770688 These are the upper-wings of a Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly. Maybe it's a female? It's perched on a fading leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It doesn't have the dark borders of the Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly. See my [Next] photo of the same butterfly showing its lower-wings. I've been trying to get photos showing upper and lower wings of these butterflies, so I can recognize them. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2013)
CommonNames: Silvery Blue; Canyon Lupine; Spring Azure
ScientificName: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Celastrina ladon
associatedTaxa: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; Celastrina ladon

File.Name: d525e_d525-018.jpg
Notes: 8834519922 These are the lower-wings of a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly. Maybe it's the same butterfly as my [Previous] photo, maybe not - but it's definitely the same kind of butterfly. It's working new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo of the same butterfly showing its upper-wings. This butterfly is very plain underneath, but quite brilliant on top. I've been trying to get photos showing upper and lower wings of these butterflies, so I can recognize them. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2013)

File.Name: d525f_d525-020.jpg
Notes: 8834324418 These are the upper-wings of a male Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly. It's working new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. See my [Next] photo of the same butterfly showing his lower-wings. This butterfly is very plain underneath, but quite brilliant on top - and more patterned than it is below. I've been trying to get photos showing upper and lower wings of these butterflies, so I can recognize them. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2013)

File.Name: d525g_d525-010.jpg
Notes: 8834099128 This is another handsome California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly perched on new flower of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2013)

File.Name: d525h_d525-003.jpg
Notes: 8833981652 This is another California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek this morning. These butterflies "puddle" in the garden, but I don't often find them perched in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2013)

File.Name: d526a_d526-049.jpg
Notes:
8848415797 Here are a mating pair of Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterflies on a flower head of non-native English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. They look like Blues (Polyommatinae) at first, but the patterns are different. Compare my [Previous] two photos. I'm not sure which butterfly is which. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)

I wasn't expecting another all-butterfly photo day today, but here it is! The NWS forecast was for more wind today, but it wasn't bad this morning. Of course, I've been capturing other insects that butterflies. I need a couple of catch-up days, but where are the rainy days?
ScientificName: Lycaena gorgon; Plantago lanceolata; Plantaginaceae; Polyommatinae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena gorgon; Plantago lanceolata; Plantaginaceae; Polyommatinae

File.Name: d526b_d526-053.jpg
Notes: 8848319341 Here are a pair of Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterflies on new flower heads of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. That's the male on the left and the very different female on the right. Are they giving each other the eye? See my [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)

File.Name: d526c_d526-055.jpg
Notes: 8848772600 I guess this is a female Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly perched on a new flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They look less colorful than the photos in my books, but this is how they are. Note the underwings, and compare my after-next photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)

File.Name: d526d_d526-040.jpg
Notes: 8848698788 This is a beautiful Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's not the sharpest photo, but you can see the butterfly's tails. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)

File.Name: d526e_d526-036.jpg
Notes:
8847923717 I believe this is a Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly perched on a flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has a pointed forewing, very short tails, and a frosted white postmedian line on the hindwing. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)

This one completes our trio of small brown hairstreaks, including the Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly and the Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly.
CommonNames: Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak; Golden Yarrow; Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak; Hedgerow Hairstreak
ScientificName: Satyrium tetra; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Satyrium auretorum; Satyrium saepium
associatedTaxa: Satyrium tetra; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Satyrium auretorum; Satyrium saepium

File.Name: d526f_d526-047.jpg
Notes: 8848458510 This is either a Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini) or an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly. See Alice's comments below. I really can't tell them apart, though the Lupine Blue favors buckwheat and the Acmon Blue favors legumes like lotus. This one is working a flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family - which is no help. The black border on the inside of the orange band does suggest Lupine Blue. Compare my [Previous] photo of (maybe) the Bernardino Blue (Euphilotes bernardino). This one is larger and doesn't have black checks on its wing margins. Whatever it is, it sure is pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)
CommonNames: Lupine Blue; Acmon Blue; Golden Yarrow; Bernardino Blue
ScientificName: Plebejus lupini; Plebejus acmon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Euphilotes bernardino
associatedTaxa: Plebejus lupini; Plebejus acmon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Euphilotes bernardino

File.Name: d526g_d526-018.jpg
Notes: 8848221156 These are the upper wings of a beautiful Blue butterfly in the subfamily Polyommatinae on working new flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It might be another Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini) or an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly, but it might also be a Bernardino Blue aka Square-spotted Blue (Euphilotes bernardino or Euphilotes battoides bernardino). It was quite small, and it seems to have black checks along what's left of its wing margins. Yes, there is native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family growing nearby. Alice thinks it is a small Lupine Blue (Plebejus lupini). See my [Next] photo and her comments. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)
CommonNames: Blue; Golden Yarrow; Lupine Blue; Acmon Blue; Bernardino Blue; Square-spotted Blue; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Plebejus lupini; Plebejus acmon; Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Plebejus lupini
associatedTaxa: Polyommatinae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Plebejus lupini; Plebejus acmon; Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Plebejus lupini

File.Name: d526h_d526-044.jpg
Notes: 8847478297 This is another Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly working a new flower of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This butterfly is very plain underneath, though it has a touch of blue. It's quite brilliant on top. See my photo yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)

File.Name: d527a_d527-020.jpg
Notes:
8862371852 Fire on the mountain!

This is the first smoke we noticed of what has been called the "White Fire" in the Paradise area along the Santa Ynez River. I got this photo around 3:24 pm, from our upstairs porch, looking north across Fremont Ridge. It looks like the fire is coming right towards us from just over the ridge, but it's actually burning east along the other side of Paradise Road. See my [Previous] photo. The wildfire now stands at 700 acres. This fire was apparently started at the White Rock picnic area - *maybe* by someone dumping hot BBQ coals. Memorial Day weekend is a dangerous time, with dangerous people. It's a good reminder to back up my hard drives! (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2013)

I had some other photos ready to go, but that seems kind of superfluous right now. There's always tomorrow.

File.Name: d527b_d527-023.jpg
Notes:
8862032144 Fire on the mountain!

This is the "White Fire" burning in Oso Canyon in the Paradise area along the Santa Ynez River. I got this photo around 5:00 pm - from East Camino Cielo, looking down from the top of the mountains. The wind has been howling over San Marcos Pass from the north, but it seems to be blowing from the west down here - blowing the fire along the river, and away from us. I like that line of fire trucks evenly spaced along Paradise Road. The firefighters will try real hard to keep the wildfire from crossing the road and burning up the Santa Ynez Mountains, towards us and Santa Barbara. This fire has already burned through some of the areas I regularly photograph. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 May 2013)

This fire apparently started at the White Rock picnic area - *maybe* by someone dumping hot BBQ coals. Memorial Day weekend is a dangerous time, with dangerous people.

File.Name: d528a_d528-003.jpg
Notes:
8873332855 Here is another photo of the (I guess) Carrion Beetle (Silphidae) in the suborder Polyphaga of the Coleoptera - maybe the Black Burying Beetle (Nicrophorus nigrita). It's perched on Julie's thumb, before I realized what it was. If you look close, you can see that it's crawling with mites (Acari). I don't think these mites are just hitch-hikers. Look how they are concentrated around the left edge of its thorax - something to do with "trachael tubes"? This beetle landed on my hand last night, and I put it in a jar to get a photo this morning. Something dead in the walls? (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2013)

Damn, there's another wildfire in the Santa Ynez Valley. The "Olive Fire" started around 3:30 pm near the junction of Highways 154 and 246. I heard something about 170 acres and highways closed. The fastest source for local news seems to be Edhat.
CommonNames: Carrion Beetle; Black Burying Beetle; mites; Olive Fire
ScientificName: Silphidae; Polyphaga; Coleoptera; Nicrophorus nigrita; Acari
associatedTaxa: Silphidae; Polyphaga; Coleoptera; Nicrophorus nigrita; Acari

File.Name: d528b_d528-007.jpg
Notes:
8873673420 I guess from its shape that this is a Carrion Beetle (Silphidae) in the suborder Polyphaga of the Coleoptera - maybe the Black Burying Beetle (Nicrophorus nigrita). It's about to take wing from Julie's hand - before I realized what it was. I don't quite understand this beetle's anatomy. Does it flip its wing covers ("elytra") over and fold them down before it takes wing? If you look close, you can see that it's crawling with mites (Acari). See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2013)

This beetle landed on my hand last night, and I put it in a jar to get a photo this morning. Something dead in the walls?

File.Name: d528c_d527-003.jpg
Notes: 8872852813 This is a another tiny Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) in the woods just waiting (and waiting...) on the awns of a seedhead of native Western Rye aka Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus) grass in the Poaceae plant family. I guess it's a Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis), but I really don't know. Not bad for a breezy morning! (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2013)

File.Name: d528d_d527-013.jpg
Notes:
8873309588 OK Alice, I'll try again...

I think this might be the real Bernardino Blue aka Square-spotted Blue (Euphilotes bernardino or Euphilotes battoides bernardino) butterfly working new flowers of a native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae shrub. It's quite small, and it seems to have black checks along its wing margins. It also lacks the black spots along the border of its upper wings, which seems to be a characteristic of Euphilotes Blues. It looks quite rusty. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2013)
CommonNames: Bernardino Blue; Square-spotted Blue; California Buckwheat; Euphilotes Blues
ScientificName: Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
