File.Name: d528e_d528-015.jpg
Notes: 8872573339 This is a beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly visiting the old-fashioned flowers of a Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden. I've seen remarkably few swallowtails in this drought year. I did everything wrong with this photo - ISO 800 and f/7.1, left over from a previous photo - but it's not bad! (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2013)

File.Name: d528f_d527-007.jpg
Notes: 8872493441 This is a new asparagus-like flower stalk of native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family that I found in the woods yesterday. They have large basal ovate leaves that wither away as the flowers appear. This one isn't flowering yet, but it looks like it will be soon. I never did find the the flowers last year, which was also dry. Maybe this year? (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2013)

File.Name: d528g_d528-016.jpg
Notes: 8872925826 This is another small casualty of the "White Fire" now burning in the Paradise area along the Santa Ynez River. It's a native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family, that was trampled at a place in the Santa Ynez Mountains where you could look out and see the fire. There was a crowd of people there yesterday afternoon, and this fine native plant took a hit. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2013)
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: d528h_d528-weather.jpg
Notes:
8872564808 This is the NWS weather forecast for the next few days - windy, very windy, breezy, windy, breezy, and breezy. Not good for the wildfire situation, and not good for photos either. The wind is coming from the north-west. I guess it funnels over San Marcos Pass south towards the ocean, but it also funnels east along the Santa Ynez River canyon towards the backcountry. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)

I did go up to the ridge above the "White Fire" again this morning. I don't want to jinx it, but it looked pretty quiet. The wildfire has actually burned its own "backfire clearing" on the river side. Even so, I wouldn't want to be on the fire lines right now. The wind is really blowing.

Apparently the rumors are true - the fire was started by someone dumping hot BBQ coals. Local TV KEYT news person John Palminteri has a couple of remarkable photos of the White Fire point of origin in the campground at White Rock on Edhat. The person who started the fire left in a hurry.

The fire now stands at 2,025 acres, and 10% contained. I'll bet that 10% is along Paradise Road on the south side. It's still burning away from us.

File.Name: d529a_d529-013.jpg
Notes:
8884471289 This is a tiny "parasitic wasp" (Hymenoptera) inserting its elegantly curved ovipositor into a flower head of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. Maybe its found the larva of a Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) which like to oviposit on these flowers? (See this photo.) I have no idea what kind of wasp it is. It was a challenge to get this photo today in the low light and wind. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2013)

We had a small but sharp earthquake this morning - just magnitude 4.8, but the epicenter was close, and it got me out of bed in a hurry! Drought, wildfires, and earthquakes, oh my! This is starting to feel downright biblical. But it's the incessant wind that's really starting to get me down.
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Tephritidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Tephritidae; Diptera

File.Name: d529b_d529-004.jpg
Notes: 8884337257 These are Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) trees in the Fagaceae plant family surrounded by burned grassland from the "Olive Fire" that happened yesterday afternoon near the junction of Highways 154 and 246. Now it just looks like some some rancher burned his grassland. The oaks look a bit toasted, but otherwise fine. The wildfire burned about 170 acres, but it was a dry grass fire that didn't get into the chaparral. Good job firefighters - it must have been tough to get a handle on this one in the wind yesterday! (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 May 2013)

File.Name: d529c_d526-001.jpg
Notes: 8884828620 This is a cute little Treehopper (family Membracidae in the old insect order Homoptera). Looking at BugGuide, it sure looks like genus Cyrtolobus. This one is perched on a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. It's small, about 1/4 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)
ScientificName: Membracidae; Homoptera; Cyrtolobus; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Membracidae; Homoptera; Cyrtolobus; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: d529d_d526-027.jpg
Notes: 8884123417 This is a wonderful little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working a new flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I guess the fly is genus Bombylius. You can see some pollen stuck to it's long proboscis, which it will transfer to the next flower. Compare my [Previous] photo to get an idea of how long its proboscis really is! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Bombylius
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Bombylius

File.Name: d529e_d526-026.jpg
Notes: 8884068103 This is a wonderful little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera showing just how long its proboscis is! It's hovering above new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I guess the fly is genus Bombylius. Compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Bombylius
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Bombylius

File.Name: d529f_d526-022.jpg
Notes: 8883977111 This is a another pretty Long-horned Beetle in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera. It's perched on a leaf of flowering native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This critter is relatively large, about an inch long. It's been munching the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)

File.Name: d529g_d526-043.jpg
Notes: 8884518760 I guess this is a little Long-horned Beetle in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera. It's perched on a leaf of flowering native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It looks like it has the "blahs". (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)

File.Name: d529h_d526-028.jpg
Notes: 8884457142 These are pretty new galls of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are monothalamus (just one wasp larvae inside) and easily detachable (you can break them off). They look like little mushrooms growing from the oak leaf, but they are the "nests" of tiny wasps. I haven't found them for a few years. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2013)
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; monothalamus; detachable
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; monothalamus; detachable

File.Name: d530a_d530-005.jpg
Notes:
8897210694 This is the tiny nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Hemiptera, on a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's only about 1/4 inch long. It looks like a tiny dragon! You can see its mighty proboscis, like a scythe below its head. I'm always happy when I can make out the "ommatidia" simple-eyes that make up the compound eyes of any insect. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2013)

It was breezy today, but not a bad as its been. I hope our latest "wind event" is all done. But maybe it blew all the larger insects out towards the ocean? I only found nymphs and aphids today. Well, there's always tomorrow.

File.Name: d530b_d530-016.jpg
Notes: 8896395489 This is the late-instar nymph of a Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. This one is on a bristly and glandular seed head of annual Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family, now moved to the Boraginaceae. It's a beautiful insect, and not easy to notice. The red eyes are remarkable. Our usual adult Stilt Bugs are brown in color. I'm not sure if this is something different, or just a step on the way. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2013)
ScientificName: Berytidae; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Hemiptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: d530c_d530-012.jpg
Notes: 8896211431 Here are ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending their aphids (family Aphididae of the order Homoptera) on a plant of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Some of the aphids have their adult wings. This plant will soon have beautiful violet flowers, but others (which look quite different) have white flowers. Native thistles are a confusing group. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2013)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d530d_d530-025.jpg
Notes: 8896775932 I believe these are the galls of the Willow Apple Gall Sawfly (Potania californica) in the family Tenthredinidae of the order Hymenoptera, on a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family. You can see how the galls protrude on both sides of the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2013)
CommonNames: Willow Apple Gall Sawfly; Arroyo Willow
ScientificName: Potania californica; Tenthredinidae; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Potania californica; Tenthredinidae; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: d530e_d530-018.jpg
Notes: 8896090801 This is another photo of beautiful new flowers of native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family, this time growing in the canyon at home. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2013)

File.Name: d530f_d530-003.jpg
Notes:
8895902729 This is a tiny Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) in the woods just waiting (and waiting...) on the awns of a seedhead of native Western Rye aka Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus) grass in the Poaceae plant family. I guess it's a Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis). (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2013)

I've photographed this same tick before, and it's still waiting, as ticks do. This grass is growing over the retaining wall at head height. Whenever I get close, it seems to raise up it's front legs in expectation. I like these photos of things we're not supposed to notice. I suppose one of these days I'll forget about it and brush against the grass - but not yet!
CommonNames: Tick; Western Rye; Blue Wildrye; Pacific Coast Tick
ScientificName: Ixodoidea; Arachnida; Elymus glaucus; Poaceae; Dermacentor occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Ixodoidea; Arachnida; Elymus glaucus; Poaceae; Dermacentor occidentalis

File.Name: d530g_d530-019.jpg
Notes: 8896465736 This is a nice clump of native Western Rye aka Blue Wildrye (Elymus glaucus) grass in the Poaceae plant family, growing along our road. The thin flower heads are quite distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2013)

File.Name: d530h_d530-008.jpg
Notes: 8896336500 This is a non-native grass that has come up on the edge of our parking area. I guess it's a Two-row Barley (Hordeum vulgare) as opposed to a six-row barley. This is the one that's used for beer. Maybe it came from the bird seed in the feeder close by? I guess it counts as a weed, though not a bad one. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2013)

File.Name: d531a_d531-044.jpg
Notes:
8907270875 These are wonderful new flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It's not common in our mountains. It's a summer wildflower. They seem early this year, but I found them on June 5 last year, which is just around the corner. The summer flowers are scarcer than spring wildflowers, but they have their charms! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2013)

It was a very nice day today - warm and not too breezy. Tomorrow is forecast to be quite hot, near 100∞, but then it will cool off again.

File.Name: d531b_d531-002.jpg
Notes: 8907778070 We had a hatch of winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) this morning - from our driveway, not the foundation of our house! The termites are emerging from a crack in the soil at the lower right - the spine of the fallen oak leaf in pointing right at it. Just below the center is a naked termite soldier, sending them on their way. Birds and lizards and jumping spiders were all interested. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2013)

File.Name: d531c_d531-004.jpg
Notes: 8907076395 We had a hatch of winged (alate) Termites (order Isoptera) this morning - from our driveway, not the foundation of our house! (See my [Next] photo.) Birds and lizards and this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) all took notice! The spider is perched on the side of Julie's car. I had to use flash for this photo in the shade. I try to avoid flash, but sometimes it's necessary. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2013)

File.Name: d531d_d531-022.jpg
Notes:
8907601668 I can't resist one more Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on new flowers of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now moved to the Boraginaceae. It's so pretty, the colors of summer. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2013)

Maybe the butterflies really did get blown out to see by the winds earlier this week. I found few today.
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: d531e_d531-032.jpg
Notes: 8906870229 This is a tiny "parasitic wasp" (Hymenoptera) sticking its ovipositer into a new seed of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. At first I was confused because it sure looks like the ovipositor is coming from the front of the abdomen rather than the rear. But look closer! I think the ovipositor is folded under its abdomen from the rear, and the wasp is guiding it down with the junction of its hind legs. I assume there's some critter inside the seed that this wasp is aiming for. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2013)

File.Name: d531f_d531-024.jpg
Notes: 8906770203 I was already focused on the Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on the right, when this other Wild Bee flew into my photo! I suspect the bee of the left is a Megachilid Bee (Megachilidae) - see my [Previous] photo. And I suspect the pretty bee on the right is a Sweat Bee in the family Halictidae. They are on new flowers of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family, now moved to the Boraginaceae. These flowers do attract insects! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2013)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Wild Bee; Megachilid Bee; Sweat Bee; Trask's Yerba Santa
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Megachilidae; Halictidae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Megachilidae; Halictidae; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: d531g_d531-015.jpg
Notes: 8906589919 This is a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a glandular flower of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family, now moved to the Boraginaceae. I'm pretty sure the bee is family Megachilidae because of the pollen hairs on it's abdomen. It seems to be committing the crime of "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2013)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: d531h_d531-008.jpg
Notes: 8907129578 This is another tiny nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Hemiptera, on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It looks spinier than the critter in my [Previous] photo. It looks serious! (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2013)

File.Name: d601a_d601-075.jpg
Notes:
8918899615 This is a stunning Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly ovipositing on new flowers of native Buck Lotus (Lotus crassifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family - now known as Hosackia crassifolia. This is the first of these butterflies I've seen this year. This interesting bushy perennial plant only grows along the summits of the mountains. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)

It was hot today - a neighbor's weather station on the web recorded a high of 102.4 ∞F, but I don't think I believe it. It was cooler at home. Tomorrow is expected to be 10+ degrees cooler.
ScientificName: Leptotes marina; Lotus crassifolius; Fabaceae; Hosackia crassifolia
associatedTaxa: Leptotes marina; Lotus crassifolius; Fabaceae; Hosackia crassifolia

File.Name: d601b_d601-042.jpg
Notes:
8918754551 I'm pretty sure this is a handsome Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on native flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This seems to be where most of the interesting butterflies are hanging out these days. You can see some speckles on the dark "eyes" of its hind wings. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)

The wind today seemed to reach a peak just when I got to this place near the Arroyo Burro saddle today. I made the best of it.

File.Name: d601c_d601-038.jpg
Notes: 8919313160 This is a female Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly showing a glimpse of her upper wings. She working native flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)

File.Name: d601d_d601-048.jpg
Notes: 8918596697 This is a beautiful Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly walking between flower heads of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. They look a lot like Blues (Polyommatinae) at first, but the patterns are different, and they are quite different on top. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)
ScientificName: Lycaena gorgon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena gorgon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae

File.Name: d601e_d601-091.jpg
Notes: 8919064224 This is an interesting Fly (order Diptera) on Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's a summer flower that is just starting to flower. I guess the fly is an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera, maybe genus Thevenetimyia? It's got a red Mite (Acari) behind the coxa of its left-rear leg. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)
CommonNames: Fly; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Bee Fly; Mite
ScientificName: Diptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Thevenetimyia; Acari
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Thevenetimyia; Acari

File.Name: d601f_d601-087.jpg
Notes: 8918922568 This is an interesting Fly (order Diptera) sharing a flower head with a small Bumble Bee (genus Bombus, family Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera). The flower is one of my favorite summer flowers - Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I'm not sure what the fly is, I guess it's an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera, maybe genus Thevenetimyia? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)
CommonNames: Fly; Bumble Bee; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Bee Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Thevenetimyia
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Thevenetimyia

File.Name: d601g_d601-034.jpg
Notes:
8918146865 This is a new flower of the brilliant native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. All of our native thistles seem to be Cirsium occidentale, but there are plants with white, violet, and red flowers. The natives are wonderful, the invasive one are horrible. If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - make sure you know the difference! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)

(And yes Thistle-Garden, I'll try to remember to collect seeds.)
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale

File.Name: d601h_d601-031.jpg
Notes: 8918681604 Here are flowers of native Sticky Snapdragon (Antirrhinum multiflorum) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family, now moved to the Plantaginaceae. I think of it as another fire-follower plant in our mountains, but here it is growing vigorously along East Camino Cielo road. Sometimes clearing is enough to set off these natives. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)

File.Name: d602a_d602-011.jpg
Notes:
8930741749 The beautiful Aliso Canyon trail won't look the same for some time. The canyon was in the direct path of the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area last week, but next year's fire-follower wildflowers should be wonderful! These are burned Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family, and a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) in the Platanaceae plant family on the left. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 June 2013)

It was gloomy on the mountain today when the marine layer spilled over the mountain, so I went down to the newly reopened Paradise Road. There were no signs or barriers, so I took a walk up the Aliso Canyon trail with my camera. I got as far as the first trail fork when a hot shot crew politely asked me to go back. When I got back, there was red tape across the trail. I reckon I was just in time!

The fog stayed on the mountain all day. It was 60's today after 100's yesterday!
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: d602b_d602-007.jpg
Notes: 8931116798 The Aliso Canyon trail wont look the same for some time. This is the first meadow of oak trees up the trail. The canyon was in the direct path of the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area last week. Next years fire-follower wildflowers should be wonderful! Note that the oak tree on the left has lost a couple of large branches. See my [Previous] photo. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 June 2013)

File.Name: d602c_d602-028.jpg
Notes: 8931019528 This is a burned and broken Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family that was in the path of the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area last week. I saw several of these large trees that broke or lost major branches. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 June 2013)

File.Name: d602d_d602-008.jpg
Notes: 8930276511 These are toasted stalks of Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family on burned ground after the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area last week. They are already showing a bit of green! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 June 2013)

File.Name: d602e_d602-035.jpg
Notes: 8930761666 This is a burned-out van and pickup truck that were in the path of the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area last week. That wildfire burned hot! I got this photo in the Sage Hill campground. Campground-host Billy did get his trailor out in time. I hope these were company cars. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 June 2013)

File.Name: d602f_d602-038.jpg
Notes:
8930019117 "Damn, forgot to take out the recycling before the wildfire came!"

This is a melted mass of bottles in the back of the burned-out pickup truck in my [Next] photo. This truck was in the path of the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area last week. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 June 2013)

File.Name: d602g_d602-043.jpg
Notes: 8930428026 This is looking east along the already dry Santa Ynez River, along the path of the "White Fire" last week. The wildfire burned up my favorite part of the river to the ridges in the distance, on the far side of Upper Oso canyon. It's interesting how you can tell the different trees by their color. The burned oaks in the background are gray, the sycamores are mostly reddish-brown, and the cottonwoods are either tan or still green. It looks a little bit like an early autumn - in June! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 June 2013)

File.Name: d602h_d602-047.jpg
Notes:
8930250646 This is the small bend in the Santa Ynez River where I've gotten many photos of late-season butterflies, wasps, and native plants over the years. It was in the path of the "White Fire" last week, that started just around the distant bend. This will be a different summer for my photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 2 June 2013)

It's interesting how you can tell the different tree by their color. The oaks are tan or pale green, the sycamores are mostly reddish-brown, and the cottonwoods are still green.

File.Name: d603a_d603-001.jpg
Notes:
8943488926 I found this cute little Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera) insect posed on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family today, with cloud-drops. The insects seem intelligent to me, and they make eye contact. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2013)

It was dark and drizzly and breezy this morning. I had to use my on-camera flash for this photo. It is nice to have some moisture in the air - we actually got 0.02 inches of moisture in the rain gauge!
ScientificName: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: d603b_d603-003.jpg
Notes: 8943318938 This is a golden Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly grounded by funky weather on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family - with cloud-drops. The drops look like they run off its back like a duck. It was dark and drizzly and breezy this morning. I had to use my on-camera flash for this photo. maybe a Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides)? (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2013)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Ochlodes sylvanoides

File.Name: d603c_d603-004.jpg
Notes: 8943188798 Here are cloud-drops on flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The drizzle last night brought the long flower stalks down to eye level. The stamens have white stems, but the pistils are blue. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2013)

File.Name: d603d_d603-012.jpg
Notes: 8942406369 These are cloud-drops on a seed head of (I guess) non-native weedy Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) in the Poaceae plant family. The grass is being clutched by a tendril of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It was a drippy morning, but it was also breezy. This photo is not quite sharp, but it's still very cool how the grass awns have collected the drops. (San Marcos Pass, 3 June 2013)
ScientificName: Bromus diandrus; Poaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Bromus diandrus; Poaceae; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: d603e_d601-062.jpg
Notes: 8942925502 These are new flower buds of native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae . It's been flowering for a while in the mountains, but I haven't got the right photo yet. A remarkable plant deserves a remarkable photo! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)

File.Name: d603f_d601-020.jpg
Notes: 8942203555 Here are the dreadlock-like male catkins of Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, with a few pistillate acorn cups below the catkins. See my [Previous] photo. This tree is related to oaks and has acorns, but the erect catkins distinguish it. It's not common in our mountains, but it grows here and there on the north-facing slopes. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)

File.Name: d603g_d601-027.jpg
Notes: 8942118673 These are new acorns of Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, with the dreadlock-like male catkins on top. It's related to oaks and has acorns, but the erect catkins distinguish it. It's not common in our mountains, but it grows here and there on the north-facing slopes. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)

File.Name: d603h_d601-069.jpg
Notes: 8942595490 These are new flowers of native Buck Lotus (Lotus crassifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family - now known as Hosackia crassifolia. Note the characteristically flat sprays of pinnate foliage. This is an uncommon plant in our mountains that only grows on the highest peaks. If you look close, you can see a couple of small Bumble Bees (genus Bombus, family Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working the flowers. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 1 June 2013)
ScientificName: Lotus crassifolius; Fabaceae; Hosackia crassifolia; Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Lotus crassifolius; Fabaceae; Hosackia crassifolia; Bombus; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: d604a_d604-020.jpg
Notes:
8954786638 Here are a couple of Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterflies working a new violet flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family. I suspect the skipper on the left is a large Umber Skipper (Poanes melane) and the smaller one on the right is a smaller Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides). (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2013)

The native thistles are as confusing as the skippers. We have red, white, and violet native thistles that all appear to be the same species, though they look quite different. There is a key here that seems to identify this one as var. californicum, but it look quite different from the white thistles in the same variety. Beats me! I'll try to make up a group photo tomorrow.

It was another gloomy morning, but not as drippy as yesterday - and the fog did clear out around 1:00 pm. Maybe the sun will be out tomorrow? We have new flowers on Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden, and it's been too cold for any butterflies to visit. QuÈ L·stima!
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; California Thistle; Umber Skipper; Woodland Skipper; Butterfly Bush
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Poanes melane; Ochlodes sylvanoides; californicum; Buddleia davidii; QuÈ L·stima!
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Poanes melane; Ochlodes sylvanoides; californicum; Buddleia davidii; QuÈ L·stima!

File.Name: d604b_d604-024.jpg
Notes:
8954650702 "Three sheets to the wind?"

This California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly is trying to warm up by tilting its wings towards the sun, just after the fog finally cleared out today. It found it perched on a leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I've see a number of different butterflies doing this, acting like little solar collectors. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2013)

File.Name: d604c_d604-010.jpg
Notes: 8954601258 Here is an odd little Plume Moth (family Pterophoridae in the order Lepidoptera) perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Their outstanding feature is their "cruciform" (cross-like) wings. More than 30 species are recorded in California. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2013)

File.Name: d604d_d604-028.jpg
Notes: 8953290143 This is looking down on a rather large and bristly Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera. It's working new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. These flies are important predators in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2013)

File.Name: d604e_d604-033.jpg
Notes: 8954379068 This is another cute little Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera) insect posed on an old leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. She's a lady - you can see her long ovipositor stretched out behind. The insects seem intelligent to me, and they make eye contact. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2013)
ScientificName: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d604f_d604-006.jpg
Notes: 8954327918 Here are the last seeds hanging on to a large dandelion-like seed head of native Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. It takes a gloomy morning to really bring out the colors of these seeds, though I could do without the breeze! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2013)

File.Name: d604g_d604-031.jpg
Notes: 8952990747 These are aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Compare my [Previous] photo of the normal flowers. You can see the dark aphids (family Aphididae of the order Homoptera) on the flowers. The aphids completely change the flowers - and their physiology, as the galled flowers stay on the plant much longer than normal. Very interesting, though I've found no information about this. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2013)

File.Name: d604h_d604-008.jpg
Notes: 8954043878 These are beautiful flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Yeah, it's not quite sharp - it was foggy with just enough breeze to keep everything moving this morning. But this photo has a nice soft quality to it that the bright sun obscures. The stamens look like little hot dogs in buns, a quality that they lose fast in the sun! As always with this species, there is a umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf that is really two separate leaves grown together and surrounding the new flower stalks. Compare my [Next] photo of aphid-galled flowers, which look very different. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2013)

File.Name: d605a_d605-035.jpg
Notes:
8965665570 Here is another California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, formerly known as Hemaris diffinis) approaching a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family - with its proboscis unrolling in expectation! See my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 June 2013)

It was a beautiful day today - sunny, but not hot. It was breezy at home over the pass, so I drove to the high place between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro.
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Hemaris diffinis; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Hemaris diffinis; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d605b_d605-032.jpg
Notes: 8965559044 This is a California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, formerly known as Hemaris diffinis) snuggling into a flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I've always wondered what insect these flowers with long exserted stamens were designed for, and this might be the one. The flowers seem a bit too long for its body, but you can see a telltale patch of blue pollen on the moth's abdomen. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 June 2013)
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Hemaris diffinis; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Hemaris diffinis; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d605c_d605-048.jpg
Notes:
8965490898 I guess by a process of elimination that this is a Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly on new flowers of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. It's a very plain hairstreak butterfly, with little tails. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 June 2013)

The little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on the left is interesting because I think another of the same kind as the prey of the Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus, Reduviidae, Hemiptera) in my [Previous] photo.
CommonNames: Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak; Toyon; California Holly; Wild Bee; Bee Assassin
ScientificName: Satyrium auretorum; Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: d605d_d605-055.jpg
Notes: 8964160969 This is a mighty Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus) - a large Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Hemiptera with a small Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) for prey. It's been hunting on new flowers of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. See my [Next] photo for the wild bee. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 June 2013)
CommonNames: Bee Assassin; Assassin Bug; Wild Bee; Toyon; California Holly

File.Name: d605e_d605-046.jpg
Notes: 8963957953 This is a remarkable little Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. I first thought they were Assassin Bugs in the family Reduviidae, but they are peaceful plant-suckers in their own family. This one has its proboscis out on new flowers of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 June 2013)
ScientificName: Berytidae; Hemiptera; Reduviidae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Hemiptera; Reduviidae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: d605f_d605-002.jpg
Notes: 8965077368 These are pretty new flower heads of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Did you notice that cute little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working the flowers in the upper-left? It's one of my favorite summer flowers. I have found it before in June, but never here in the mountains. I'm concerned that the summer flowers are coming early in this very dry year. I wonder what I'll find to photograph in September? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 June 2013)
CommonNames: Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Bee Fly

File.Name: d605g_d605-017.jpg
Notes:
8964983262 This is a new "flower" of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Orobanchaceae. They shouldn't be flowering yet. I think of this as a July-September plant, but here they are at the beginning of June. I'm concerned that the summer flowers are coming early in this very dry year. I wonder what I'll find to photograph in September? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 June 2013)

The small white flowers are hard to find in the green leafy bracts, and they never open much more than this. But it's a tough plant that flowers during the dry times, and you have to respect it.

File.Name: d605h_d605-009.jpg
Notes: 8963663761 These are new summer flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family - but they shouldn't be flowering yet. I think of this as a July-September plant, but here they are at the beginning of June. I'm concerned that the summer flowers are coming early in this very dry year. I wonder what I'll find to photograph in September? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 5 June 2013)

File.Name: d606a_d606-012.jpg
Notes:
8974583916 This is the first caterpillar I've seen this year of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly - showing its brilliant orange "osmetarium". It really looks like gummy bear candy! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)

The first time I saw this, it caught me by surprise and I jumped - but there's a trick to it. Take a dead grass stem and stroke the caterpillar above its eyes, and see what happens. Reminds me of that scene in the movie "Blazing Saddles" - "Excuse me while I whip this out!" I found this caterpillar on the stem of a non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family, where they all seem to be. It's a native butterfly that prefers a non-native host plant. Well, good - I wish there were more of them!

Today was another sunny but relatively cool day. There was enough of an annoying breeze that none of these photos is quite sharp.

File.Name: d606b_d606-023.jpg
Notes: 8973304687 This is a fantastic male Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly, showing the rich colors on its upper wings. This one must be newly emerged - they get duller with age. (Don't we all.) This one is perched on a leaf of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now moved to the Boraginaceae. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Trask's Yerba Santa
ScientificName: Lycaena arota; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena arota; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: d606c_d606-034.jpg
Notes: 8974433008 Here is a beautiful Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure it's a male. I found quite a few of these today, perched on different leaves. I reckon they're waiting for females - not unlike the scene at any local bar! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Scrub Oak

File.Name: d606d_d606-037.jpg
Notes:
8973076601 Look close! At the top is a new flower head of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family - now renamed Acmispon glaber, which is a much less harmonious name and harder to remember. Below the flowers is a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae), and below that is an unfortunate butterfly in its grip. This spider has good taste in butterflies - I believe that is a small Bernardino Blue aka Square-spotted Blue (Euphilotes bernardino or Euphilotes battoides bernardino) butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)

Whenever you notice a butterfly hanging at a strange angle, investigate!
CommonNames: Deerweed; Crab Spider; Bernardino Blue; Square-spotted Blue
ScientificName: Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Acmispon glaber; Thomisidae; Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino

File.Name: d606e_d606-006.jpg
Notes: 8974201236 This is a tattered Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly on sweet-smelling Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii) in the Lamiaceae plant family - now renamed Clinopodium douglasii. See my [Previous] photo. It's probably working a new flower, but I can't make it out. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae; Clinopodium douglasii
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae; Clinopodium douglasii

File.Name: d606f_d606-008.jpg
Notes: 8972839499 These are new flowers of sweet-smelling Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii) in the Lamiaceae plant family - now renamed Clinopodium douglasii. It smells like peppermint! I appreciate that "yerba buena" means any local plant that smells like peppermint, but in California this is THE yerba buena, the original namesake of the city of San Francisco. It has a spicy taste and makes great peppermint tea when dried. It's not common in our woods, but I find it here and there. There's a large native colony in the canyon, and this garden plant is from a cutting taken many years ago. It doesn't mind summer water. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)

File.Name: d606g_d606-003.jpg
Notes:
8972783183 "Fearful symmetry"

This photo shows new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family, with stamens crossed and blue-tipped pistils to the side. It's like a heraldic badge - with flowers instead of lions! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)

File.Name: d606h_d606-044.jpg
Notes: 8973934052 These are the wonderful new flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It's not common in our mountains. It's a summer wildflower. They seem early this year. They are growing on a very steep hillside, with a spiny yucca at the bottom. I haven't ventured down for a closer view! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)

File.Name: d607a_d607-031.jpg
Notes:
8982206133 This is a single flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family - our native Tiger Lily. It's the first one I've seen this year. They are quite large - the size of my open hand. It looks like it belongs in a garden, but this one is growing wild and free. These flowers are not rare, but not common either. It's always a surprise to find them. All it needs is a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working the flower! These flowers seem early this year, but checking the archive they flowered last year on June 11. I guess they're right on time. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

It was another beautiful day in paradise. A little breezy in the morning, but dead calm as the day went on. I got to this lily flower just as the sun was leaving. I'll try to get back earlier tomorrow.
ScientificName: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio rutulus
associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio rutulus

File.Name: d607b_d607-021.jpg
Notes: 8983243632 I'm confused by local Hairstreak (subfamily Theclinae) butterflies. This one might well be the same Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly as my [Previous] photo in better light, but it looks different and it acted differently. Instead of peacefully sharing Toyon flowers, this one is perched on a leaf and is aggressive, chasing all comers from a patch of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It doesn't have the "white frosting" of a typical Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) butterfly, and I didn't notice the color of the upper wings when it took flight. Whatever it is, it's quite pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)
CommonNames: Hedgerow Hairstreak; Golden Yarrow; Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak
ScientificName: Theclinae; Satyrium saepium; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Satyrium tetra
associatedTaxa: Theclinae; Satyrium saepium; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Satyrium tetra

File.Name: d607c_d607-024.jpg
Notes: 8981933923 I believe this is a plain Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly working new flowers of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. There were quite a few of these butterflies on the flowers, and they had rusty-orange upper wings when they took to the air. The light is contrasty, so it's hard to make out details. Compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d607d_d607-009.jpg
Notes: 8983054588 This is an atypical little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. It has gold flecks on its abdomen and thorax, very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d607e_d607-029.jpg
Notes: 8983013408 These are brilliant new flowers of native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae . It's been flowering for a while in the mountains - it took some time to get a photo I liked. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d607f_d607-015.jpg
Notes: 8981726037 Here are pretty new flowers of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus var. foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flower buds are especially colorful. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d607g_d607-010.jpg
Notes: 8981600627 I'm afraid that this is all there'll be of new flowers of Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's an unusual composite with heads of two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know - but I don't believe we'll see any of the pretty flowers in this drought year. I guess these are "cleistogamous" - flowering without opening? I also noticed this last year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d607h_d606-047.jpg
Notes:
8981540977 This is a new flowering plant of native Hemp Dogbane aka Indian Hemp (Apocynum cannabinum) in the Apocynaceae plant family. That's hemp as in fiber, not smoking. It's an uncommon plant in our mountains - I only know it from a few places. I wish the photo was taken in some uncommon habitat, but I found this one growing from a crack in the old asphalt of Kinevan Road! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)

This was one of the most important native plants for the native American Chumash in our mountains - for string, rope, nets, and clothing. For more info, see Jan Timbrook's book on Chumash Ethnobotany (2007).

File.Name: d608a_d608-005.jpg
Notes:
8991268871 This is the beautiful nymph of a "True Bug" in the insect order Hemiptera on a ripening seed pod of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I first thought it was a weevil beetle, but you can clearly see it's sucking proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2013)

This was a crummy day for new photos, though I got a couple. We were stuck in the "June Gloom" fog layer, with wind. It never did really clear up today, though I saw some blue sky at 4:30 or so. I hope it's not like this for the Live Oak Music Festival which is coming up next weekend. Our kids, grandkids, and good friends will all arrive on Wednesday for the occasion. I've been looking forward to this all year, like I do every year!

File.Name: d608b_d606-019.jpg
Notes: 8992307000 This is a scene on new flowers of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family, now moved to the Boraginaceae. There's a Long-horned Beetle in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera in front, and a couple of interesting little Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in back. The untidy flowers never open much more than this, but many insects like them. As Alice pointed out in her comment, the beetle sure looks like Judolia sexspilota. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)
CommonNames: Branching Phacelia; Long-horned Beetle; Bee Flies
ScientificName: Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Judolia sexspilota

File.Name: d608c_d606-032.jpg
Notes: 8992198958 This is a pretty little Beetle (Coleoptera) hiding in a furry leaf native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family - now moved to the Boraginaceae. It might be Trirhabda flavolimbata in the Chrysomelidae family. They have pretty iridescent green larvae on the same plants. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Trirhabda flavolimbata; Chrysomelidae

File.Name: d608d_d606-027.jpg
Notes: 8990831957 This is an odd wasp gall on leaves of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It sure looks like the gall of a Yellow Wig Gall Wasp (Andricus fullawayi) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but I usually find those on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) - a "white oak." Maybe the wasps cross over to this "intermediate oak" - or maybe it's something else? (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2013)
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Yellow Wig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: d608e_d606-016.jpg
Notes:
8991707068 This is a triptych of native thistles. It's curious that they are all considered the same species - Cirsium occidentale in the Asteraceae plant family, but there are differences. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2013, 6 June 2013, 4 June 2013)

The Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) on the left is the most distinctive. I believe the "White Thistle" in the middle is the California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm not sure of the "Violet Thistle" on the right. It may just be a variant of the "White Thistle", but it really does look different - for example the involucral bracts are less hairy. I usually call it Cirsium occidentale without the variety epitaph. There is a key to Cirsium occidentale which places the white and violet thistles together. They do look different to me, but the botanists are in charge.

If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - make sure you know the difference between the natives and the weeds!
Locality: Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm not sure of the "Violet Thistle" on the right. It may just be a variant of the "White Thistle
CommonNames: Red Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; California Thistle
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale

File.Name: d608f_d608-003.jpg
Notes: 8991590878 It was gloomy and windy today, but I got this photo of the native trees on the wild edge of our garden. Those are Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae plant family up front; with Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family that is getting ready to flower behind; and a background of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family. The Madrones look a little ragged, but I guess they're just dropping old leaves. I don't think that's water stress - yet. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2013)
CommonNames: Madrone; Tanbark Oak; Tanoak; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d608g_d607-001.jpg
Notes: 8991497212 These are new flowers of native Chalk Lettuce (Dudleya pulverulenta) in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family, but this plant is growing in our garden. See my [Previous] photo. These plants occur naturally across the valley on Figueroa Mountain, on the sunnier side of the valley. I believe we got this plant from Ron and Diane Griffin, who grew it from seed at Manzanita Nursery in Solvang. It seems happy in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d608h_d607-004.jpg
Notes: 8990193523 This is a garden photo, showing on the right a flowering native Chalk Lettuce (Dudleya pulverulenta) in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family. This plant is happily growing in our garden, but it occurs native across the valley on Figueroa Mountain, on the sunnier side of the valley. I believe we got this plant from Ron and Diane Griffin, who grew it from seed at Manzanita Nursery in Solvang. There are other natives in the photo. We have that kind of garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d609a_d609-019.jpg
Notes:
9001707339 These are "June Gloom" marine layer clouds rolling over San Marcos Pass today. I got tired of the fog and went for a drive up East Camino Cielo road today. I did get above the fog, but not the "fog winds" that go with it - that layer of turbulent air where the cool air from the coast on the left meets the warmer inland air. Our house is about 1,000 feet down in that gloom. It never cleared today. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 June 2013)

The good news is that the Santa Ynez Valley looked mostly clear. I'll try to get down there tomorrow. And the Live Oak Music Festival which is coming up next weekend - camping in the valley with lots of music. Our kids, grandkids, and good friends will all arrive on Wednesday for the occasion. I've been looking forward to this all year, like I do every year!

File.Name: d609b_d609-016.jpg
Notes: 9002795648 "June Gloom" marine layer clouds rolling over San Marcos Pass today. I got tired of the fog and went for a drive up East Camino Cielo road today. I did get above the fog, but not the "fog winds" that go with it - that layer of turbulent air where the cool air from the coast meets the warmer inland air. Our house is somewhere down in that gloom. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 9 June 2013)

File.Name: d609c_d609-009.jpg
Notes:
9001441581 Pretty Good Camouflage - despite the brown colors in the green woods!

I found this small Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) along our road this morning. It went up the hillside and stopped to nibble - and I got this photo from the open window of my truck, resting my camera on the window frame. It's the size of a goat, so I reckon it's a yearling. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2013)

File.Name: d609d_d609-001.jpg
Notes: 9002521482 I found this small Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera today on a drippy morning. I guess it's an Issid Planthopper (Issidae) - very likely Danepteryx robusta with those narrow wings. (See BugGuide.) They have amazing eyes. I guess they need them to see where they're hopping. It's perched on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family - with cloud drops. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2013)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Danepteryx robusta; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Danepteryx robusta; Lathyrus vestitus; Fabaceae

File.Name: d609e_d609-013.jpg
Notes:
9002387764 These are nearly-open flowers of native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. The flowers open in the late afternoon. I was hoping the flowers would be more open on a gloomy day, but I guess not. See my [Previous] photo for a shot of the whole plants. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2013)

The plants were useful for the Chumash people for food, soap, fiber, glue, and ... fish poison! The bulbs were crushed and stirred into pools on a creak, and the fish floated to the surface. Apparently the soapy saponins in the root messed with the fish's gills and didn't affect the edibility. I've always wanted to test that trick.

File.Name: d609f_d609-014.jpg
Notes:
9000960175 These are tall asparagus-like flower stalks of native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. They stand taller than I am! The crinkly leaves at the base are already brown in this dry year. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2013)

The plants have large fist-sized bulbs covered with coarse brown fibers. They definitely feel soapy. We have a couple in the garden that we've found over the years, but they've never flowered. They were useful for the Chumash people for food, soap, fiber, glue, and ... fish poison! The bulbs were crushed and stirred into pools on a creak, and the fish floated to the surface. Apparently the soapy saponins in the root messed with the fish's gills and didn't affect the edibility. I've always wanted to test that trick.

File.Name: d609g_d609-005.jpg
Notes: 9002038172 Here is the rose-like yellow flower of a native "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia) cacti in the Cactaceae plant family - with cloud drops. See my [Previous] photo. Julie vaguely recalls that we collected a piece of this cactus many years ago in the Santa Monica mountains. It seems happy in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2013)

File.Name: d609h_d601-008.jpg
Notes: 9001912434 This is the "cactus corner" of our garden, with mostly native "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia) cacti that we've collected over the years. I don't know what any of them are, but Julie vaguely recalls that the yellow-flowered one on the right with long thorns might be from the Santa Monica mountains, and the pink or rose flowered one with short thorns might be a hybrid from Baja. The pink ones have ripe fruit from last year's flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2013)

File.Name: d610a_d610-008.jpg
Notes:
9012922608 Here's another photo of the interesting Stiletto Fly, probably genus Thereva in the family Therevidae of the order Diptera, on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. If compare my [Previous] photo, you can see how her abdomen is more distended. I think she's ovipositing - laying her eggs - or at least getting in the mood. It's abdomen reminds me of those collapsible cups they used to sell for backpacking. Here the cups are more opened up. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2013)

The "June gloom" marine layer did clear out today, and it became quite nice - but unseasonably cool, only in the upper 60s. This might be my last new photo for a week. I have to do shopping tomorrow, our kids and grandkids and friends are coming on Wednesday, and the lineup for the Live Oak Music Festival starts on Thursday afternoon. That is really the first day of the festival! See my photos from last year - start here and click [Previous].

File.Name: d610c_d610-011.jpg
Notes: 9011480325 This is a relatively small Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera. It's perched on a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2013)

File.Name: d610d_d610-016.jpg
Notes: 9011363341 This is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, munching leaves or flower buds of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. These caterpillars are web-spinners. Sometimes they shroud the leaves with their webs, like tent caterpillars. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2013)

Notes: 9012444328 This is the beautiful caterpillar of a native Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin. It has clown colors! This one is on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The caterpillars are beautiful, but the adult moths are not. The females are wingless and just emerge to mate and lay eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2013)

File.Name: d610f_d610-021.jpg
Notes: 9011172277 This is a beautiful Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) - with mating colors. It's a fence lizard on a fence post. This one is in a strange pose, but it about to take off. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2013)

File.Name: d610g_d610-031.jpg
Notes: 9012302710 These are wonderful new flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I used to wonder what insect the long pistils and stamens were adapted for, but maybe I discovered it in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2013)

File.Name: d610h_d610-035.jpg
Notes: 9012208452 This is another open yellow flower of a native "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia) cacti in the Cactaceae plant family. Julie and I worked through the keys, and I believe this is the Coastal Pricklypear (Opuntia littoralis), probably from the Santa Monica Mountains. It has long spines. We collected a piece of it many years ago, and it's doing well in the "cactus corner" of our garden - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2013)

File.Name: d611a_d611-026.jpg
Notes:
9020134361 I believe this is an uncommon Carrot Wasp in the family Gasteruptiidae in the order Hymenoptera - almost certainly genus Gasteruption, maybe Gasteruption visaliae. I have no idea what the common name means. It looks a lot like an Ichneumon Wasp (Ichneumonidae), but the distinct neck distinguishes it. I've seen them before. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2013)

It turns out our kids don't arrive until 10 pm tomorrow night. I put off shopping for the Live Oak Music Festival until tomorrow, but I've been distracted today. I don't know if I'll be able to post photos tomorrow night. The lineup starts on Thursday afternoon, really the first day of the festival. See my photos from last year - start here and click [Previous].

Notes:
9022173880 I believe this is an uncommon Carrot Wasp in the family Gasteruptiidae in the order Hymenoptera - almost certainly genus Gasteruption, maybe Gasteruption visaliae. I have no idea what the common name means. It looks a lot like an Ichneumon Wasp (Ichneumonidae), but the distinct neck distinguishes it. File.Name: d611c_d611-001.jpg
Notes: 9019895245 I found this pretty little gold-flecked Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. I don't know anything about it, but I've seen them before. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2013)

File.Name: d611d_d611-023.jpg
Notes: 9021870126 I believe this is a large Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) in the family Syrphidae of the order Diptera - or kin. When I first found them, I mistook them for Horse Flies - that's how big they are. This one is working new flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. It has a remarkable proboscis that is barely visible in this photo - it literally blows out its face to produce it! I know this isn't the best photo - it was breezy today and the flower stalks were swinging back and forth. I did what I could. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2013)

Look how dried out the leaves on the stem are in this drought year. I can't recall ever seeing this before. This is a tough year for wildflowers, but you have to admire these plants that give it their all. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2013)

File.Name: d611h_d610-019.jpg
Notes: 9019417963 Here are very beautiful new flowers of native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family, growing in the canyon at home. I'm not sure what I like best - the open flowers or the waxy flower buds. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2013)

File.Name: d612a_d612-001.jpg
Notes:
9028539103 Here is a large black Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera). It's grounded on our upstairs porch railing because the coastal marine layer fog rolled over the mountains this morning, and the temperature dropped dramatically. It seems to be missing its right foot. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2013)

This was my only photo today. I've been busy getting ready for the Live Oak Music Festival this weekend. Our kids and grandkids and friends arrive later tonight. The lineup starts on Thursday afternoon, really the first day of the festival. See my photos from last year - start here and click [Previous]. If you attend the festival, look (and listen!) for me at the SW corner of Mandolin Lane and the upper bend of Live Oak Way. I'm the guy with the long white beard!

Notes: 9028270067 These are peas in a pod of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family - three good seeds, one shriveled one, and many aborted ones. I'm not surprised the seed count is so low in this very dry drought year. The good news is that there are three seeds! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2013)

File.Name: d612e_d607-017.jpg
Notes: 9030461084 These are unripe fruit of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. The old spanish name for this plant is Islay. The ripe red fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit are actually poisonous. The flesh is very thin, and the Chumash people mostly ate the large pits after leaching them. If you crush a leaf between your fingers, you can smell the wild cherry! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d612f_d607-014.jpg
Notes: 9028198475 These are ripening red berries of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. This shrub is scattered in the chaparral and woodland. The berries get juicy when they are ripe, but they are quite bitter. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d612g_d607-013.jpg
Notes: 9030326548 These are large clumps of the native grass Small-flowered Melic (Mellica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family. It does well after wildfires and brush clearings, like this place. Unlike the weedy annual grasses in the foreground, these perennials are not dead. If you look close, you can still see some green in there. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2013)

File.Name: d612h_d604-004.jpg
Notes: 9030244196 This is an odd Agaricus mushroom - odd because of where and when it's fruiting. I found this in a big "cannibal pot" full of soil in the garden, and it's fruiting now in a very dry summer. I have no idea what kind of mushroom it is. The spores have left a neat brown spore print on the top of the ring. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2013)

Notes:
9079495653 This is the music jam in our campground at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend. It was actually a pretty quiet weekend for us, since our main singer Wil didn't show up, but we still managed to play late every night. The musicians are Ewan, Josh, John, Jake, Spencer, Charlie, Brianna, Colleen, Bill, Kristin, Aubry, and a few more who wandered in. These are people I love! I'll get back to my nature photos tomorrow, as weather and company allow. It was very windy today, and wind is in the forecast for the next couple of days. And we have a house full of kids and grandkids!

File.Name: d619a_d619-007.jpg
Notes:
9090190946 This is the large Darkling Ground Beetle (family Tenebrionidae, order Coleoptera) that I found under the odd Agaricus mushroom in my my two [Previous] photos. There are many different kinds of these beetles, so I won't try to guess which one this is. It seems to to have fused wing covers. It also seems to be missing its right-front leg. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2013)

Just four photos today because I've been busy with my kids and grandkids. We went to Refugio Beach today and had a great time.

Today was another very windy day with scary "Sundowner Winds". We don't get real Santa Ana winds in the Santa Barbara mountains because there is no direct path to the desert. But sometimes the winds blow over the mountain passes and build in intensity and heat as they compress and blow through the canyons towards the coast. It was in the 80s today on San Marcos Pass, but in the 90s in Santa Barbara. One of these wind events helped the Paint Fire of June 1990. The fire started in the mountains and headed south for the coast, crossing Highway 101 before the winds changed. This fire could have literally taken out the city of Santa Barbara.

An extreme sundowner wind event may have happened on June 17, 1859 when it is said that the temperature in Goleta rose to 133∞F. This local legend has been questioned. See this web page from UCSB.

File.Name: d619b_d619-002.jpg
Notes: 9090085586 I unearthed this odd Agaricus mushroom on the dry side of our garden this morning, and at the same time I unearthed a large Darkling Ground Beetle (family Tenebrionidae, order Coleoptera) - which you can see scurrying away in the middle-left. See my [Next] photo for a closer shot of the beetle and my [Previous] photo for the mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2013)

File.Name: d619c_d619-011.jpg
Notes: 9087756887 This is an odd Agaricus mushroom - odd because of where and when it's fruiting. It's fruiting in a sandy place on the dry edge of our garden where we never water, and it's fruiting now in our very dry almost-summer. (The summer solstice happens on June 21.) I'm not sure what kind of Agaricus it is, and I have no time for research tonight. If you have an idea, please leave a comment. Note the tiny fly or wasp on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2013)

File.Name: d619d_d619-013.jpg
Notes: 9089927334 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on the tip of a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Yeah, it's a bit overexposed, and I only had one chance for the photo before the spider jumped away. But it looks so cute with those puppy-dog eyes (and eyes)! (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2013)

File.Name: d620a_d620-028.jpg
Notes:
9095061197 If you were driving over San Marcos Pass today, the last thing on your mind would be wild orchids in the summer-dry woods, but here they are! These are fully-open new flowers of native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family that I found in the woods today. They have large basal ovate leaves that wither away before the flowers appear. The plants really look like little asparagus shoots, 1 or 2 feet tall. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2013)

Today is another windy day with "sundowner winds" just starting to develop. A sure sign is that the Santa Barbara foothills are warmer than the mountains. I'm hoping that tonight will be the end of the winds.

The summer solstice is officially tomorrow on June 21, but it really happens tonight on June 20 at 10:04 p.m. PDT. That's at 05:04 UTC in Greenwich, England - where time starts. It's the first day of summer when the sun is highest above the ecliptic and the path of the sun is highest in the sky. *Sigh* That means we have the rest of summer to look forward to - something to reckon with in this very dry year.

File.Name: d620b_d620-032.jpg
Notes: 9097222644 These are pretty new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's an unusual composite with heads of two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. I didn't think they would flower in this very dry summer - see this photo - but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2013)

File.Name: d620c_d620-009.jpg
Notes: 9094940755 This is a remarkable little Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. I first thought they were Assassin Bugs in the family Reduviidae, but these are peaceful plant-suckers in their own family. They are remarkably thin, like little walking sticks. This one is walking across a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2013)

File.Name: d620d_d620-004.jpg
Notes: 9094745901 I guess this is a Leatherwing Beetle (family Cantharidae, order Coleoptera). It's perched with head down on a leaf of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Maybe this beetle is genus Cantharis. There was low light and already a breeze this morning - nothing to do except bump up the ISO another step. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2013)

File.Name: d621a_d621-029.jpg
Notes:
9104559684 This is a wonderful California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, formerly known as Hemaris diffinis) working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's a diurnal (daytime) Sphinx moth (Sphingidae). It's great fun to try to catch these moths in the air while they fly among the flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 June 2013)

The wicked "sundowner winds" seem to have finally run their course. We're back to normal afternoon winds. I drove up to the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro today. I was looking for Calochortus fimbriata - not yet, but they are coming.

File.Name: d621c_d621-021.jpg
Notes:
9104401892 Be careful little wing, some of these flowers have claws!

Here is a small but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the old order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It's well-camouflaged in a summer flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The bugs are small, but once you notice one you'll probably find more. It has a sucking proboscis folded under its head and large crab-like front legs with claws for grasping its prey. They can bring down insects much larger than they are. This is the first one I've noticed this year. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 June 2013)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
