Notes:
9104347552 Be careful little wing, some of these flowers have fangs!

This is a large white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting... waiting... on a summer flower head of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. He's sure to catch something - these flowers are one of the few that are still flowering in the summer-dry mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 June 2013)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: d622a_d622-037.jpg
Notes:
9112674100 I believe this is a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly resting on leaves of native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's leaning over to catch some sun rays. This butterfly looks very similar to the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra), but that one is grayer with white frosting beyond the white postmedian line. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2013)

Butterflies have been scarce ever since the very windy days earlier this week. I wonder is the the butterflies really did get blown away! Today was breezy, with wind from the coast instead of hot winds from the interior. Our house is still full of kids and grandkids. Four photos is just fine today.
CommonNames: Hedgerow Hairstreak; Pitcher Sage; Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak
ScientificName: Satyrium saepium; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae; Satyrium tetra
associatedTaxa: Satyrium saepium; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae; Satyrium tetra

File.Name: d622b_d622-011.jpg
Notes: 9112612296 Here is a Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly on open - as open as they ever are - flowers of Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Yep, the flowers don't look like much, but the spent flower heads are nice in floral arrangements. My mother used to call these "Pearly Everlasting" - but the real Pearly Everlasting (Anaphalis margaritacea) grows further north and has whiter flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2013)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Gnaphalium californicum; Asteraceae; Anaphalis margaritacea
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Gnaphalium californicum; Asteraceae; Anaphalis margaritacea

File.Name: d622c_d622-001.jpg
Notes:
9112467960 It looks like this little fly did a face-plant on the leaf!

Actually, it's dying. This is a small but gorgeous iridescent green - and blue and orange, depending how you look at it - Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the dry edge of our garden. It was twitching when I noticed it, and then it just... died. We've never used any poisons in our garden, so it's curious. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2013)

File.Name: d622d_d622-016.jpg
Notes: 9110186247 Here are more fantastic new flowers of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family - our native Tiger Lily. The plants grow in the canyons. This one is about 7 feet tall with a single tall stalk with whorled leaves and 12 flower buds. It's growing along West Camino Cielo Road, and it's a wonder that some fool hasn't cut the stalk. They look too beautiful to be native, but they are. There are still no Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterflies working them. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2013)
ScientificName: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio rutulus
associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio rutulus

File.Name: d623a_d623-013.jpg
Notes:
9122607672 These are the intricate new flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. They are native, but kind of weedy in the Santa Ynez Valley. I found this plant on Jacob Grant's Roots Organic Farm near Los Olivos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 June 2013)

We took the kids and grandkids to visit Jacob's farm today. Jacob is a former student of mine at Dunn Middle School from way back, and we still stay in touch. He's doing well selling truck crops at local farmers' markets. We had a great time picking strawberries, carrots, and cut flowers.

The kids fly back to Portland, Oregon tomorrow morning at 8:00 am. I hate to see them go, but it will be nice to get some time back.

File.Name: d623b_d623-051.jpg
Notes:
9120287811 I don't think I've ever seen so many "oak apple" galls in a tight cluster like this. These are the galls of California Gall Wasps (Andricus quercuscalifornicus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera). They are growing on a twig of flowering Valley Oak (Quercus lobata in the Fagaceae plant family. But why are there so many galls clustered on this one branch?

I found the galls on Jacob Grant's Roots Organic Farm near Los Olivos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 June 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae

File.Name: d623c_d623-049.jpg
Notes: 9122420024 Here are kids playing on an old piece of farm equipment on Jacob Grant's Roots Organic Farm near Los Olivos. I guess it's a hay loader, made to scoop up bales and load it into a truck? But to the kids, it was an airplane taking them to Africa! From left to right, that's Amelie, Otto, Kelvin, and Orin. Otto and Kelvin are my grandkids. Amelie and Orin are Jacob and Genevieve's kids. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 June 2013)

File.Name: d623d_d623-009.jpg
Notes: 9120045007 This is the lettuce corner of Jacob Grant's Roots Organic Farm near Los Olivos. Jacob is a former student of mine at Dunn Middle School from way back, and we still stay in touch. He's doing well selling truck crops at local farmers' markets. We took our kids and grandkids to visit today, and had a great time picking strawberries, carrots, and cut flowers. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 June 2013)

File.Name: d624a_d624-031.jpg
Notes:
9129535579 This is another pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly. It's a little hard to see in my photo, but this butterfly is tilted sideways, like a sail. I guess it's try to catch some sun rays on a gloomy day. You can clearly see the iridescent speckles on its hindwing marginal spots. This one is perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. There was native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2013)

It's interesting that these butterflies trying to get warm always hold their wings together instead of separating them. With solar panels, we need maximum exposure; but maybe these butterflies need to minimize exposure to reduce heat loss?

It was dark and gloomy and breezy today as a dry storm front passed over - really a lousy day for photos, but I got a few. Our kids and grandkids got on their plane back to Portland early this morning. The house feels quiet, and empty.
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Coast Live Oak; Deerweed
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: d624b_d624-026.jpg
Notes: 9131591754 This is a pretty little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly. You can clearly see the iridescent speckles on its hindwing marginal spots. It's a little hard to see in my photo, but this butterfly is tilted sideways, like a sail. I guess it's try to catch some sun rays on a gloomy day. This one is perched on a dead stem of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. There was native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2013)
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: d624c_d624-034.jpg
Notes: 9131457774 Here is a cute little snout-nosed Weevil in the superfamily Curculionidae of the Coleoptera. Maybe it's a Tooth-nosed Snout Weevils (Rhynchitinae) in the family Attelabidae? There were quite a few of these on this one Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I suspect one of these weevils became prey for the Jumping Spider (Salticidae) in my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2013)
CommonNames: Weevil; Tooth-nosed Snout Weevils; Coast Live Oak; Jumping Spider

File.Name: d624d_d624-019.jpg
Notes:
9129224633 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's got a small Beetle (Coleoptera) as prey, probably a Weevil in the family Curculionidae. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2013)

I know this photo isn't quite sharp, but it's such a great pose! It was dark and gloomy and breezy today as a dry storm front passed through. Oh well, this is what I got. Try leaning back a few feet. It looks best in the thumbnail.
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Coast Live Oak; Beetle; Weevil
ScientificName: Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Coleoptera; Curculionidae

File.Name: d624f_d624-006.jpg
Notes: 9131296624 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera trying to warm up in the garden on a gloomy morning. It looks so ferocious! (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2013)

File.Name: d624g_d624-010.jpg
Notes: 9129027023 These are aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Compare this photo of the normal flowers. Note the umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf at the base of the flowers, and compare my other photo. I'm sure these abnormal flowers are caused by aphids (family Aphididae of the order Homoptera), though I can't see them in this photo. The aphids completely change the flowers - the galled flowers look different and stay on the plant much longer than normal. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2013)

File.Name: d624h_d624-005.jpg
Notes: 9128987855 Here are a pair of pea-sized galls of (I think) the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) looks similar, but maybe it's restricted to Blue Oaks (Quercus douglasii)? These galls are growing on twigs of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. I guess the one on the left is from this year, and the other one is from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2013)
CommonNames: Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp; Round Gall Wasp; Blue Oaks; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Besbicus conspicuus; Quercus douglasii; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d625a_d625-034.jpg
Notes:
9140846726 This is another wonderful California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, formerly known as Hemaris diffinis) flying into new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2013)

Today was a beautiful day. It was a bit breezy, but not too bad. It's expected to become quite hot over the next few days. I'll be sweating, but it should be good for butterflies.
CommonNames: California Clearwing Moth; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Hemaris diffinis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Hemaris diffinis; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d625b_d625-008.jpg
Notes:
9138535327 This is a handsome Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly with the sun behind its paper-thin wings. It's working new flowers of the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden this morning. This one is missing its tails. Life is hard for these butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2013)

Swallowtail butterflies have been scarce in this drought year. This was a quick "point and shoot" photo. It's interesting how these are often the keepers!

File.Name: d625c_d625-027.jpg
Notes: 9138457949 This is a brilliant new flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. There's a little Beetle (Coleoptera) in the upper-left looking into the flower. It was quite windy where I got this photo today. I'll do better as new flowers appear. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2013)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Beetle
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae; Coleoptera

File.Name: d625d_d625-042.jpg
Notes: 9138310335 This is looking into a brilliant new flower of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. All of our native thistles seem to be Cirsium occidentale, but there are plants in different varieties with white, violet, and red flowers. (See this photo.) The natives are wonderful, the invasive thistle are horrible. If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - make sure you know the difference! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2013)
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Cirsium occidentale

File.Name: d625e_d625-045.jpg
Notes:
9140390964 These are maybe the last flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family in our mountains this year. These plants have been heavily browsed by deer, but there are still a few of the brilliant summer flowers. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2013)

The deer have been terrible pests in our garden this year - nibbling on phacelia, columbine, roses, all our nectarines and apricots, and lots more. I guess they are hungry in this drought year. I understand, but summer has just started. What will they be eating in three months?

File.Name: d625f_d625-028.jpg
Notes: 9138086497 This is a small but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the old order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica), on a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's clutching a small Beetle (Coleoptera) in its crab-like claws and giving it the "icepick in the neck" treatment with its proboscis. These small bugs are effective predators. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2013)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Beetle
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Coleoptera

File.Name: d625g_d625-035.jpg
Notes: 9137983389 This is looking down on a single flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. If you look close, you can spot a couple of Ambush Bugs (family Phymatidae) in the old order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). (Look at 12:00 and 6:00.) These are predators that can bring down insects much larger than they are. See my [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2013)
CommonNames: Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Ambush Bugs; Pacific Ambush Bug
ScientificName: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica
associatedTaxa: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica

File.Name: d625h_d621-025.jpg
Notes:
9137923751 These are new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I reckon they are my favorite summer flower in our summer-dry mountains. The flower heads look like little pom-poms! Many insects depend on these flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 June 2013)

We have these plants in the canyon at home, but they probably won't flower for another month. It's interesting that in the spring, flowers start low and work higher in the mountains. But in the summer, flowers start high and work lower.

File.Name: d626a_d626-051.jpg
Notes:
9148743828 This is a remarkable Fly (order Diptera) on a new summer flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I guess the fly is genus Thevenetimyia in the Bee Fly (Bombyliidae) group - maybe Thevenetimyia californica? It's quite large for a fly, and it has a very long proboscis - just what it needs for these deep flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)

Today was very nice, but a bit breezy. It was in the high 80s today. The real heat is expected this weekend - into the 100s!
ScientificName: Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia; Bombyliidae; Thevenetimyia californica
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Thevenetimyia; Bombyliidae; Thevenetimyia californica

File.Name: d626b_d626-006.jpg
Notes: 9148680034 This is an interesting Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) - maybe subfamily Asilinae. It's perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the dry edge of our garden. It stayed in one place for some time. Maybe it's trying to oviposit through its extended abdomen? (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)
ScientificName: Asilidae; Diptera; Asilinae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Asilidae; Diptera; Asilinae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d626c_d626-023.jpg
Notes: 9146361427 Here is another new flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. This one is closer to home. The amazing flowers are quite variable. This one is quite pale. You can see the bulbous glands at the base of the petals in front. They attract the little Rove Beetles in the family Staphylinidae of the order Coleoptera, and many more insects. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Rove Beetles
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae; Staphylinidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: d626d_d626-028.jpg
Notes: 9148549724 This is a new flower bud of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. The stamens are itching to get out, like little birds ready to leave the nest. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: d626e_d626-031.jpg
Notes: 9148451518 This is a beautiful Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly perched on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. If you look under the leaf below the butterfly's head, you can see an interesting wild ant (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera). This butterfly stayed still for some time. I first thought it might be ovipositing, but I think they prefer currants or gooseberries (Ribes, Grossulariaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Scrub Oak; ant
ScientificName: Lycaena arota; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Ribes; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena arota; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Ribes; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: d626f_d626-020.jpg
Notes:
9146032873 This is a beautiful Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. It's perched for the moment on a spent flower stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family, but it's really interested in the weedy non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family growing down the hillside. This swallowtail is diferent because the black on it forewing runs along the wing rather than across it. I know it's not the best photo, but this is my record shot. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)

It's interesting that this native butterfly prefers nom-native fennel for it's host plant. See this photo of the caterpillars. Before fennel was introduced, I guess they used Lomatium, Sanicula, and Tauschia in the same Apiaceae plant family. I wish there were more of them to control the fennel!
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Lomatium; Sanicula; Tauschia; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Lomatium; Sanicula; Tauschia; Apiaceae

File.Name: d626g_d626-013.jpg
Notes:
9148132218 This is a beautiful female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working new flowers of well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii) in the garden. This is the first one I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)

The females are pretty enough but the males take the prize as the "California State Butterfly". See this photo.

File.Name: d626h_d626-046.jpg
Notes:
9148061928 This is another wonderful California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, formerly known as Hemaris diffinis) working a new summer flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I've had good luck photographing these moths this year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)

Our native thistles are confusing, since we have red, white, and violet varieties that all seem to be the same species. See my "triptych photo" at http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/8991707068/.

If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - make sure you know the difference between the natives and the weeds! If in doubt, leave it.
ScientificName: Hemaris thetis; Hemaris diffinis; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Hemaris diffinis; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d627a_d627-034.jpg
Notes:
9154871051 Wildfire does expose some secrets...

These are scorched deer bones under what used to be a canopy of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Did a deer crawl under here to die? Or was it the lair of a couger? I guess we'll never know. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2013)

I hiked out the the "River Trail" that starts at the Sage Hill campground toay. It was burned by the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area a month ago. What a transformation - everything is black and white instead of green like its always been.

There was an early report that the fire was started by someone emptying burning coals into dry grass, but apparently the story was false. The wildfire was started accidentally by sparks from an approved BBQ grill. I think it's amazing that the firefighters managed to control this fire with the winds. Thank you!

It was HOT today at the river, in the 90's at least. It's expected to get hotter tomorrow, and hotter again on Saturday.

File.Name: d627b_d627-047.jpg
Notes:
9154739593 Despite wildfire, life goes on!

These are a few stump-sprouters that are coming back after the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area a month ago. On the left is Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. In the middle is California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. And on the right is Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This is what we should see after a wildfire, but the combination of wildfire and drought must be difficult. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2013)

It's interesting that not every chaparral shrub is a stump-sprouter. For example, Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) is not.
CommonNames: Bicolored Yerba Santa; California Brickellbush; Coast Live Oak; Bigberry Manzanita
ScientificName: Eriodictyon crassifolium; nigrescens; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Brickellia californica; Asteraceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Eriodictyon crassifolium; nigrescens; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Brickellia californica; Asteraceae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: d627c_d627-010.jpg
Notes:
9154621709 This is the burned-out overflow channel of the Santa Ynez River. I've gotten hundreds of photos here over the years - but not this year. This was burned by the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area a month ago. The wildfire burned as far as the ridge in the background before the firefighters somehow stopped it, but it's interesting how it stopped along the river.

The main "River Trail" goes from left to right. That track that heads off to the left might well be "Treebeard's Trail" - I've hiked it enough times to leave a track! I know, it will all come back, but it brings a tear to my eye to see it like this. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2013)

File.Name: d627d_d627-008.jpg
Notes: 9156778316 This is another family burned-out native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. They were burned by the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area a month ago. They look like pineapples. These plants are mostly showing some green. They might make it, though the combination of wildfire and very dry drought soil might do them in. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2013)
ScientificName: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: d627e_d627-045.jpg
Notes: 9156734968 Here is a family of burned-out native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. They were burned by the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area a month ago. At least the largest one will probably make it - to flower and die, as they do. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2013)

File.Name: d627f_d627-059.jpg
Notes: 9156663190 Here is a large burned-out Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It was burned by the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area a month ago. There is still some green on top, and I hope it makes it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2013)

File.Name: d627g_d627-019.jpg
Notes: 9154376167 This is a burned-out shrub framed against a fire-blackened outcrop of Monterey Shale. I like how the shrub transitions from dark-on-light below to light on dark above. It was burned by the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area a month ago. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2013)

File.Name: d627h_d627-030.jpg
Notes:
9154289843 This is a burned-out Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family on the "River Trail" that starts at the Sage Hill campground. It was burned by the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area a month ago. I hiked along the trail today to inspect the damage. It was near total, but next year's fire-follower wildflowers should be wonderful! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2013)

I've always photographed Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata, Lamiaceae) just to the right of this, but not this year.
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Monardella lanceolata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Monardella lanceolata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d628a_d628-020.jpg
Notes:
9161490415 This is a two-fer of Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies on a flower of native Humboldt Lily Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family. You can see how the pistil of the flower is touching the right-most butterfly's wing, where the pollen is. You can also see a bit of pollen on the left-most butterfly's wing. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2013)

The butterfly on the right almost looks golden like a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly, but I'm sure that's just the "light tent" effect of the sun shining through the flower petals.

It was downright hot today - 97 ∞F according to a neighbor's weather station. It's expected to be even hotter tomorrow, into triple digits. On the other hand, the breeze was moderate - I'll take heat over wind any day!
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Humboldt Lily; Western Tiger Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio rutulus
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio rutulus

File.Name: d628b_d628-033.jpg
Notes: 9161383703 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on a flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family. The butterfly is getting nectar from the flower, but it's also doing it a service - collecting pollen on it's wings and carrying it to another flower. It's a perfect match between flower and butterfly. I wish all exchanges were so equitable! I don't think any other critter can pollinate these flowers - except for the more golden Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly that I still haven't photographed this year. (I've seen a few.) No swallowtails means no tiger lilies. It's that simple. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2013)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Humboldt Lily; Western Tiger Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio rutulus
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio rutulus

File.Name: d628c_d628-025.jpg
Notes: 9163582984 Here are more fantastic new flowers of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family - with Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies! This is our native Tiger Lily. The plants grow in the canyons. They look too beautiful to be native, but they are. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2013)
ScientificName: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio eurymedon
associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae; Papilio eurymedon

File.Name: d628d_d628-039.jpg
Notes: 9163537220 Here are more fantastic new flowers of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family - our native Tiger Lily. The plants grow in the canyons. They look too beautiful to be native, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2013)

File.Name: d628e_d628-051.jpg
Notes: 9161288259 This is a closer shot of the new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see that it has heads of two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. Very pretty! I did see a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on the flowers today, but no photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2013)

File.Name: d628f_d628-049.jpg
Notes: 9163447078 A while back, I said that the native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family wouldn't flower this year. Well, I was wrong - about this cluster of plants anyway. It's an unusual composite with an interesting common name. It has heads of two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2013)

File.Name: d628g_d628-054.jpg
Notes: 9163414492 Here are fully-open new flowers of native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family. They have large basal ovate leaves that wither away before the flowers appear. The plants really look like little asparagus shoots, 1 or 2 feet tall. It's a real orchid, though not the kind you would use for a corsage. Note the long nectar spurs below the flowers. Interesting as the are, I don't think I've ever seen insects visiting these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2013)

File.Name: d628h_d628-040.jpg
Notes: 9161114263 These are flowering stems of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family. They stand about 8 feet tall. It is said that the Chumash people used the hollow stems for arrow shafts, tobacco pipes, house thatching, paintbrush handles, and more. It does well along mountain roads, but also in the deep woods. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2013)

File.Name: d629a_d629-001.jpg
Notes:
9169770091 This is a remarkable Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera. It's "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden today. It's quite large, nearly an inch long. They have wonderful patterned eyes. There is a Black Soldier Fly Blog! (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2013)

Lordy, it was hot today. I noticed 104∞F on the upstairs porch, and a neighbor with an automated weather station logged 107∞F. The weather station at Los Prietos aka Paradise (where I was two days ago) reported 110∞F. That's too darn hot for insects and photographers! And tomorrow is forecast to be a degree or two hotter...

File.Name: d629b_d629-007.jpg
Notes:
9171867450 I'm pretty sure this is a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - I guess Eulonchus smaragdinus. It's working a new flower of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. These flies seem made for these flowers - just the right size to get in, and a long enough proboscis to reach the sweet stuff inside. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2013)

The flower is in sharp focus, which shows how fast this fly moves. It has a very long proboscis, and favors these Monkey Flowers. They are fast and seem to pick flowers at random. All you can do is pre-focus on a flower, and try to get a photo when the fly backs out. Note to self - use 800 ISO next time!
CommonNames: Metallic Small-Headed Fly; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Eulonchus; Acroceridae; Diptera; Eulonchus smaragdinus; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: d629c_d629-005.jpg
Notes: 9169467745 This is an American Lady (Vanessa virginensis) butterfly, with wings open (right) and wings closed (left). She has four wing spots above, but only two below. This one is "puddling" on damp soil in the garden. I've been seeing them for a while, but this is my first photo of the year. These butterflies oviposit on native Everlasting (Gnaphalium) plants. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2013)

File.Name: d629d_d629-013.jpg
Notes:
9171585040 This is a pretty new violet flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family - maybe the last one of the year. Note the native ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)

I'm not sure what this is. It may just be a variant of the "White Thistle", but it really does look different - for example the involucral bracts are less hairy. There is a key to Cirsium occidentale which places the white and violet thistles together. They do look different to me, but the botanists are in charge.

All of our native thistles are Cirsium occidentale, but there are plants in different varieties with white, violet, and red flowers. (See this photo.) The natives are wonderful, the invasive thistle are horrible. If you do any kind of restoration work - pulling weeds - make sure you know the difference!

Thistle-Garden - I have collected some seed for you. The white and red varieties, but you'll have to wait a bit for these violet ones to mature. Please email me about what I should do with them.
Locality: See this photo.) The natives are wonderful
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; Asteraceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale

File.Name: d629e_d626-042.jpg
Notes:
9169254753 Here is a closer photo of the new web-spinning caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies, munching a new leaf of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. See my [Previous] photo. They look like like tent caterpillars, but they are really next years butterflies, so treat them well! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)

They caterpillars may kill a branch or two of the plants, but they do not do too much damage. They spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year!
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Plantain Family
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

Notes: 9171375172 These are new web-spinning caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies, spinning a shroud over a should-be flowering stem of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. See my [Next] photo. They look like like tent caterpillars, but they are really next years butterflies, so treat them well! (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2013)

File.Name: d629g_d625-019.jpg
Notes: 9169079337 These are new flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. This is what they look like without the caterpillars - see my [Next] two photos. The flower stems mostly climb and hang from another shrub, and the flowers point backwards from the tips. Very elegant. These are classic "hummingbird flowers" - though I didn't see any hummers visiting on this day. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2013)

File.Name: d629g_d625-047.jpg
Notes: 9171244414 These are new flowers of the Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. It has "ternate" leaves in whorls of three. They are growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. It really does have bluish stems - in a "blue dun" trout fly sort of way. These are classic "hummingbird flowers" - though I didn't see any hummers visiting on this day. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 June 2013)

File.Name: d630a_d630-025.jpg
Notes:
9178007297 Here is another new flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2013)

These flowers are individuals, just like you and me. They are all built on the same form, but they are recognizable as individuals with their shape and different amounts of pigment. Maybe all flowers are like this, and we just don't notice.

It was another very hot day - really too hot for insects and photographers, but these flowers seemed to be digging it. There was a haze that I thought cooled it off a few degrees, but a neighbor's automatic weather station still recorded a high of 106∞F on the pass, and it got to 111∞F at the river, with 2% "Fuel Moisture". I'm not sure what that means or how it's measured, but it sounds terrible. Tomorrow is expected to be a little bit cooler. I'm ready for that!
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. These fantastic flowers have bulbous nectar glands at the base of each petal. Some critter has eaten into the one on the right. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2013)

I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. See my [Next] few photos to appreciate how wonderful the flowers are. Of course they are also wonderful because they are flowering now, in a drought year, on a 100∞F day! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 June 2013)

File.Name: d630f_d630-008.jpg
Notes: 9179890530 This is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working old-fashioned flowers of a Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden. The holy grail of local butterfly photographers must surely be to capture the iridescent "dog face" on the upper wings of this butterfly. Well, this photo shows the lower wings. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2013)

File.Name: d630g_d630-012.jpg
Notes: 9179795662 This is a little white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting... waiting... on the tip of a leaf of Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2013)

File.Name: d630h_d630-002.jpg
Notes: 9177522665 This is a flower head of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family. It's in full flower, with stamens dangling. They stand 5-10 feet tall, and were useful for the Chumash people. See my comments on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2013)

File.Name: d701a_d701-001.jpg
Notes:
9187391115 I believe this is a Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia) in the tribe Bembicini of the Apoidea of the Hymenoptera on a flower of (maybe) non-native English Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) in the Lamiaceae plant family in our garden this morning. They have remarkable goggle-eyes and a long proboscis that's hidden in the flower. These wasps are hard to photograph as they move so fast between the flowers. They seem intelligent, and they don't like being photographed! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2013)

I'm curious - what is that little pointed dingus-like "dingus" on the upper part of it's abdomen?

Today was MUCH cooler than yesterday - warmer in the morning, but it ended up in the 70s rather than the 100s! There was a cloud layer suggesting monsoon tropical weather. I'm happy with the coolness, but it was also pretty breezy all day.

I have to go the L.A. tomorrow. I don't know if I'll be back in time to post photos, or whether I'll have any photos to post.
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Lavandula angustifolia; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d701b_d701-019.jpg
Notes:
9187096001 Here are two consecutive photos of a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - I guess Eulonchus smaragdinus. It's working a new flower of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. I got these two photos in a burst from my camera in CH mode, at 5 fps. These flies move that fast as they come out of the flowers! They seem made for these flowers - just the right size to get in, and a long enough proboscis to reach the sweet stuff inside. I did use ISO 800 for this photo - these flies are fast! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2013)

That white thing hanging in the throat of the flower like an epiglottis is the stigma of the flower. This one looks closed , which means the flower has been visited before. When the flowers haven't been visited yet, the flap is open. See this photo for both positions. It's great fun when hiking with kids to tickle the open stigma with a grass blade, and watch it close. It happens in seconds! (See this PDF.)
CommonNames: Metallic Small-Headed Fly; Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: d701c_d701-024.jpg
Notes:
9189824468 I call this a "Steel Bridge Fly". It's a fairly large Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera - that I always seem to find at this time of year perched on the steel guard rails of the little bridge across our creek. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2013)

Hah - it looks like this fly took a dump on the bridge, but I think that's a bit of bird poop that just happened to be in the right place.

File.Name: d701d_d628-001.jpg
Notes: 9189767612 I've been seeing lots of these small Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera on the native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) plants in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess they are in mating mode - looking for mates and chasing anything that moves, including my camera! They are hard to photograph, quite camera shy. This is a tight crop of a photo from a few days ago. I've been hoping to get a better shot, but maybe not. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2013)

File.Name: d701e_d701-011.jpg
Notes: 9189529310 This is a little bee-like and bee-size Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera. It dragging its butt over the gravel on the dry edge of our garden. I guess it has worms, or more likely it's oviposting. I wish this photo was sharper, but this is the best I got. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2013)

File.Name: d701f_d627-005.jpg
Notes:
9186566911 This is the small nymph of a Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) starting to munch a leaf of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. I'm pretty sure this is Oedaleonotus phryneicus, one of the the Spur-throated Grasshoppers ( Melanoplinae). (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2013)

I have to give my thanks (again!) to Aliceinwl1 who helped me understand local grasshoppers last year. Thanks Alice, I hope I remembered right!
Locality: again!) to Aliceinwl1 who helped me understand local grasshoppers last year. Thanks Alice

File.Name: d701g_d701-029.jpg
Notes: 9186536009 This is looking into a large dandelion-like seed head of native Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2013)

File.Name: d701h_d701-008.jpg
Notes: 9186474905 This photo shows the peeling bark - and green skin underneath - of a beautiful Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. I got this photo at home in the woods. The trees are hanging on (so far) through drought and hot weather. It's cooling just to see it. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2013)

File.Name: d702a_d701-013.jpg
Notes: 9202431627 I believe this is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on new growth of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family, on the dry edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2013)

File.Name: d702b_d630-004.jpg
Notes: 9202386817 This is a beautiful fungus-riddled leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the dry edge of our garden. The sun is behind. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2013)

File.Name: d703a_d703-005.jpg
Notes:
9202810817 This is just a fly... I'm not sure what kind of Fly (order Diptera) this is, but it's bristly enough to suggest family Tachinidae. (Maybe it's just another Muscidae?) It's resting for a moment on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I love photographing flies even when I don't know what they are - that's most of the time! They have so much structural detail. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2013)

I've been busy taking care of business in L.A. - with more stuff to do in Santa Barbara today. It should all be done by July 15, but I'll probably miss another day or two before then. Believe me, I'd rather be on the mountain taking photos!

Has anyone figure out a way to get rid of that stupid Yahoo toolbar that appeared to my browser?

File.Name: d703b_d703-014.jpg
Notes: 9205542726 I believe from the shape and colors that this is a small Carrot Wasp in the family Gasteruptiidae in the order Hymenoptera - almost certainly genus Gasteruption, maybe Gasteruption visaliae. It's working new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I have no idea what the common name of this wasp means. It looks a lot like an Ichneumon Wasp (Ichneumonidae), but it has a distinct neck - which you can't see in this photo. It was breezy today, and I was lucky to get any photo of this interesting wasp! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2013)

Notes: 9205469280 This is a small but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the old order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica), on new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's just waiting for prey to come by. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2013)

File.Name: d703d_d703-011.jpg
Notes: 9202611915 Here are new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's one of our most prolific summer flowers, and the red stamens are quite pretty up close - look on the left edge. I'll have a better photo tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2013)

File.Name: d703e_d620-013.jpg
Notes:
9202537221 This is a very odd tiny Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I've been holding on to these photos for a couple of weeks hoping to see it again and get a better photo, but I might never see it again!

The left photo is from the side, and the right photo is a "butt shot" showing it's odd hollow abdomen. This critter has green flecks, like it's growing algae on its back. My usual photo size is 800 pixels high. I had to increase the size of the original photos a bit. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2013)

File.Name: d703f_d703-025.jpg
Notes: 9205261446 These are new summer flowers and buds of native Yellow-flowered Hawkweed (Hiericium argutum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing on a rock ledge in the mountains. It's just starting to flower in our summer-dry mountains. It favors cool rocky places. It's a hard one to photograph because the flowers are spread out on long stalks that sway in the slightest breeze. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2013)

File.Name: d704a_d704-046.jpg
Notes:
9210549309 Who needs fireworks when we have such brilliant flowers in the mountains?

This is a brilliant RED flower of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It looks like a firework. What can I say? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2013)

Today's photos are red, white, and blue wildflowers for Independence Day - three times! I got most of the photos today on a drive up East Camino Cielo towards La Cumbre Peak. It was a fun project. I'm not big on flags, but wildflowers are just fine!

File.Name: d704b_d701-014.jpg
Notes:
9213262102 This is looking into a large WHITE dandelion-like seed head of native Large-flowered Mountain-dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. the pappus bristles look like starburst fireworks! (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2013)

I have another photo of this seedhead taken later in the day and focused on the seeds.

File.Name: d704c_d704-057.jpg
Notes:
9210381875 These are the last of the BLUE flowers of native Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. They look like they are singing joyful tunes! (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2013)

If you look close in the throat of the flower on the right, you can see its stigma hanging down from the top. That little white tongue-like structure like a Rolling Stones tongue is a non-fertile "staminode". It's the "fifth stamen" - which gives this "pen-stamen" family it's name.

File.Name: d704d_d703-006.jpg
Notes: 9213074310 This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly trying to position itself on a RED flower of an old-fashioned Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden. I'm pretty sure we got this geranium as a cutting from the old "Casa Vieja" adobe at Christy Ranch on Santa Cruz Island. It's a real old-fashioned geranium! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2013)

File.Name: d704e_d703-017.jpg
Notes: 9210271235 This is looking close at pretty WHITE summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's one of our most prolific summer flowers, and the red stamens are like little flags up close. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2013)

File.Name: d704f_d704-031.jpg
Notes: 9210237793 These are fantastic BLUE summer flowers of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. If you look close, you the see a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on the flower. It's getting close to the end for these wonderful flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2013)
ScientificName: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: d704g_d704-052.jpg
Notes: 9210143039 These are fantastic RED flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family in our mountains. These plants have been heavily browsed by deer, but they are still producing a few of the brilliant summer flowers. Maybe they're still flowering because they've been browsed by deer? See this photo (June 25) of what I also thought were the last flowers of the year. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2013)

File.Name: d704h_d704-024.jpg
Notes:
9212880348 These are new sumer flowers of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family, shot against a sandstone outcrop. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. The flowers on the left are mostly WHITE, but the flowers on the right are more magenta. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2013)

These flowers are individuals, just like you and me. They are all built on the same form, but they are recognizable as individuals with their shape and different amounts of pigment. Maybe all flowers are like this, and we just don't notice.
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: d704i_d704-028.jpg
Notes:
9210047881 Here are new BLUE summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Well, they are mostly blue, there's some magenta. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 July 2013)

Today's photos are red, white, and blue wildflowers for Independence Day - three times! I got most of the photos today on a drive up East Camino Cielo towards La Cumbre Peak. A fun project.

File.Name: d705a_d705-018.jpg
Notes:
9220077324 I believe this is a Large Milkweed Bug (Oncopeltus fasciatus or kin) in the family Lygaeidae of the old order Hemiptera. It is working new flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. These summer flowers are remarkably complex. (San Lucas Ranch, Santa Ynez Valley, 5 July 2013)

I had an errand to do in the Santa Ynez Valley this morning, so I wanted to make the best of it with a photo. But it was frustrating trying to photograph this insect today - it was quite windy and the cars zooming by on Highway 154 only made it worse.

Just five photos today. Julie and I MIGHT have a date to attend an outdoor screening of Alfred Hitchcock's film Vertigo tonight at the SB courthouse. Then again we might just stay home. Either way, I'll be ready.
ScientificName: Oncopeltus fasciatus; Lygaeidae; Hemiptera; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Vertigo
associatedTaxa: Oncopeltus fasciatus; Lygaeidae; Hemiptera; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Vertigo

File.Name: d705b_d705-012.jpg
Notes: 9220013830 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) on a perfect ball of intricate new flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. The plants are native, but kind of weedy in the Santa Ynez Valley. The summer flowers are remarkably complex, but I'll have to wait for a day that isn't so windy to illustrate just how complex they are. (San Lucas Ranch, Santa Ynez Valley, 5 July 2013)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae

File.Name: d705c_d705-002.jpg
Notes: 9217214967 I'm not sure, but I guess this is a small Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera. It's perched (for a moment) on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2013)

File.Name: d705d_d704-003.jpg
Notes: 9219917142 This is the tiny nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera). The adults appear in late-summer and fill the nights with song. This one is resting on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2013)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d705e_d704-019.jpg
Notes: 9219884930 Here are the elegant curves of new seeds of the same red-flowered old-fashioned Geranium (really a Pelargonium, family Geraniaceae) in the garden that I showed yesterday. Very graceful. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2013)

File.Name: d706a_d706-022.jpg
Notes:
9227879900 This is a remarkable Robber Fly (family Asilidae, order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I guess it's one of the Bee-like Robber Flies in the genus Laphria. These flies are predators that catch their prey on the wing, like dragonflies. Whenever I can make out the ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up the compound eyes of any fly, I'm happy. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2013)

I didn't plan for today's photos to be all interesting flies, but it happened.

We never made it to the outdoor showing of Vertigo last night. Partly it was inertia, but also because TCM was showing the whole cycle of "Antoine Doinel" films by FranÁois Truffaut, starting with "The 400 Blows" and ending (after midnight) with "Love On The Run". I didn't quite make it until the end.
CommonNames: Robber Fly; Bitter Gooseberry; Bee-like Robber Flies
ScientificName: Asilidae; Diptera; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; Laphria; ommatidia; Vertigo
associatedTaxa: Asilidae; Diptera; Ribes amarum; Grossulariaceae; Laphria; ommatidia; Vertigo

File.Name: d706b_d706-002.jpg
Notes:
9227791206 I first thought this different little fly (order Diptera) was a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae, but Tjdegraaf informs me that it's genus Metopia, subfamily Miltogramminae, in the family Sarcophagidae. Live and learn. Thanks for the update Tjdegraaf!

It is still quite bristly. This one is perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. This fly was hard to photograph - it kept jumping around from leaf to leaf, but finally I got it. It's most recognizable features are its small size and white face. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2013)

I got many of today's photos at this one place along our driveway that catches early morning light. All the photos taken on wild blackberry were taken here,

File.Name: d706c_d705-003.jpg
Notes: 9227737642 I believe this is a bristly Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera - perched for just a moment on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) that's caught on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2013)

File.Name: d706d_d706-007.jpg
Notes:
9227620350 I guess this is a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae of the order Diptera with those bright red eyes - but I'm not sure. This one is perched on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. It doesn't have the bristles of the tachinid fly in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2013)

I'm never sure with flies. I'm bewildered by the diversity of flies, but I love to photograph them. They have so much structural detail!
ScientificName: Sarcophagidae; Diptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae
associatedTaxa: Sarcophagidae; Diptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: d706e_d705-009.jpg
Notes: 9224803029 This is a different little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera on the native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) plants in the Rosaceae plant family. It perched for a moment, and then it was gone. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2013)

File.Name: d706f_d706-004.jpg
Notes: 9224744283 This is a small Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera on the native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) plants in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess they are in mating mode - looking for mates and chasing anything that moves, including my camera! They are hard to photograph, quite camera shy. The flies look black from a distance, but they are quite brown up close like this. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2013)

File.Name: d706g_d706-043.jpg
Notes: 9227483822 I first thought this was a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. But Alice sounds pretty sure it's a Stiletto Fly (Therevidae), and I can see now that the antennae are wrong for a syrphid. So many flies, so little time! The fog came over the mountain pass above us about when I got this photo, and this handsome little fly was grounded by cool weather. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2013)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak; Stiletto Fly
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Therevidae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Therevidae

File.Name: d706h_d706-026.jpg
Notes:
9227372056 I have no idea what kind of Fly (order Diptera) this is. It's quite tiny. I found it perched on the steel railing over the bridge in our canyon. I believe it's waiting to waiting to hook up with a mate - I did see a pair of them interested in each other. If you have an idea, please let me know. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2013)

So many flies, so little time! This one does have a Robber Fly (family Asilidae) look with those bristly legs and bulbous "see everything" eyes...

File.Name: d707a_d706-049.jpg
Notes:
9233892585 This is a rather large (an inch or more) Longhorned Beetle (Cerambycidae) in the order Coleoptera. I found this one in the bathroom, and asked Julie to be my wrangler to release it on the porch. It immediately started moving and climbed up her hand! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2013)

After browsing at BugGuide, I guess this is a Yellow Douglas Fir Borer (Centrodera spurca) - which is curious, because we don't have any kind of Doug Fir growing within 10 miles. Maybe it likes the Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family growing up the hill? I've seen these before, on our lamps at night. It's not uncommon.

Today was a great day for getting photos - warm and calm winds. But insects are getting scarce in the woods. It's no surprise. It's our second drought year in a row, so there are fewer flowers this summer. That means there are fewer pollinator insects. And that means there are fewer predators of those insects. Ripples in the food web... If we don't get rain next year, it really will have an impact that people won't like.
Locality: Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family growing up the hill? I've seen these before
CommonNames: Longhorned Beetle; Yellow Douglas Fir Borer; Coulter Pines
ScientificName: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Centrodera spurca; Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae
associatedTaxa: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Centrodera spurca; Pinus coulteri; Pinaceae

File.Name: d707b_d707-018.jpg
Notes: 9236513896 My first guess is that this is another Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly. My second guess is that it's a Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) - it does have some "white frosting" and white wing borders. Either way, it's perched on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The butterflies are disappearing. I guess I should try to document the last butterflies I see as well as the first. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)
CommonNames: Hedgerow Hairstreak; Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Satyrium saepium; Satyrium tetra; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Satyrium saepium; Satyrium tetra; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d707c_d707-039.jpg
Notes: 9233613299 I guess this is another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae of the order Diptera with those red eyes and that red abdomen. They are [nasty/wonderful] (select one)! Personally, I like them. They are large and remarkable sculpted. This one is perched on a leaf of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family that is already turning red to drop its leaves in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)

File.Name: d707d_d707-005.jpg
Notes: 9233531639 This is a Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera. It's "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden today. It's quite large, nearly an inch long. They have wonderful patterned eyes, unfortunately not very visible here. There is a Black Soldier Fly Blog! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)

File.Name: d707e_d707-053.jpg
Notes: 9233489319 Here are more new galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can still make out the individual capsules of the galls, but they are starting to grow together. In time, it will look like one mass of red hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d707f_d707-049.jpg
Notes: 9233385275 This is a fantastic cluster of new galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This photo shows that the galls are made of individual capsules containing just one gall wasp larvae each. As they mature, the hairs of the capsules become intertwined to make a hairy mass, and the individual capsules become lost. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d707g_d707-052.jpg
Notes: 9236123642 This is a handsome new gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are monothalamus (just one wasp larvae inside) and easily detachable (you can break them off). They look like little mushrooms growing from the oak leaf, but they are really the "nests" of tiny wasps. These are the first ones I've noticed this year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; monothalamus; detachable
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; monothalamus; detachable

File.Name: d707h_d706-024.jpg
Notes:
9233214167 Here is a new shroud of web-spinning caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies on a flowering branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. If you look close, you can see a few of the caterpillars on the web. They look like tent caterpillars, but they are really next years butterflies, so treat them well! (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2013)

They caterpillars may kill a branch or two of the plants, but they do not do too much damage. They spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year!
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Plantain Family
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: d708a_d708-019.jpg
Notes:
9245357478 This is a remarkable Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia) in the tribe Bembicini of the Apoidea of the Hymenoptera on a flower of (maybe) non-native English Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) in the Lamiaceae plant family in our garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2013)

They have remarkable goggle-eyes and a long proboscis that's hidden in the flower. See my [Previous] photo to appreciate just how long its proboscis is - long enough to get to the nectar of this flower! These wasps are hard to photograph as they move so fast between the flowers. They seem intelligent, and they don't like being photographed!

Today was another beautiful in paradise. I did go to the river today - where it was windy. But all my photos today are from the garden at home.

associatedTaxa: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Lavandula angustifolia; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d708b_d708-014.jpg
Notes: 9242430681 Here are two photos of a remarkable Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia) in the tribe Bembicini of the Apoidea of the Hymenoptera on a flower of (maybe) non-native English Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) in the Lamiaceae plant family in our garden this morning. You can see it's long proboscis in the upper photo, partly inserted into a flower in the lower photo. See my [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2013)

File.Name: d708c_d708-024.jpg
Notes:
9245082154 *Sigh* The things we do for a photo...

This is a perfect Deer Fly (family Tabanidae in the order Diptera) biting the upper part of my left hand. It did draw a bit of blood, like a shot from the doctor. The flies are quite beautiful, but they do bite. They have amazing patterned eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2013)

Hah - this fly latched on to my left hand in the garden today. I walked over to the garden table, adjusted my camera with my right hand, and the fly never budged through the whole process. I got the photo!

File.Name: d708d_d708-034.jpg
Notes: 9242250289 This is another Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens or kin) in the family Stratiomyidae of the insect order Diptera. It's "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden today. It's quite large, nearly an inch long. They have wonderful patterned eyes and antennae like an old-fashioned rabbit-ear TV antenna. I've been seeing quite a few of them lately. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2013)

File.Name: d708e_d708-027.jpg
Notes: 9244984704 This is a large black Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into a new flower of (I guess) an Aloe plant in the Aloaceae or Asphodeloideae or Xanthorrhoeaceae plant family, depending on your authority. I also caught it taking a shortcut and committing "nectar theft" on the flowers - maybe getting the very last drop? (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2013)
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Aloaceae; Asphodeloideae; Xanthorrhoeaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Aloaceae; Asphodeloideae; Xanthorrhoeaceae

File.Name: d708f_d708-031.jpg
Notes: 9244901042 I believe this is a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae) in the order Hymenoptera. It has dark wings, curled antennae, a muscular body, and "so blue it looks black" colors. This one is "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2013)

File.Name: d708g_d708-020.jpg
Notes: 9241952301 This is Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of native perennial Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I guess the bee is a Megachilid Bee (Megachilidae) because of the pollen collecting hairs on it's abdomen. I see lots of these tiny wild bees that are 1/4 inch long or less. I don't know how important they are, but I find them everywhere there are flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2013)

ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: d708h_d708-011.jpg
Notes: 9241907869 This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly "casting its line' into a flower of (maybe) non-native English Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) in the Lamiaceae plant family in our garden this morning. The male butterflies get all the glory, but the ladies are quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2013)

File.Name: d709a_d709-033.jpg
Notes:
9253036022 This was a lucky photo - for me, not for the unfortunate fly.

I'm sure this is a remarkable Metallic Small-Headed Fly in the genus Eulonchus of the uncommon family Acroceridae of the order Diptera - probably Eulonchus smaragdinus. I found it just like this, dead on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Maybe it got tangled in the spider web, or maybe it just died because it's favorite native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family are just about done for the year. You can see the interesting "hinge" that brings it's long proboscis from down to up. See my photos here, here, and here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2013)

It was warm today - 103∞F according to a neighbor's weather station on the web, and 108∞F according to the weather station at Paradise down by the river! No wonder I felt wiped out today.

I have to go the L.A. again tomorrow, and I probably won't be back in time to post photos. Believe me, I'd rather be on the mountain taking photos!

ScientificName: Eulonchus; Acroceridae; Diptera; Eulonchus smaragdinus; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
