File.Name: d709b_d709-019.jpg
Notes: 9250126843 This is another remarkable Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia) in the tribe Bembicini of the Apoidea of the Hymenoptera. I got this photo in the garden. According to the tag, this is Mountain Monardella (Monardella odoratissima) in the Lamiaceae plant family, from the SB Botanic Garden. It's native further north and in the sierras, but it's doing well in the garden and the critters dig it. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2013)
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Monardella odoratissima; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Monardella odoratissima; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d709c_d709-005.jpg
Notes:
9252862568 This is a newly emerged California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" in the garden. But age gets us all. Compare my [Previous] photo of a well-aged specimen. It's hard to believe they are the same species, but the are. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2013)

"Old man look at my life,
I'm a lot like you were...

File.Name: d709d_d708-003.jpg
Notes: 9249844893 Age gets us all, including this California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly. Compare my [Next] photo of a newly emerged butterfly - so brilliant and beautiful, compared to this well-aged specimen. It's a survivor, and that counts as wisdom for butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2013)

File.Name: d709e_d709-001.jpg
Notes: 9252594814 Here is another female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a flower of (maybe) non-native English Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) in the Lamiaceae plant family in our garden this morning. She has such wonderful colors on her face. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2013)

File.Name: d709f_d709-024.jpg
Notes: 9249780325 This is a small Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's actually rather large for a leafhopper, greater than 1/4 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2013)

File.Name: d709g_d709-031.jpg
Notes: 9252475138 This is a clutch of tiny insect eggs under a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I guess it takes an eye to find something like this, but it's more a matter of noticing than seeing. Trust me - my glasses are in terrible shape. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2013)

File.Name: d709h_d709-028.jpg
Notes: 9252444954 This is the first new flower - and maybe the last - of native California Wild Rose (Rosa californica) in the Rosaceae plant family. I really didn't think we'd see any flowers on these shrubs in this very dry year, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2013)

File.Name: d710a_d707-028.jpg
Notes:
9259954584 These are new feathery seed plumes of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family. They look brilliant white with the sun behind them, though they look grayish when the sun is in front. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)

I got back from L.A. early enough to post a few photos - but these four are enough. We had some interesting monsoon weather today. We actually had a 20% chance of rain. I don't think it ever happened - I wasn't home - but the look and smells are different. Maybe tomorrow?

File.Name: d710b_d708-055.jpg
Notes: 9259896096 These are the new seeds of a native Foothill Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. The seeds are just half of a double-winged maple "samara" - they flutter more than fly. The plants grow as shrubs or small trees, mostly fairly close to the river - though there is one odd plant colony up in the mountains on San Marcos Pass. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 July 2013)

File.Name: d710c_d707-060.jpg
Notes: 9259799936 This is a new acorn on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. The smooth acorns and foliage are distinct - very different from the Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) that grows nearby. (See my [Previous] photo.) I usually find these plants as shrubs or small trees in the mountains, but there are huge trees scattered in some of the north-facing canyons. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Scrub Oak

File.Name: d710d_d707-041.jpg
Notes: 9256987163 These are new acorns on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They don't always come in 3's like this, but it is a handsome arrangement. I was worried that there wouldn't be an acorn crop is this drought year, but they seem to be doing fine. The Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia trees aren't doing so well. That's important, since so many critters depend on these shrubs and trees. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)

File.Name: d711a_d711-006.jpg
Notes:
9264264953 This is a handsome Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) - probably a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae. It's still immature, but I guess it's just one step away from being an adult. It must be a female with that interesting ovipostitor. This one is perched in foliage of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2013)

I know this photo isn't quite sharp - none of my photos today are. We had summer monsoon weather today, dark and breezy. It really looked like rain, but it never smelled like rain, and finally blew away. I did the best I could for new photos.
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; California Angle-winged Katydid; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; looked; smelled

File.Name: d711b_d711-022.jpg
Notes: 9266864880 This is a handsome little Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera or Raphidioptera) insect posed on a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Snakeflies are now moved to their own Raphidioptera insect order. They aren't real flies (order Diptera) of course. I know, it's not quite sharp - none of my photos today are. It was cloudy-dark and breezy today. I tried. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2013)

File.Name: d711c_d711-016.jpg
Notes: 9263944691 This is a beautiful little humpbacked Fly (order Diptera) on a single late flower of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I guess this is a tiny atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae), but I suppose it could also be a Dance Fly in the family Empididae. I know, it's not quite sharp - none of my photos today are. It was cloudy-dark and breezy today. I tried. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2013)

File.Name: d711d_d711-010.jpg
Notes: 9263902847 This is the tiny nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera - 1/4 inch long or less. It appears to have captured an ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera). It's a spiny little devil! (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2013)

File.Name: d711e_d711-011.jpg
Notes: 9266588412 I noticed this spent chrysalis of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It was dark and breezy today. None of my photos is quite sharp, but I tried. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2013)

File.Name: d711f_d706-041.jpg
Notes:
9266526486 "Who knows what evil lurks in the hearts of men? The Spider knows!"

I know it's The Shadow who knows, but I dig the shadow in this photo, like a film noir movie poster! This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae on a leaf of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella or R. californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's the first one of these spiders I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2013)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Rhamnus tomentella; R. californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Rhamnus tomentella; R. californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: d711g_d707-036.jpg
Notes: 9266458576 I'm pretty sure this is a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae of the order Diptera. They are [nasty/wonderful] (select one)! Personally, I like them. They are large and remarkable sculpted. This one is perched on a leaf of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family that is already turning red to drop its leaves in this drought year. See this photo of the same fly on a different leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)

File.Name: d711h_d707-007.jpg
Notes: 9266401296 These are the first new flowers I've seen of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium - aka Zauschneria californica) - in the Onagraceae plant family in the woods. They keep the hummingbirds going during summer and autumn. Very beautiful, and surprising in our hot, dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2013)
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: d712a_d712-037.jpg
Notes:
9274066596 I believe this is a Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) butterfly perched on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. They have a floppy flight like a moth, but they are quite camera shy. It actually has two eyespots on it's front wing, but this one is shy. I'll bet they are rarely photographed! I feel lucky to get this one. They seemed to be feeling frisky, in a mating mood. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

Today was quite nice, in the 80's, a bit breezy from the ocean side which kept the temperature down. I got most of my photos today on one particular scrub oak shrub - both butterflies, the robber fly, and several galls. I don't know why that one shrub is so busy, but I'll be back.

File.Name: d712b_d712-031.jpg
Notes: 9271220209 This is a female Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly perched on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. She's a bit tattered, but you can still make out her tails. My books say their host plant is gooseberry or currant (Ribes, Grossulariaceae), but they sure were active on this one particular scrub oak. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Lycaena arota; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Ribes; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena arota; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Ribes; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: d712c_d712-040.jpg
Notes:
9271137045 This is a mighty Robber Fly (family Asilidae, order Diptera) sprawled across a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I guess it's in the subfamily Asilinae, but that's as far as I go. It's quite large. They catch their prey in the air, like dragonflies, between those bristly front legs. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

Are those butterfly scales on it's eyes? This was the same shrub where I found quite a few Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterflies. See my [Next] photo.
CommonNames: Robber Fly; Scrub Oak; Tailed Copper; Arota Copper
ScientificName: Asilidae; Diptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Asilinae; Lycaena arota
associatedTaxa: Asilidae; Diptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Asilinae; Lycaena arota

File.Name: d712d_d712-069.jpg
Notes:
9271087471 I believe these are new galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. This is on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls make real honey on their surface, and the vicious-looking ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) are protecting it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

It's a fair trade. The wasps (and oak) make honey for the ants, and the ants keep away predators that would go after the critters inside the galls. Galls are so interesting. See my [Previous] few photos.
Locality: and oak) make honey for the ants
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; ants
ScientificName: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: d712e_d712-026.jpg
Notes:
9270948311 This is the surprisingly beautiful grape-sized gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It is growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It might be our most beautiful gall. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

Remember that these galls are literally part of the oak, induced by "something" in the wasp's ovipositor. I'm surprised the details aren't better known. I love photographing galls because they are so beautiful and strange. They nicely peak between the insects and the mushrooms! If you live in the western US, there is a very good book to guide you - Ron Russo's "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006).
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Field Guide to Plant Galls

File.Name: d712f_d712-065.jpg
Notes: 9273700550 This is a perfect new gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are monothalamus (just one wasp larvae inside) and easily detachable (you can break them off). They look like little mushrooms growing from the oak leaf, but they are really the "nests" of tiny wasps - made by the oaks! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; monothalamus; detachable
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; monothalamus; detachable

File.Name: d712g_d712-033.jpg
Notes: 9273633040 This looks like a magical goblet, but it's the gall of a wasp. It's a solitary Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's an odd one, because these galls usually form in masses. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d712h_d712-036.jpg
Notes: 9273583226 I guess this is the gall of the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andricus gigas) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It is growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The larval chamber is that little bump in the center. Like many of our interesting oak galls, this one is the summer unisexual generation that follows a spring bisexual generation. Galls are complicated, but fascinating. What a neat trick these tiny wasps have - being able to change the normal growth of an oak to make a house! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus gigas; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus gigas; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d713a_d713-016.jpg
Notes:
9278734961 I believe this is an unusual male Mealybug (family Pseudococcidae of the old order Homoptera), though I wouldn't bet the farm on it! I actually recognized the long white tails in the air, and reached out and grabbed it. Here it is posed by the whorl of my left index finger, while I tried to work the camera with my right hand. (I'm not as steady as I used to be.) The long white tails are really just long hairs covered by a waxy exudate. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2013)

Try leaning back a bit - this photo looks better in the thumbnail. It sure looks like a two-winged fly, but it's not. The body is about 1/8 inch long. The females are very different unwinged lumps. I believe these critters are rarely photographed. I'll post another photo tomorrow.

It was a nice day today, but pretty warm. It got up to 95∞F on the upstairs porch.

File.Name: d713b_d712-009.jpg
Notes:
9281387672 This photo needs a story...

I found the little Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera dead on a leaf. I moved it to a different leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family that was easier to photograph. Almost immediately, this red-eyed Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae of the order Diptera came into my photo. It proceeded to roll the unfortunate crane fly off the edge of the leaf so that it fell away. This whole story happened on a dusty leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

I'm just the photographer - I don't have to understand what I see. But as a naturalist, I'd like to!
CommonNames: Crane Fly; Wild Blackberry; Flesh Fly; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Sarcophagidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Sarcophagidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d713c_d713-007.jpg
Notes: 9281325778 This is another tiny nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera. The adults appear later in the summer and fill the nights with song. This one is resting on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2013)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d713d_d712-004.jpg
Notes:
9278487781 This is another handsome Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) - probably a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae. It's still immature, but I guess it's just one step away from being an adult. Is that the beginning of a female ovipostitor at its rear end? (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

Don't you just want to saddle up on that smooth patch behind the head and ride away!

File.Name: d713e_d712-055.jpg
Notes: 9278407095 This is a new summer flower of native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a small plant, less than one foot tall. It is rather pretty, with those violet stamens and pistils. I.m not sure how any new flowers can grow in the bone-dry soil in this drought year - nut I guess it's always bone-dry in the woods in mid-summer! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

File.Name: d713f_d713-019.jpg
Notes: 9281135992 This is another new summer flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. This one is flowering in the canyon at home, as low as I've ever see these flowers at about 2400 feet. Notice the neat little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) at about 2:00 around the flower rim. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2013)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily; Wild Bee
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: d713g_d713-008.jpg
Notes: 9278316455 This is a humble non-native Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) butterfly working the Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria, Lamiaceae) flowers on the edge of the garden. This butterfly does have its charms. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2013)

File.Name: d713h_d712-074.jpg
Notes:
9280981322 Here is a different Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) butterfly perched on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. Does it think it's hiding from me? I can see it's ocular eyes and the wonderful "owl eye" on it's hind wing. That eye on it's wing is winking at me! This butterfly has a remarkable "salt and pepper" pattern on it's wings. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

I just noticed, but I think its face is hiding behind a gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. See this photo from yesterday. Everything is related!
CommonNames: Great Basin Wood-Nymph; Scrub Oak; Spined Turban Gall Wasp

File.Name: d714a_d713-027.jpg
Notes:
9290132426 We had a close encounter with this fledgling California Spotted Owl aka Southern Spotted Owl (Strix occidentalis occidentalis) on the upstairs porch last night! First it landed on the roof, then it moved to the porch. It seemed pretty clumsy, so maybe this was its first hunting flight out from the nest? I like the hunched over pose, like it knows to be wary. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2013)

It was almost dark, but I got my camera on a tripod and set the ISO to 1600 - and got a few photos. Julie could hear its parents in the trees squeaking in high-pitched tones, and I heard the parents bark like seals as they flew off. These owls are quite uncommon, and it was a treat to see one up close like this. We sent the photo around to a few people who know, and got confirmation.

Today was a pretty miserable day - hot and windy and very dry. We got to 95∞ at home, and it got to 101∞ at the river, with 8% relative humidity and 2% fuel moisture. It was too windy for photos today, though I got a few in the morning. These are real "sundowner winds". A sure sign is that the Santa Barbara foothills are warmer than the mountains.
CommonNames: California Spotted Owl; Southern Spotted Owl

Notes:

First it landed on the roof, then it moved to the porch. It seemed pretty clumsy, so maybe this was its first hunting flight out from the nest? It's sitting in a perfect "owl of Athena" pose. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2013)

It was almost dark, but I got my camera on a tripod and set the ISO to 1600 - and got a few photos. Julie could hear its parents in the trees squeaking in high-pitched tones, and I heard the parents bark like seals as they flew off. These owls are quite uncommon, and it was a treat to see one up close like this. We sent the photo around to a few people who know, and got confirmation.

File.Name: d714c_d714-005.jpg
Notes: 9286841395 This is the nymph of a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. It's perched on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. These nymphs are very beautiful when young - see this photo. They lose their colors as they mature into big green adults - see this photo. This one is in between. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2013)

File.Name: d714d_d712-076.jpg
Notes:
9286758139 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae with its spider prey under a flower head of Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I like to think that's a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae) in the order Hymenoptera - the predator becomes prey! But I'm really not sure what kind of wasp it is. It is rather large. (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

I got this photo at the end of my hike when I was tired and thirst, and I had to crouch down low for the photo... but I like it. The stoop was worth it!

Notes: 9286632025 I can't resist one more photo of a fantastic new summer flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. This one is flowering along West Camino Cielo. Notice the interesting mating Wild Bees (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) at the bottom of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2013)

File.Name: d714g_d714-006.jpg
Notes: 9289371594 This is an elegant seed pod of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family - as if the elegant flowers aren't enough! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2013)

File.Name: d714h_d713-015.jpg
Notes:
9286523901 Here is another photo of the unusual male Mealybug (family Pseudococcidae of the old order Homoptera) that I found yesterday. I actually recognized the long white tails in the air, and reached out and grabbed it. Here it is posed on my left hand, while I tried to work the camera with my right hand. (I'm not as steady as I used to be.) The long white tails are really just long hairs covered by a waxy exudate. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2013)

This photo shows the critter better than my [Previous] photo, but I don't like how the tails are off center. Try leaning back a bit - this photo looks better in the thumbnail. It sure looks like a two-winged fly, but it's not. The body is about 1/8 inch long. The females are very different unwinged lumps. I believe these critters are rarely photographed.

File.Name: d715a_d715-045.jpg
Notes:
9298014766 This is a beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It must be newly emerged with those blue colors still showing on its forewings. This is just the second buckeye that I've manged to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2013)

Today was still warm, but the nasty "sundowner winds" were gone. I'm grateful.

File.Name: d715b_d715-021.jpg
Notes: 9297939714 I believe this is a different little Robber Fly (family Asilidae) in the order Diptera. Look at its proboscis and the way its head is hollowed out between the eyes. This one is quite small. I found it perched on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2013)

File.Name: d715c_d715-017.jpg
Notes:
9295050285 Just a fly... probably a red-eyed and red-butted Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae of the order Diptera. I got this photo on a dusty leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2013)

Just a fly... but I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail!

File.Name: d715d_d715-033.jpg
Notes: 9297765236 This is a tiny crab-like Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae, Hemiptera) - probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It's perched on one of the last flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. What do the insects do when the flowers disappear? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 15 July 2013)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bug; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d715e_d715-011.jpg
Notes: 9294957779 This is a another perfect Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera). I was first thinking it's a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae, but maybe it's a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia)? It's still immature, but I guess it's just one step away from being an adult. It must be a female with that interesting ovipostitor. This one is perched in foliage of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2013)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; California Angle-winged Katydid; Bush Katydid; Wild Blackberry

File.Name: d715f_d715-030.jpg
Notes: 9297619564 I guess this is a little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. It has a little spider web house (on the left) with an orb-web attached an inch or two away. Its "house" is made up from the dead bodies of its prey, pirate fashion. This one has made a house in flowering native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2013)

File.Name: d715g_d715-037.jpg
Notes: 9294656535 These are galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family - with an acorn. These galls - maybe all galls? - form on the undersides of the leaves, and I have to gently fold them over. But this is just as I found these galls. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: d715h_d715-035.jpg
Notes:
9297376300 This is a new late-season "flower" of uncommon (in our mountains) Rough-leaved Aster - Aster radulinus in the Asteraceae plant family. If you look very close, you can spot a few violet petals on the upper flower, but I think most of the flowers will be "cleistogamous" - flowering without opening - in the very dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2013)

This native plant is growing on bone dry soil in a place that was cleared for a firebreak a few years ago. I only know it from near this place on the Pass. It's a fire-follower plant, but in this case clearing the chaparral down to bare ground was good enough to give them a place.

File.Name: d716a_d716-003.jpg
Notes:
9305418326 This is a another remarkably thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the insect order Hemiptera. This one is perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's got its sucking proboscis into something black, but I can't tell if it's a seed of a beetle carapace. I've been looking for them, but they really look just like a piece of detritus on the leaves. They are difficult to notice! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2013)

We've been working to sell my Dad's house in Pacific Palisades. Escrow closed today. I'm glad that's all over!

File.Name: d716b_d716-004.jpg
Notes: 9305308804 This is the tiny nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera trying to be invisible under the fold of a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It didn't work! The adults appear later in summer and fill the nights with song. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2013)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d716c_d716-007.jpg
Notes:
9302285479 I found this interesting Fly (order Diptera) on a twig of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family this morning. I'm not sure what kind of fly it is, but it is definitely ant-like, with a nodal bump and even a tiny thorn. There are "ant-like flies" in the family Richardiidae, but none of the photos at BugGuide look quite like this. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2013)

I should apologize for this photo. It was in the shade this morning, and I didn't think to use flash because I so rarely it. But it is an interesting little fly and a nice silhouette!

File.Name: d716d_d715-026.jpg
Notes: 9304996236 I found this handsome little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) on a stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family yesterday. It must be miles away from any creek water in this dry year, so it must have come some distance. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2013)

File.Name: d716e_d716-021.jpg
Notes: 9304928538 Here are new caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies munching seed heads of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. Compare my photos here and here. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2013)
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Climbing Penstemon; Plantain Family
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: d716f_d716-025.jpg
Notes:
9304845326 This is another flowering branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. It's shrouded with web-spinning caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies. The caterpillars do kill a branch now and then. They look like tent caterpillars, but they are really next years butterflies, so treat them well! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2013)

The caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they emerge and pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year!
CommonNames: Climbing Penstemon; Plantain Family; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: d716g_d716-029.jpg
Notes: 9304764278 There are still new flowers of Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in the canyon. (That name sound like something from Harry Potter, year 4!) These are one of our most dependable mid-summer flowers. The blue pollen is striking, but the flowers should also look more blue. Sometimes my camera gets confused between blue and magenta. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2013)

File.Name: d716h_d716-017.jpg
Notes: 9304712350 I believe this is another native Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica) in the Onagraceae plant family still flowering in the canyon in mid-summer - without the speckles. These were flowering a month ago, but a few plants have hung on. These flowers also go by the name Farewell-to-Spring, though spring is long gone. That's longevity! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2013)

File.Name: d717a_d717-046.jpg
Notes:
9315857264 This is another Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) butterfly perched on native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. A pair of these were romantically inclined, fluttering around the shrub together. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)

Flickr went down last night while I was posting my photos. I'll back-date a couple of these.

File.Name: d717b_d717-037.jpg
Notes: 9315778446 This is another small but wonderful Robber Fly (family Asilidae) in the order Diptera. It's perched for a moment on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Look at its proboscis and the way its head is hollowed out between the eyes like a dragonfly. It's quite small, only about 1/4 inch long. Compare this recent photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)

File.Name: d717c_d717-007.jpg
Notes: 9309366465 Mystery solved! Thanks to tjdegraaf, I believe this is a Black scavenger fly or Ensign fly, genus Sepsis in the family Sepsidae. It's an interesting interesting ant-like fly (order Diptera) on native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family, with a "waist" and shiny black body. It's doing morning yoga on the leaf, moving around constantly and cleaning its wings, which are pretty distinctive. One meaning of the word "ensign" is a flag - and these little flies wave their wings constantly. "Ensign Fly" is a good name for them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)
CommonNames: Black scavenger fly; Ensign fly; fly; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Sepsis; Sepsidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Sepsis; Sepsidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d717d_d717-021.jpg
Notes: 9309338441 This is a small but gorgeous Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the dry edge of our garden. What color is it? It has iridescent green and blue and orange, depending how you look at it. These little flies have been scarce in this drought year, and hard to photograph because they dance around so much. This is a 800x800 pixel crop from my 4800x3200 original. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)

File.Name: d717e_d717-051.jpg
Notes: 9309255259 This is an interesting "True Bug" (Hemiptera) on new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It has dark beetle-like colors, but it's definitely a bug. You can see it's thin proboscis in front supported by the jointed hand-like labium in back. It's interesting to see them in separate pieces like this. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)

File.Name: d717f_d717-048.jpg
Notes: 9309188287 This is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae on a bare stem of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. This one has a rather thin abdomen, but others are fat. I assume the males are smaller. I'm confused. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: d717g_d717-029.jpg
Notes: 9311936476 These are pom-pom-like seed heads of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Ranunculaceae plant family. Just beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)

File.Name: d717h_d717-039.jpg
Notes:
9309118215 For some reason, we're having a bumper crop of wild cherries in this drought year. Go figure.

These are unripe fruit of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family - lots of them! The old spanish name for this plant is Islay. The ripe red fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit are actually poisonous. The flesh is thin, and the Chumash people mostly ate the large pits after leaching them. If you crush a leaf between your fingers, you can smell the wild cherry! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)

File.Name: d718a_d718-003.jpg
Notes:
9318804860 This is a cute little net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Issidae. This is the first one I've seen this year. I got this photo in my usual morning place on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2013)

Whenever your photos are feeling stale, just park by something in flower and wait. Photos will happen! Summer flowers are getting scarce in this drought year, but I keep finding new insects. We're heading to Oregon "Real Soon Now" - before the end of the month at least. There's lots to do before we leave.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Neaethus; Issidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Neaethus; Issidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d718b_d718-020.jpg
Notes: 9318702922 I guess this is a tiny Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, order Diptera) working a new summer flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It looks flecked with gold dust. Maybe it's subfamily Toxophorinae, but that's just a guess. A different one of these flies met a bad end with a spider in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2013)

associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Toxophorinae

File.Name: d718c_d718-016.jpg
Notes: 9315867829 This tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) has captured a tiny fly (order Diptera) on summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I suspect the fly is a atypical Bee Fly in the family Bombyliidae. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2013)

File.Name: d718d_d718-024.jpg
Notes: 9318590810 This is a tiny crab-like Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae, Hemiptera) - probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) - standing at attention on new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2013)

associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: d718e_d718-012.jpg
Notes:
9318507766 This is a small "looper" type caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. I guess it will become a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae). Julie looked at this photo and said "It must be a Buckwheat Caterpillar!" It is well camouflaged with those white and brown colors on the white and brown flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2013)

Whenever your photos are feeling stale, just park by something in flower and wait. Photos will happen! Though the summer flowers are getting scarce in this drought year...

File.Name: d718f_d717-011.jpg
Notes:
9318459428 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family - next to a bird splat on the leaf. I've seen this before, that the wasps land by the bird splats! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)

These wasps make their paper nests under our eaves. Ferocious as they look, they've never bothered me. Leave them be.
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d718g_d717-015.jpg
Notes:
9315512789 This is a another photo of the perfect Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) - probably a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia, but I'm not sure until it matures. It looks a lot like images of the Mexican Bush Katydid (Scudderia mexicana) at BugGuide.

It's still immature with "training wings", but I guess it's just one step away from being an adult. I guess those paddles on the back are its interesting ovipositor. This one is perched in foliage of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's been perched in about the same place for the last few day. This particular critter is a well-photographed celebrity! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)
CommonNames: Katydid; Bush Katydid; Mexican Bush Katydid; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Scudderia mexicana; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d718h_d717-002.jpg
Notes: 9318256494 This is another tiny nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera on a stem of native Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I reckon it's trying to be invisible, not eat the dry flower heads. I keep finding these critters trying (and failing) to be invisible. I guess the nymphs, like the adults, work the night shift. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Gnaphalium californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Gnaphalium californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d719a_d719-020.jpg
Notes:
9322993355 This is a fantastic mating pair of crab-like Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae, Hemiptera) - probably Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica). They are having their nuptials on a new mid-summer flower of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. They look like they're digging it! My wife Julie looked at this photo and said "They look like dragons!" These little critters are color-shifters, and you can see the one on top is a bit yellow to match the flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 July 2013)

There was an odd orange cast to the light this morning. I didn't see or smell smoke, but then I realized this is a thin layer of smoke from the Mountain Fire way down in the San Jacinto Mountains. It's a sign that the weather is changing, setting up a monsoonal weather flow from east to west.
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bugs; California Goldenrod
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Solidago californica; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Solidago californica; Asteraceae

File.Name: d719b_d719-012.jpg
Notes:
9322845603 This is a beautiful little Blue (subfamily Polyommatinae) butterfly working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. But what kind is it?

It might be a Bernardino Blue aka Square-spotted Blue (Euphilotes bernardino or Euphilotes battoides bernardino) butterfly - but I suspect it's an out-of-season Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly. The Bernardinos have heavier markings and the wings are spotted along the margins. The Acmons lack the spots and have the iridescent sprinkles on its hindwings, which this one seems to have. It's doubly confusing because the Square-spotted Blues have buckwheat as their host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2013)
CommonNames: Blue; California Buckwheat; Bernardino Blue; Square-spotted Blue; Acmon Blue
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino; Plebejus acmon
associatedTaxa: Polyommatinae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino; Plebejus acmon

File.Name: d719c_d719-007.jpg
Notes:
9322807249 I found this handsome little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) on a dead stem of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It must be miles away from any creek water in this dry year, so it must have come some distance. It has gold colors on it's abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2013)

It's hard to fit these skinny critters into a normal photo frame!

File.Name: d719d_d719-041.jpg
Notes: 9325556342 I guess this is a Megachilid Bee (Megachilidae, Hymenoptera) on a new summer flower of native composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. The bee is the size of a honey bee. These plants are just starting to flower, and already the bees are finding them! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 July 2013)

File.Name: d719e_d719-044.jpg
Notes: 9325525306 Here are new late-season flowers of native composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are just starting to flower - when most wildflowers have been done for a couple of months! These plants are real survivors, and insects like them. See my [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 July 2013)

File.Name: d719f_d719-026.jpg
Notes: 9322637979 Here are new mid-summer flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family against a background of not quite flowering Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's a wonder that anything can flower in this dry summer of a drought year, but here they are. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 July 2013)
ScientificName: Solidago californica; Asteraceae; Eriogonum elongatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Solidago californica; Asteraceae; Eriogonum elongatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: d719g_d719-029.jpg
Notes:
9325337484 Here are more new flowers of the Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Plantain Family (Plantaginaceae) for reasons that escape me. It has "ternate" leaves in whorls of three, though it's hard to see in this photo. They are growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. It really does have bluish stems - in a "blue dun" trout fly sort of way. These are classic "hummingbird flowers" - though I didn't see any hummers visiting on this day. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 18 July 2013)

It's interesting that our late season summer flowers seem to divide between red hummingbird flowers and yellow flowers for everyone else. I did spot new yellow flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum, Asteraceae) by the river yesterday. I'll go back when it's not so windy.
CommonNames: Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella; Plantain Family; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Keckiella ternata; septentrionalis; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Keckiella ternata; septentrionalis; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d719h_d718-001.jpg
Notes: 9325305868 This is another photo of the same cute little net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera that I show in my [Previous] photo. It has a front end like an old truck! I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Issidae. I got this photo in my usual morning place on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2013)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Neaethus; Issidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Neaethus; Issidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: d720a_d720-045.jpg
Notes:
9329437493 This is a remarkable Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia) in the tribe Bembicini of the Apoidea of the Hymenoptera working a new flower of of the late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. I had to stand on tip-toes for this photo, but it's a keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2013)

It was a pleasant surprise to find these new flowers today, I've been waiting for them. It was pleasant today, a bit cooler than its been. But it was breezy this morning, as the marine layer clouds started to spill over the mountains. I call these "fog winds" - caused by the unstable air at the upper edge of the temperature inversion.
CommonNames: Sand Wasp; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria
ScientificName: Steniolia; Bembicini; Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae

File.Name: d720b_d720-033.jpg
Notes:
9332177172 Watch out little beetle - danger ahead!

This is a small Metallic Wood-boring Beetle (Buprestidae) in the order Coleoptera on a new flower of the late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's almost touching the butt of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae! But that spider is already busy - see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2013)

I agree, it's a creepy photo - but I record what I see. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2013)

They start so beautiful before they mature into large green adults. This one is perched on a leaf of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. File.Name: d720h_d720-040.jpg
Notes: 9331738552 These are new nuts forming on unusual native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. The pea-size nuts are tasty if you can get them before the birds and squirrels. The white flowers that come in the spring are among the most beautiful flowers in our mountains. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2013)

File.Name: d721a_d721-013.jpg
Notes:
9337534267 These are ripening fruit of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. They sure look like little avocados, in the same family! The fruit taste very bitter unless they are dead ripe, but the nuts are delicious, almost like chocolate when they are prepared right. See this YouTube video. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2013)

We had interesting weather today, but it wasn't a good day for photos - cloudy and windy, with wind from the east which is unusual. I did the best I could. It's hard to get these close photos when everything is moving, not just my camera!

I've been at a disadvantage tonight because TCM is showing "Mon Oncle" - the fabulous movie by Jacques Tati. The TV is just around the corner from my desk, so I go back and forth. I hope my comments make sense tonight!

Update 9:00 AM - We actually had a bit of rain early this morning. Just 0.03" in the rain gauge, but it smells nice.

File.Name: d721b_d721-005.jpg
Notes: 9337412337 This is a another remarkably thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the insect order Hemiptera. This one is perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I try to get the eyes in focus on my photos, but this one has the sharp proboscis in focus. Good enough! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2013)

File.Name: d721c_d721-008.jpg
Notes: 9337244637 This is my favorite Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) that I've shown before. It perches in about the same place every morning, it's almost a pet! It's probably a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia, but I'm not sure until it matures. It looks a lot like images of the Mexican Bush Katydid (Scudderia mexicana) at BugGuide. It has an odd paddle-like ovipositor. This one is mostly perched on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2013)

9337058985 This is another different Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The bristles coming from its butt are remarkable, like jet exhaust or optical fiber! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2013)

We had interesting weather today, but it wasn't a good day for photos - cloudy and windy, with wind from the east which is unusual. I did the best I could. It's hard to get these close photos when everything is moving, not just my camera!

Notes:
9339638148 I first thought this was a Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae) because of its shape - but the antennae are below the eyes and it doesn't have spines on its rear legs. This is a definitely a different Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2013)

We had interesting weather today, but it wasn't a good day for photos - cloudy and windy, with wind from the east which is unusual. I did the best I could. It's hard to get these close photos when everything is moving, not just my camera!

Notes: 9336760935 Here is another fantastic summer flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. There's an open flower on the right, a spent one on the left, and a new seed pod in the back. This one is growing in the canyon at home. See what you're missing Mark! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2013)

File.Name: d721h_d720-041.jpg
Notes: 9336760935 Here is another photo of a new flower of late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family, without a wasp in the way. The purple-tipped stamens look like they've been dipped in sugar. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2013)

File.Name: d722a_d722-016.jpg
Notes:
9345786189 This is the summer form of a Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly, perched (for a moment) on a leaf of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. They look quite different in the spring, compare this photo. These butterflies are kind of moth-like with weak fluttery flight - but they are still not easy to photograph! They are definitely butterflies, knobby antennae and all. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2013)

We had some active weather today - a bit of summer rain early this morning, mostly cloudy skies, and quite breezy. It was a challenge to get new photos, so I had to visit the archives.

File.Name: d722c_d722-010.jpg
Notes:
9345603431 I found this interesting little fly (order Diptera) this morning, close to the tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in my [Previous] photo. It's perched on a leaf of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. I guess from the antennae and proboscis and curled-under abdomen that it's a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae), maybe the genus Zodion. Other members of the family are remarkable wasp mimics, so it's interesting to find one that isn't. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2013)

It was pretty breezy today. I took *many* photos to get this one keeper.

It really is tiny - judge by the flowers! It was pretty breezy today. I took *many* photos to get this one keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2013)

File.Name: d722e_d720-005.jpg
Notes:
9348161702 Just a fly... but I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds!

This might be just another Muscidae in the order Diptera, but it looks sprinkled with gold dust. I found it on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae. I don't know what kind of fly it is, but this might be the same kind of fly that's receiving the "kiss of death" by a spider in my [Previous photo] taken on the same day. Maybe it's the same fly? (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2013)

File.Name: d722f_d717-038.jpg
Notes: 9345335827 This is another new gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They look like little mushrooms growing from the oak leaf, but they are really the "nests" of tiny wasps - made by the oaks! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2013)

Notes: 9345208443 I'm not sure, but maybe this is the gall of a Red Cone Gall Wasp (Andricus kingi), which is a tiny Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). It kind of looks like a little acorn sprouting from the leaf of this Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm confused - maybe that's why I've been sitting on this photo for a while! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

File.Name: d722h_d712-050.jpg
Notes:
9347893748 Up front on the right is an odd wasp gall on leaves of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It sure looks like the gall of a Yellow Wig Gall Wasp (Andricus fullawayi) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but I usually find those on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) - a "white oak." Maybe the wasps cross over to this "intermediate oak" - or maybe it's something else?

In the back-left is a different "lumpy gall" (my name). These stem galls always cause the stems to bend at an angle. I don't know what critter makes them happen. I'm confused - maybe that's why I've been sitting on this photo for a while! (San Marcos Pass, 12 July 2013)

9356261502 This is a California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, formerly known as Hemaris diffinis) working the new flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. It's a new crop of flowers on the shrub, and this may be a new brood of moths working the flowers - at least I haven't seen them for a while. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2013)

Today was another beautiful day in paradise, still a bit breezy, but not too bad - there were moments of calm between the gusts. The rain clouds are all gone. QuÈ l·stima.

Notes:
9353261775 This is a large Silver Argiope (Argiope argenatata) spider in the family Araneidae sitting on its web. This is the first one I've seen this year, and I was excited enough that I ruined its web fussing with my tripod.

This one is has made its web among the now-leafless stems of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. You can see a bit of the spider's "stabilimentum" in the lower-right. Try leaning back a bit to see the amazing face on its abdomen! (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2013)

File.Name: d723e_d723-035.jpg
Notes:
9355901666 These are new acorns on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's usually another "scrub oak" in our mountains, though they sometimes grow into large trees in the north-facing canyons of the Santa Ynez Mountains. The acorns are short and squat, with thin scales. The leaves are smooth or spiny, often dusted with gold powder on the underside. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2013)

We also have Quercus berberidifolia and Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens in our mountains that grow as scrub oaks. I'll see if I can make one photo that shows them all tomorrow.

File.Name: d723f_d722-013.jpg
Notes: 9353029715 Here are new acorns of a different Scrub Oak - Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens in the Fagaceae plant family. The cup scales are flat and thin and the leaves are shiny underneath, quite different from Quercus berberidifolia or Quercus chrysolepis. I rotated a leaf at about 5:00 so you can see the underside. For some reason, I mostly find them on the west side of Highway 154. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2013)
ScientificName: Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Quercus chrysolepis
associatedTaxa: Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Quercus chrysolepis

File.Name: d723g_d723-033.jpg
Notes:
9355712262 This is a large satisfying acorn of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The acorns come from a lumpy or "tubercled" cup and the leaves are spiny and dull underneath. This is by far the most common of our scrub oaks. Compare my [Next] photos. They are the staff of life in the dry parts of our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2013)

We also have Quercus chrysolepis and Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens in our mountains that grow as scrub oaks. I'll see if I can make one photo that shows them all tomorrow.

File.Name: d723h_d723-052.jpg
Notes: 9352859371 These are the fantastic galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family - with acorns! There also seems to be a plumed seed of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha) in the Ranunculaceae plant family in the photo. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2013)

File.Name: d724a_d724-029.jpg
Notes:
9368996906 I believe this is a large female California Mantid (Stagmomantis californica) in the family Mantidae of the order Mantodea. It's large, maybe 2+ inches long, and it has short wings, indicating a female. It's climbing along a branch of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. This one doesn't have dark transverse bands on its abdomen, but maybe it's newly molted? It has real "alien eyes" and delicate toes! They seem quite uncommon in our mountains, but they look just like dead grass stems and would be easy to overlook. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2013)

It was quite warm today, in the 90s - with calm winds for a change. I'm posted early and just two photos because Flickr is scheduled to do down from 4:00-10:00 pm tonight, just when I usually post. It was nice of them to let us know. I hope there are no unpleasant surprises when it comes back up. (Pleasant surprises are ok!)

Notes: 9366147961 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly in the woods today - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. This is the first one I've seen this year, and hopefully not the last. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2013)

Notes:
9359406459 This is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a slightly tattered flower of some kind of genus Fuchsia (family Onagraceae) in the garden. The male butterflies get all the glory, but the ladies are quite beautiful also. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2013)

I'm posting early - and just these four photos - because Julie and I are stepping out for dinner tonight with friends.

File.Name: d724d_d724-002.jpg
Notes: 9362141352 This is another California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, formerly known as Hemaris diffinis) hitting the center of a new flower of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. What beautiful creatures they are! (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2013)

associatedTaxa: Hemaris thetis; Hemaris diffinis; Buddleja davidii; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: d725a_d725-049.jpg
Notes: 9362049838 This is a two-fer of insect pollinators on a new flower cluster of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleja davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden. There's a remarkable Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia) in the tribe Bembicini of the Apoidea of the Hymenoptera in the upper-left, showing its stout proboscis; and an equally remarkable California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, formerly known as Hemaris diffinis) in the back on the lower-right. These have been busy flowers for the past couple of days! This is the "second brood" of flowers on these well-named shrubs. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2013)

File.Name: d725b_d725-037.jpg
Notes: 9362004408 I believe this is a little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. It really has two webs - a neat orb-web, and a disorganized cobweb a few inches away where it makes a retreat. It has faces on its abdomen - at least two of them. There are sad-looking upturned eyes on top, but also a rather perfect cat face down below. It's a stunning spider. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2013)

File.Name: d726a_d725-043.jpg
Notes:
9375240948 Here is another photo of the large female California Mantid (Stagmomantis californica) in the family Mantidae of the order Mantodea that I found yesterday. I found it on a stem of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2013)

I went back again today, but I couldn't find it. It's quite large, maybe 2+ inches long, and it has short wings, indicating a female. It has real "alien eyes"! They are quite uncommon in our mountains, but they look just like dead grass stems and would be easy to overlook.

We had interesting weather today - mostly cloudy with some monsoon flow, but no rain. The clouds kept it cool, but there was some wind. I went to the river to look for bigger things that wouldn't flutter so much.
