File.Name: d726d_d725-056.jpg
Notes: 9375105092 This is a pretty little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) on spent flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It must be miles away from any creek water in this dry year, so it must have come some distance. It's munching on some tiny prey, and there's a little bee or fly bugging it! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2013)

File.Name: d726e_d726-032.jpg
Notes:
9372213489 It's interesting that some native shrubs aren't stump-spouting after the "White Fire" burned along the river about two months ago. This is Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family, without a sign of a stump-sprout. It's doubly interesting because these shrubs sometimes form large rings in the woods, and there's a bit of a ring-like structure in this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 July 2013)

Note that this shrub was growing from a mound. See my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: d726f_d726-066.jpg
Notes:
9372121549 This is an ecology photo - probably the hardest kind of photo to take. It shows the burned out vegetation in an overflow channel of the Santa Ynez River where I've gotten many of my late-summer photos over the years. It was burned by the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area about two months ago. I can't remember when the river has actually flowed through this channel. Maybe the 1969 floods?

The shrubs along the river are mostly stump-spouting, as they should be. But what's more interesting to me are the dark mounds around the burned-out shrubs. I guess this is the result of the shrubs catching dust from the mostly right-to-left (west-to-east) winds. You can see that the gradient of the mounds is greater on the left side. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 July 2013)

File.Name: d726g_d726-040.jpg
Notes: 9372054039 This is a recovering family of native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) plants in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. They were burned by the "White Fire" that started at White Rock picnic area about two months ago. You can see new growth on most of them - the green below the tan. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 July 2013)
ScientificName: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: d726h_d726-061.jpg
Notes: 9374757734 This cluster of Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) trees in the Platanaceae plant family is stump-sprouting after the "White Fire" roared up the river a couple of months ago on May 27, 2013. Some of the sycamores are also sprouting new growth from their branches. Look - even the rocks are charred! Life goes on. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 July 2013)

File.Name: d727a_d727-043.jpg
Notes:
9381782802 This is a native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family with a Megachilid Bee (Megachilidae, Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera). The bee came into my photo as I was taking the photo. It startled me in the viewfinder, and as a result neither bee nor flower is quite in focus. Good enough! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 July 2013)

It was another beautiful day in the mountains - a bit breezy, but not a problem. We're hoping to head for Oregon next Friday. It's time. I'm getting that vague (and FALSE!) feeling that I've seen everything.
CommonNames: Red Thistle; Cobweb Thistle; Megachilid Bee
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Megachilidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; venustum; Asteraceae; Megachilidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: d727b_d727-033.jpg
Notes: 9378931641 I believe these are new flowers of Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's a tough plant to photograph. If you get in close enough to actually show the pretty flower clusters, it looks like any other buckwheat! This is a compromise shot. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 July 2013)

File.Name: d727c_d727-022.jpg
Notes: 9381654632 These are new summer flowers of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. The shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. That's the staminate (pollen-bearing) male on the left and the pistillate (seed-bearing) female plant on the right. It's a Southern California endemic, but it's pretty common in our mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 July 2013)

File.Name: d727d_d727-025.jpg
Notes: 9378814091 These are odd new "flowers" of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Orobanchaceae. Yep, the white petals show that these are really flowers. They are common in mid-summer, though you have to hunt for the white petals. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 July 2013)

File.Name: d727e_d727-014.jpg
Notes: 9381535768 This is a large fat acorn of native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It still has a couple of erect dreadlock-like male catkins, which distinguish it in the keys from the oaks with droopy catkins. The acorns are remarkable with that scaly cup. They are not common in our mountains, but they grow here and there on north-facing slopes. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 July 2013)

File.Name: d727f_d725-032.jpg
Notes: 9378708109 These are new acorns of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's the dominant tree in our woods, but mature acorns are scare in this drought year. The acorn cups have thin overlapping scales. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2013)

File.Name: d727g_d726-043.jpg
Notes:
9378635611 Even after wildfire and drought, life goes on.

This is a burned branch of a fairly large Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family showing new life after the "White Fire" burned through. There's new life on the branch. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 July 2013)

File.Name: d727h_d726-054.jpg
Notes: 9378567037 Paradise still lives up to its name - if you know where to go. This is a Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Salicaceae plant family hanging over a spring-fed pool, with cattails. The "White Fire" burned along the left side of the river and crossed it just once in this place. You can see a singed tree in the background on the right. I won't show the location on my map, but I'll show where the trail starts. The water is deep enough to sit in, and thoroughly pleasant! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 July 2013)

File.Name: d728a_d728-055.jpg
Notes:
9390313682 I did get one close shot of a large male Tarantula Hawk (Pepsis mildei, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) today. It's working a new flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. They are so blue that they look black. This is a pretty tight crop because it wouldn't let me get closer! (See my [Previous] photo.) I reckon it's a male because of its smaller size and straight antennae. The females are larger with coiled antennae - dragonfly size! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

I went back to the river today when the marine layer fog came over the mountains at home, with "fog winds" caused by the turbulent air at the temperature inversion. It was windy at the river as well, but I did get some nice photos.
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d728b_d728-052.jpg
Notes:
9390214432 I reckon this is a mighty male Tarantula Hawk (Pepsis mildei, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera) - flying away from me after working the flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

Darn, these large wasps are camera shy! I hardly had time to position my tripod when it flew away. This episode was repeated several times. This was the main view I had!
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae

File.Name: d728c_d728-038.jpg
Notes: 9390095824 This is a male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working a new flower of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I got this photo by the river today. It was pretty windy, but I did what I could. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

File.Name: d728d_d728-045.jpg
Notes: 9389987340 Here are a pair of pretty Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae, order Diptera). The one on the left appears to be interested in the flower of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family, but the one on the right is interested in it! I reckon it has mating on its mind. Why do I guess that's the female on the left and the male on the right?? It was windy when I got this photo - I was lucky to get even one fly in focus! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

File.Name: d728e_d728-008.jpg
Notes: 9387102123 This is a another remarkably thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the insect order Hemiptera about to take wing from a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. Yep, I bugged it that much. I know the focus isn't quite right - I try to get the eyes sharp - but it's still a pretty cool photo. You can see its slender proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 28 July 2013)

File.Name: d728f_d728-016.jpg
Notes:
9386903601 These are new summer flowers of Cliff Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. It was breezy, so I couldn't get closer, but the flowers really are pretty with those yellow stamens. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

It's interesting that the more succulent Malacothrix saxatilis var. saxatilis grows along the coastal bluffs near Santa Barbara, and this much more common variety grows in the interior country - but the first named collection remains official. Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family is another example of this.
CommonNames: Cliff Desert Dandelion; Santa Barbara Honeysuckle
ScientificName: Malacothrix saxatilis; tenuifolia; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; saxatilis; Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Malacothrix saxatilis; tenuifolia; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; saxatilis; Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: d728f_d728-028.jpg
Notes:
9386841197 These are pretty new flowers of a native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

It's a beautiful large shrub that has an odd distribution. I find it in the foothills in Santa Barbara; along the Santa Ynez River at Paradise; and in the remote back country by Pool Rock - but not in between. It's interesting that all these places are arranged nearly north-south across our east-west mountains.

File.Name: d728g_d728-063.jpg
Notes:
9386599097 This is an odd Calabazilla aka Coyote Melon or Buffalo Gourd (Cucurbita foetidissima) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. These plants are usually huge, but I suspect they were mowed down not too long ago. I found the old gourd on the ground nearby and placed it in the photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

The species name tells how it smells, but the apple-size gourds were used by Chumash native Americans for soap, tools, and rattles. These native gourds are not common. This colony near Paradise Camp has been here as long as I can remember. I wonder if it dates back to the old days.

File.Name: d729a_d729-023.jpg
Notes:
9395712487 This is a handsome caterpillar (Lepidoptera) munching new mid-summer very glandular flowers of native Coast Tarweed (Madia sativa) in the Asteraceae plant family. I have no idea what of moth or butterfly this caterpillar will become, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2013)

Today started nice enough, but then the marine layer clouds blew over the mountains, with resulting winds. The clouds went away, but it was windy all day. I've been distracted anyway, getting ready for our trip to Oregon in a few days.

File.Name: d729b_d729-002.jpg
Notes:
9395607437 Just a fly... but I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds!

I reckon this is a small Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera. It's perched for a moment on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2013)

File.Name: d729c_d729-013.jpg
Notes:
9395548023 "Not tonight honey, it's that time..."

This is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) molting it's old skin. It's a regular problem for critters with hard scales or shells that they have to shed them to grow. I can't recall seeing a lizard like this! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2013)

File.Name: d729d_d729-035.jpg
Notes:
9395416823 This is a beautiful new red flower of native Scarlet Monkeyflower (Mimulus cardinalis) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. It's growing in our "water garden" - just an old fiberglass tub filled with water and plants, not to mention frogs and worms. I can't remember where we collected it, probably rescued from a slide along some creek. It's been flowering here every year for many years. If you look close, you can spot a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, order Diptera) in the air flying away from the flower because I disturbed it. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2013)

I've been watching this plant and hoping it would flower before we leave for Oregon next Friday!
ScientificName: Mimulus cardinalis; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Mimulus cardinalis; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera

File.Name: d729e_d729-009.jpg
Notes: 9398137020 Here are new flower stalks of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family growing along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains today. I really like this photo, taken as the fog/clouds were just clearing out today. Nice light! (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2013)

File.Name: d729f_d728-023.jpg
Notes:
9398032748 These are new yellow composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. It's a "buggy plant" that attracts many pollinator insects, and I depend on it in late summer for my photos! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

I reckon our real midsummer day is August 1, Lammas or Lughnasadh, the cross-quarter day midway between solstice and equinox. That's when these yellow-flowered composites come really into their own.

File.Name: d729g_d728-029.jpg
Notes:
9397956932 These are new yellow composite flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. It's a "buggy plant" that attracts many insects, though it was too windy when I took this to get close. If you look close, you can see a pretty little emerald-green Sweat Bee (family Halictidae, Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) in the middle-right. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

I reckon our real midsummer day is August 1, Lammas or Lughnasadh, the cross-quarter day midway between solstice and equinox. That's when these yellow-flowered composites come really into their own.
ScientificName: Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae; Halictidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae; Halictidae; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: d729h_d726-051.jpg
Notes: 9395030715 These are new yellow composite flowers of native Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I reckon our real midsummer day is August 1, Lammas or Lughnasadh, the cross-quarter day midway between solstice and equinox. That's when the yellow-flowered composites come into their own. This one is less common. It grows in marshy and springy places. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 July 2013)

File.Name: d730a_d730-040.jpg
Notes:
9403478917 This is a large mature female Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) perched on a on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. It more obvious genitalia than the male in my [Previous] photo. I'm not sure if it is Scudderia furcata or Scudderia mexicana, but I reckon it's one of those. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2013)

It was luck to find these katydids this morning! I've been pretty distracted getting ready for our trip to Oregon. We're hopefully leaving on Friday morning. There's the usual packing, but we also have to assume our house might burn in a wildfire - so we're putting art, old photos, hard drives, etc. into storage. Tomorrow is moving day, so I won't have time for new photos, but the archives are in good shape.
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Scudderia furcata; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Scudderia furcata; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: d730b_d730-045.jpg
Notes: 9406068888 This is a large mature male Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It has less obvious genitalia than the female in my [Next] photo. I'm not sure if it is Scudderia furcata or Scudderia mexicana, but I reckon it's one of those. (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2013)
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Scudderia furcata; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Scudderia furcata; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: d730c_d730-047.jpg
Notes: 9403181053 I found this odd husk of an insect today on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It looks kind of cicada-like, but it's small, only about 1/4 inch long. Maybe it's the nymph of a Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera? The husk is split along the front - and look, it has its proboscis planted in the leaf! Maybe that's to get some leverage when climbing out of its old skin? (San Marcos Pass, 30 July 2013)

File.Name: d730d_d729-001.jpg
Notes:
9405858560 Just a fly... but I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds!

Maybe this is another Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae of the order Diptera - those red eyes seem distinctive, though this one was quite small. Maybe it's just another Muscidae? Whatever it is, it's interesting. I'm better at finding flies than identifying them. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2013)

File.Name: d730e_d729-031.jpg
Notes: 9402941587 This is the nest of the caterpillar of an American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly on native Fragrant Everlasting (Gnaphalium canescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. I have found the caterpillars outside the nest before - see this photo (on Fotolog). The plants are remarkable soft to the touch. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2013)

File.Name: d730f_d726-069.jpg
Notes:
9405583262 These are pretty new midsummer flowers of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. Hooray, it's got it proper name back in Jepson, and I'm glad. (Just wait, and all will be made right!)

I love the way it sounds - "cor-e-thro-gyn-e-fil-ag-in-i-folia" - it kind of rolls off the tongue. These are the first new flowers I've found this year, but other plants are getting ready to flower. It's amazing that anything can flower after the dry summer in this drought year. In some years they flower into the new year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 July 2013)

File.Name: d730g_d727-029.jpg
Notes: 9402794447 Here are pretty new flowers of late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a wonder that anything can flower in this drought year, but here they are. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 July 2013)

File.Name: d730h_d724-022.jpg
Notes:
9405482886 Fall colors in July, it's that kind of year.

This photo shows the pretty foliage of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family that is already turning red to drop its leaves in this drought year. Well, it's a good survival trick that I'll bet the other evergreen shrubs wish they could do. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2013)

File.Name: d731a_d731-051.jpg
Notes:
9410835017 This is another handsome white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly working flowers of Cat Mint (Nepeta cataria, Lamiaceae) on the edge of our garden. I like how the wings are in motion, but the head and coiled proboscis are still. See this photo for a clear photo of the wings. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2013)

Yeah, these are all the same insects I shown before, but they are all new photos, taken today. We're getting ready for our trip to Oregon for most of the month of August. Today we put our art, old photos, hard drives, etc. into storage in case the house burns down - a real possibility in this drought year. I did have some time for photos this morning, and I used it! Tomorrow is packing day if we keep our schedule, so I won't have much chance for new photos.

File.Name: d731b_d731-046.jpg
Notes: 9413525324 This is another photo of the large mature female Bush Katydid (Scudderia, Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) perched on a on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. I got this shot this morning. The critter moved a few feet from yesterday onto a different frond. I'm not sure if it is Scudderia furcata or Scudderia mexicana, but I reckon it's one of those. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2013)
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Scudderia furcata; Scudderia mexicana
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae; Scudderia furcata; Scudderia mexicana

File.Name: d731c_d731-039.jpg
Notes: 9413468546 This is another red-eyed Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae of the order Diptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. Look - it has a little bright red Mite (Acari) on its butt! This mite appears to be sucking like a tick, not just hitchhiking. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2013)
ScientificName: Sarcophagidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Acari
associatedTaxa: Sarcophagidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Acari

File.Name: d731d_d731-040.jpg
Notes:
9410618799 This is yet another photo of a another remarkably thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the insect order Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2013)

Yeah, these are same insects I shown before, but they are new photos. We're getting ready for our trip to Oregon for most of the month of August. Today we put our art, old photos, hard drives, etc. into storage in case the house burns down - a real possibility in this drought year. But I did have some time for photos this morning, and I used it!

File.Name: d731e_d728-043.jpg
Notes: 9410589529 This is a perfect mandala of new yellow composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. Very beautiful! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

File.Name: d731f_d728-050.jpg
Notes: 9413318790 These are the soft white flowers and stems of native Fragrant Everlasting (Gnaphalium canescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. There really are flowers here - you can see some yellow petals on the right! American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterflies prefer these flowers for their nests, see this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 July 2013)

File.Name: d731g_d729-026.jpg
Notes:
9413176988 This really is a non-native weed, and I'm sorry to find it in the mountains. These are new flowers and seeds of Common Beggar-ticks (Bidens pilosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. I found an infestation by the old Goddard Campground along West Camino Cielo road. I can't recall seeing it in the mountains before, but it only grows in this one place so far. (San Marcos Pass, 29 July 2013)

You have to go way back in SB to remember Goddard Campground! I used to camp here when I visited my friends at UCSB. There's a nice piece about it here.

File.Name: d731h_d726-058.jpg
Notes: 9410273089 I believe this is the Small flowered Nightshade aka American Black Nightshade (Solanum americanum aka S. nodiflorum) in the Solanaceae plant family growing like a weed along the Santa Ynez River. I've always figured this was a non-native weed, but I just looked it up and it's now considered to be a native weed. It grows at home as well, and I've been pulling it as a weed for years. (It's still doing well, like a weed.) Well, live and learn! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 July 2013)
CommonNames: Small flowered Nightshade; American Black Nightshade

File.Name: d801a_d801-058.jpg
Notes:
9417209731 Here is another fantastic Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae, Hemiptera) - probably Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica) waiting for prey on a single flower of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. You can clearly see its mighty proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2013)

Well, we're not getting away to Oregon on Friday - there's just too much to do before we leave. Hopefully we'll get away on Saturday.

What's up with the Flickr stat counts? 1,000+ hits a day is flattering, but it's not right for this page.
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bugs; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: d801b_d801-060.jpg
Notes: 9419940550 Here is a fantastic Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae, Hemiptera) - probably Pacific Ambush Bugs (Phymata pacifica) waiting for prey on flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. These predator insects are small but mighty. They wait... (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2013)
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Pacific Ambush Bugs; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Phymata pacifica; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: d801c_d801-052.jpg
Notes: 9417093263 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae has a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) for prey on a flower cluster of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's going for the neck. (San Marcos Pass, 1 August 2013)

File.Name: d902a_d822-202.jpg
Notes:
9660477954 Here are large summer-run Steelhead Trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss) holding in Big Bend Pool on Steamboat Creek, a major tributary of the North Umpqua River. The fish are 2+ feet long and weigh 10-20 pounds (sometimes more). These fish are all native, with a visible "adipose fin" on the top-rear. (Hatchery fish have clipped fins.) See my [Previous] photos for more info, and the FishBase website. (Big Bend Pool, Steamboat Creek, North Umpqua River, 28 August 2013)

I used to fly fish for these delicious creatures, but I lost that killer instinct. These fish have been through so much to get this far up the river - let them be! I do still enjoy breaking the hook off a fly and fishing for the strike. Steamboat Creek is closed to all fishing.

File.Name: d902b_d828-271.jpg
Notes: 9657213381 This is a closer photo of hundreds of large summer-run Steelhead Trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss) holding in Big Bend Pool on Steamboat Creek, a major tributary of the North Umpqua River. The fish are 2+ feet long and weigh 10-20 pounds (sometimes more). Look how the fish are pointing into the current from the right. The current brings cold water in from Big Bend Creek a bit upstream. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (Big Bend Pool, Steamboat Creek, North Umpqua River, 28 August 2013)

File.Name: d902c_d828-266.jpg
Notes:
9656855321 Look close in the water! Here are hundreds of summer-run Steelhead Trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss) holding in Big Bend Pool on Steamboat Creek, a major tributary of the North Umpqua River. The fish are 2+ feet long and weigh 10-20 pounds (sometimes more). Look how the fish are pointing into the current from the right. The current brings cold water in from Big Bend Creek a bit upstream. (Big Bend Pool, Steamboat Creek, North Umpqua River, 28 August 2013)

There are hundreds of summer-run fish in this cool water pool waiting for the first winter rains, when they will move into the upstream tributaries to finally spawn in February or March. The babies live in the river for two years, then swim downriver to the ocean for two more years, the swim back up their natal river to spawn. Steelhead don't always die after spawning, but only a few percent make it back to the ocean to do it all again.

This pool is an easy target for poaching. With salmon going for $10 and up a pound, you can see why - there's a ton or more of meat here, and a stick of dynamite makes for an easy harvest. Fortunately Lee Spencer is guarding the pool. See this photo. Lee has an excellent article on Summer Steelhead that goes into the life cycle.

File.Name: d902d_d828-275.jpg
Notes:
9656559381 This is Lee Spencer and his dog in their shelter above Big Bend Pool on Steamboat Creek, a major tributary of the North Umpqua River. For 17 years so far, 6 or 7 months each year, Lee has been the guardian of the Summer Steelhead trout in Big Bend Pool - see my [Next] few photos. In years past, poachers have dynamited this pool, but Lee now keeps watch every year from May until the fall rains come and the fish swim up into the smaller tributaries. (Big Bend Pool, Steamboat Creek, North Umpqua River, 28 August 2013)

Lee is sponsored (and I hope paid) by the North Umpqua Foundation which is a group worth supporting. A year by year collection of Lee's observation notes is available from the [Lee's Steelhead Notes] and [News from Big Bend Pool] buttons on the left edge of the web page. Lee is a genuine environmental hero, worth supporting.

File.Name: d902e_d822-219.jpg

My parents discovered this river back in the 1960's and bought property and built a cabin - which has come to us with their passing. It's a wonderful river and a diverse woodland. Every year we seem to spend more and more time up there. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 August 2013)

File.Name: d903a_d819-129.jpg
Notes:
9666395113 I guess this is a male Great Spangled Fritillary (Speyeria cybele) butterfly in the Oregon woods, though there are other large fritillaries that are candidates. I also saw the very different females in the woods, but I couldn't get a shot - but see this photo. It's working what I believe is a non-native Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) in the Asteraceae plant family. Weeds are still flowering, and that's where the butterflies are. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2013)

*Sigh* I was laid up with sciatica-type back and leg pains on most of this Oregon trip. I guess it's a pinched nerve, but it felt more like a choke hold! I got out with my camera most days in Oregon, but I didn't get far and couldn't bend very well - let alone chase butterflies. On the other hand, what a great place to be laid up. I am feeling better now - with butt pains from sitting so much!

9677115398 Here is a large Cicada (family Cicadidae in the old order Homoptera) insect on a winged stem of flowering Perennial Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. Julie says they were singing in Oregon, but I really can't hear them. Their chorus is the same pitch as my tinnitus - that constant ringing in my ears that comes from too many years of too-loud music. Be warned! (On the other hand, a constant chorus of cicadas isn't bad!)

This is a remarkable late-instar nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. This one is perched on a dusty leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It looks a bit like the photos of genus Pselliopus at BugGuide. See my [Previous] photo. It kind of looks like a Bush Katydid (Scudderia) nymph. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2013)

I'm only posting six photos tonight and for the next few days. I have a newsletter to produce in the next few days for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society, and Alfred Hitchcock's Vertigo is just starting on TCM. Life is full!

Notes:
9680316145 This is a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae. It's a female - you can clearly see her dingus-like epigynum at the screen-bottom of her abdomen. It is missing its left rear leg. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 13 August 2013)

It's interesting that I haven't seen any of these large spiders at home yet. I'm not surprised as there are few insects on the wing for them to capture in this drought summer. I hope they will still appear.

File.Name: d906a_d906-020.jpg
Notes:
9687801401 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is looking head-on at a different Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - probably the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus). You can see its mighty proboscis and the four spurs across the back of its thorax behind its head. I found this one today on yellow composite flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. See my [Previous] photo of the same insect. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2013)

I was hoping for butterflies by the river today, but I only saw a few. Maybe it was just too darn hot for them - it was 104∞F where I was at the river today! But insects are generally missing in the drought-weary woods this year. I'll be happy when we get some real rain, but that might not be for another month or three. It's a tough year for nature.

File.Name: d906c_d805-035.jpg
Notes: 9687574795 I believe this is the Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family - aka Habenaria unalascensis ssp. elata. It's an orchid, but it really looks more like an asparagus stalk when it flowers. They have large basal ovate leaves that wither away before the flowers appear. It's curious that we have this same orchid species at home - known as the Chaparral Orchid. You can see its long nectar spurs and (if you look close) the twisted ovaries that turn the flowers upside down, like all orchid flowers. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 August 2013)

File.Name: d906d_d805-002.jpg
Notes: 9687524235 These are new flowers of Rattlesnake Orchid (Goodyera oblongifolia) in the Orchidaceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods. They are the most common of the mid-summer orchids near our cabin by far, appearing after most other wildflowers. They have beautiful green leaves at the base - they are not saprophytes. I'll post a photo of the leaves in a day or three. The plants by our cabin were all done by the end of August, but they were still going higher in the mountains near Twin Lakes. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 August 2013)

File.Name: d906e_d821-186.jpg
Notes:
9690673448 These are the strange white plants of Indian Pipes (Monotropa uniflora). This oddball is now considered in the Ericaceae plant family, but it used to be in its own Monotropaceae plant family. The plants and flowers are pure white and do not contain chlorophyll like most plants. These stems are pushing up mushroom-like through rotten wood from a fallen tree. The plants near our cabin at a lower elevation are all done and quite black. (Lemolo Lake, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2013)

These plants seem to be completely parasitic on forest trees. Or more likely they are myco-heterotrophs which are dependent on mycorrhizal fungi that are parasitic on trees. They have a fungal connection with tree roots in a three-way relationship mediated by a mycorrhizal connection between flower-fungus-tree. We only see them at all because they have to flower - like all other seed-bearing plants.

Maybe the whole forest is connected by fungi? See the interesting speculations by Paul Stamets .

File.Name: d906f_d821-193.jpg
Notes: 9687358059 This is just a small piece of a tall flowering stalk of native Pinedrops (Pterospora andromedea) in the Ericaceae plant family. This a total saprophyte without chlorophyll, with a fungal connection to a different host plant. See my [Next] photo. It's the tallest saprophyte in the woods, 3-4 feet tall. These particular flowers are past their prime, on their way to becoming seed pods. (Lemolo Lake, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2013)

File.Name: d907a_d826-225.jpg
Notes:
9698164270 This is another native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the Oregon woods - maybe a Pacific Sideband (Monadena fidelis) snail? I found it in the woods after a good summer rain. I wonder what that little leg on the right edge of the photo is? I know, the photo isn't quite sharp - but it's as good as the low light and my bad back allowed. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 August 2013)

*Sigh* I spent the entire day working on the new Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society newsletter. I never got outside today, except for walks back and forth on our upstairs deck, but it's almost done! I have to check spelling and the column spacing, then send it out to the SYVNHS board for changes, then off to the printers.

Just these two snail photos today, but I hope I'll be back taking new photos tomorrow!

File.Name: d907b_d826-240.jpg
Notes:
9698094328 The great escape?

This is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the Oregon woods - maybe a Pacific Sideband (Monadena fidelis) snail? I found it after a good summer rain, and placed it on a mossy stump... and waited. After about 15 minutes, it decided to make a run for it, leaving a pile of snail-poo behind. I know, the photo isn't quite sharp - but it's as good as the low light and my bad back allowed. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 August 2013)

File.Name: d908a_d908-037.jpg
Notes:
9703964173 This is a pretty little Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly in the woods today - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). They are by far the most common butterflies in the mountains right now. This one is working a new summer flower of native composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family - now renamed as Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii). Despite drought and heat, the flowers continue! (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 September 2013)

I felt better today, and the SYVNHS newsletter is done! I enjoyed getting out today. Oregon photos can wait.

File.Name: d908b_d908-015.jpg
Notes: 9703711645 There's lots happening in this photo! The scene is a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Then there are the interesting galls of (I guess) the Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis mellifica) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on the stem. The galls make real honey on their surface. You can lick the galls and they taste sweet. The honey attracts insects and (usually) ants. Here is an interesting "True Bug" (Hemiptera, maybe family Largidae?) and a little gray Wasp (Hymenoptera) - with a different tiny wasp airborne in the lower right corner. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2013)

File.Name: d908d_d908-027.jpg
Notes: 9706666124 Here is a pretty new flower of late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a wonder that anything can flower in this drought year, but here it is. The long exserted stamens look like little serpent tongues! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 September 2013)

File.Name: d908e_d908-040.jpg
Notes: 9703342587 These are pretty new late summer flowers of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. Hooray, it's got it proper name back in Jepson, and I'm glad. (Just wait, and all will be made right!) I love the way it's name sounds - "cor-e-thro-gyn-e-fil-ag-in-i-fol-ia" - it kind of rolls off the tongue. It's a tough plant that flowers despite drought, sometimes into spring. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 September 2013)

File.Name: d908f_d908-047.jpg
Notes: 9703288525 This is a whole shrub of native Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a bit past it's prime, but still quite attractive with especially beautiful foliage. It's a large shrub, 6 or 7 feet tall. Late summer seems to be the time for yellow composites. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 September 2013)

File.Name: d909a_d909-008.jpg
Notes:
9714732790 This is a remarkable bee-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) which has become breakfast for a Crab Spider (Thomisidae). I guess the fly is genus Eristalis. It's such a remarkable bee-mimic that I didn't realize it was a fly until I get the photo up on my computer screen. If I realized what I had, I would have spent more time taking the photo! (But there's something about the eyes...) I found it on new male staminate pollen-bearing flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2013)

Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best! Oregon photos can wait another day. The Coyote Bush is just starting to flower, though flowers are scarce in the mountains. It's been a tough drought year for natives.

File.Name: d909b_d909-004.jpg
Notes:
9711362957 These are pretty new berries of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family on the edge of our garden. The tricolored berries start yellow, turn red, and finally become almost black when they are ripe, The birds favor them, especially the acrobatic Bushtits (Psaltriparus minimus) which we rarely see in the garden except when these berries are ripe. The berries have "water marks" from our hard well-water. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2013)

This plant has leaves that are glabrous underneath. Other shrubs have white fuzz or hairs underneath and have been referred to Rhamnus tomentosa. I see both kinds in our canyon.

File.Name: d909c_d909-014.jpg
Notes: 9711325021 There are still new flowers of Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in the summer-dry woods. The plants look mostly dead, but there are still a few green parts with these pretty blue flowers. The blue pollen is striking. This is one of the few late-summer flowering plants that has spring-like flowers. California Fuschia is another. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2013)

File.Name: d909d_d909-002.jpg
Notes:
9714459036 These are new flowers of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plants look wind-pollinated like their cousins in the genus Artemisia such as sagebrush, but they really do flower, and insects do visit the flowers. This was good timing. Usually the actual flowers are less obvious. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2013)

"Mug-wort" is the mug plant, and of course you drink beer from mugs. I've heard that mugwort is used to flavor Porter Beers - but maybe that's a different mugwort? This one has a sharp sage-like smell that is not unpleasant, but not what I want in my beer!

File.Name: d909e_d909-013.jpg
Notes:
9711187489 These are new flowers and cone-like seed heads of native composite Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. They are rather pretty when you catch them in flower, but the remarkable thing is when they flower - now, at the end of our hot dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2013)

It's interesting how so many of our late-season flowers are composites - especially yellow composites.

File.Name: d909f_d908-062.jpg
Notes: 9714379008 This is a whole plant with summer flowers of native composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family - now renamed Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii). It's growing with the ubiquitous weedy grasses. Despite drought and heat, it keeps making pretty flowers! (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 September 2013)

File.Name: d910a_d821-198.jpg
Notes:
9722085598 This is a remarkable "ping pong gall" of a Speckled Gall Wasp (Besbicus mirabilis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a leaf of Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana) in the Fagaceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods. It is the "nest" of tiny wasps, beautiful and strange - but also part of the oak tree and grown from oak tissue. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2013)

It was a crummy day for new photos, foggy and windy - and I had errands to do in town, including dropping off the new SYVNHS newsletter at the printers. It's a good day to post some Oregon photos from our recent trip.

File.Name: d910c_d821-179.jpg
Notes: 9718555849 These are interesting insect galls on leaves of what I guess is some kind of huckleberry or blueberry - genus Vaccinium in the Ericaceae plant family. I found them in the Oregon woods near Lemolo Lake at about 4,000 ft. elevation. There are many species of Vaccinium in Pacific Northwest, so I won't try to guess which one this is with those notched leaves. (Lemolo Lake, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2013)

File.Name: d910d_d819-119+d329-019.jpg
Notes:
9721612420 I was disturbed when I saw the Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae plant family turning brown from a leaf blight last March in the Oregon woods. They look better now in summer. This photo shows the same tree in March (left) and again in August (right). It looks fatal, but the trees do recover. Then again, it can't be doing them any good! Apparently this is a new blight, first noticed in 2009. Suspects include the fungi Mycosphaerella arbuticola and Phacidiopycnis washingtonensis, but it's my impression that the specific cause in still unknown. This is NOT caused by drought. We have madrones at home too, and I hope we didn't inadvertently bring any spores back! This most beautiful of trees should not look like it's dying! (Wright Creek Bridge, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 March 2013 and 19 August 2013)

Try a google search on madrone blight oregon for more info.
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Mycosphaerella arbuticola; Phacidiopycnis washingtonensis
associatedTaxa: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Mycosphaerella arbuticola; Phacidiopycnis washingtonensis

File.Name: d910e_d819-122.jpg
Notes: 9718318163 Here are new seeds and foliage of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family. It's fun to knock the seeds out of the trees in autumn and watch them twirl down like little helicopters. (Wright Creek Bridge, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2013)

File.Name: d910f_d815-108.jpg
Notes: 9718267853 This is the lovely foliage of native Vine Maple (Acer circinatum) in the Aceraceae plant family growing by our deck in the Oregon woods. The leaves look more like the maple leaf flag of Canada than the much larger Bigleaf Maples (Acer macrophyllum). They look and feel cool even when it's hot. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2013)

File.Name: d911a_d911-025.jpg
Notes:
9729177600 Well blow me down... (as Popeye used to say)

I found this new Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working the new staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see from the exposed bit of its upper wing why they are called Blues. I haven't seen one of these butterflies since July 19. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2013)

I'm still dealing with sciatica-type back and leg pains, but it is (very slowly) getting better. I'm still dealing with sciatica-type back and leg pains, but it is (very slowly) getting better. I have been getting out every day - hobbling with cane and tripod, but getting out.

File.Name: d911b_d911-012.jpg
Notes:
9729110962 This is a fully adult Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. I found it today on a dusty leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family about 10 feet away from the nymph in my [Previous] photo. It has the same red spots on its ankles, though it has lost the pretty black and white spots on its legs. I'm not sure what kind of reduviid it is. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2013)

Fall is the time for predators like these Assassin Bugs and spiders to pick off the pollinator insects, but in this drought summer the pollinators are already scarce because the flowers are scarce. We'll see what happens.

File.Name: d911c_d911-003.jpg
Notes: 9729006502 This is another late-instar nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. It has very short wings that will become larger in its final molt, and a remarkable proboscis folded under its head. This one is perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I'm not sure about its taxonomy, but I may have captured a photo of the adult in my [Next] photo. It's quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2013)

File.Name: d911d_d911-008.jpg
Notes: 9728943882 This is a different large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a non-native composite garden flower, maybe a small Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the Asteraceae plant family, in the garden this morning. The dramatic light is just right. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2013)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: d911e_d911-032.jpg
Notes: 9728910872 This is a fairly large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, with a neaby leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family below. It's abdomen looks empty, and I'm not surprised. All insects are scarce in the woods right now at the end of this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2013)

File.Name: d911f_d911-027.jpg
Notes: 9725653261 Here are new flowers of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) - in the Onagraceae plant family in the woods. They keep the hummingbirds going during late summer and autumn. Very beautiful, and surprising in our hot, dry summer. The white pollen grains are remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2013)

File.Name: d912a_d912-046.jpg
Notes:
9732793119 I believe this is an extraordinary Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae) in the order Hymenoptera - maybe genus Aporus. It's working new native composite flowers of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family a dry wash by the river. Both body and wings have that "so blue it's black" color. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2013)

Tarantula Hawks should also be working these flowers, but I haven't seen them since we got back from Oregon - or any large tarantula spiders.

File.Name: d912b_d912-055.jpg
Notes:
9735911882 This is an uncommon Paper Wasp (genus Polistes) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera - maybe Polistes dorsalis californicus. There are a number of these wasps with "desert colors" - all handsome. This one is working flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2013)

That's a little Bagrada Bug (Bagrada hilaris) out-of-focus on the left. See my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: d912c_d912-053.jpg
Notes:
9735820224 I believe this is a notorious Bagrada Bug (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera on a flower of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river. It has "bug eyes!" They look a lot like native Harlequin Bugs (Murgantia histrionica), but there are little differences. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2013)

These bugs were in the local news last year. Apparently they are native to northern Africa, and are now munching kale, Brussels sprouts, and broccoli in Lompoc. See this link and this. It's a new invasion, but they've been here for a while. If these bugs are really destructive pests as reported, I don't think there's a chance of containing them. They seem well established in the backcountry on native shrubs. I've seen them for at least two years - see my old photo (29 Aug 2011).

File.Name: d912d_d912-017.jpg
Notes: 9735710238 This is another beautiful California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly, still flying around through the woods. This one must be newly emerged with those fantastic blue colors on its forewings. (Compare this photo.) They are odd butterflies that never seem to visit flowers. I guess they emerge with a full belly and fly until they are empty. They don't feed, but they do come down to "puddle" on the damp soil in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2013)

File.Name: d912e_d912-054.jpg
Notes: 9735665352 I believe this is a Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new yellow composite flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. It was breezy, and it was difficult to get photos. The butterfly's upper wings are more interesting, but this is what I got. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2013)

File.Name: d912f_d912-039.jpg
Notes:
9735568586 This is another pretty little Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly in the woods today - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working new yellow composite flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2013)

These are by far the most common butterflies in the mountains right now, but I also got a good glimpse of a much rarer Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly today on similar flowers. I couldn't get a photo before it took off, but it's good to know they're out there. As Arnold says in the movie Terminator, "I'll be back!

File.Name: d913a_d912-048.jpg
Notes:
9736273823 These are new yellow composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry wash by the river. This shrub and no ray flowers and no leaves to speak of on the stems. It's one of my favorite late-summer shrubs that produce flowers when little else is. Butterflies and wasps like it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2013)

Today's photos are all yellow composite flowers that are flowering now, in the heat of late summer. These are flowers that keep the insects alive during the hardest part of the year. There are more, give me a few days.

File.Name: d913b_d912-041.jpg
Notes: 9736071771 Here are new yellow composite flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I think of this as a plant that grows near the ocean, but here it is in dry country. Maybe this one is really Isocoma acradenia? Anyway, here it is. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2013)

File.Name: d913c_d908-054.jpg
Notes: 9736071771 These are new yellow composite flower heads of Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. See this photo. The shrubs are leafy with slender leaves and no ray flowers. It only grows in the higher mountains. A Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) is working the flowers. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 September 2013)

File.Name: d913d_d908-066.jpg
Notes: 9736019731 These are new yellow flowers of native composite California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family - with Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterflies - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). Butterflies and wasps like these late-season yellow flowers. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 September 2013)

File.Name: d913e_d908-003.jpg
Notes:
9735899767 These are new yellow composite flowers of native Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a slender plant with long slender leaves and no ray flowers that favors marshy places, but here it is in a bone-dry ditch along East Camino Cielo Road. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2013)

Did you notice that tiny Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae, Hemiptera) in the upper left of my photo? They like these yellow composite flowers.

File.Name: d913f_d912-061.jpg
Notes:
9735824637 These are late-season composite flowers of native Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash along the river. My photos today are all yellow flowered composites. This one has fewer flowers than my [Next] few photos, but it does qualify. It has hairy stems and flowers with ray flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2013)

It's related to another native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) that is flowering now. That plant grows like a weed along roads, and it's surprising that it's native - but it is. I'll get a photo one of these days.
Locality: Heterotheca grandiflora) that is flowering now. That plant grows like a weed along roads
ScientificName: Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides; Asteraceae; Heterotheca grandiflora

File.Name: d914a_d914-026.jpg
Notes:
9753426092 This is a handsome Paper Wasp in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera getting honey from a gall of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. This is happening on a twig of on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2013)

I don't think this is our usual Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) at home because of the brown on its thorax. There are many large paper wasps, so I won't try to guess what this one is. There's also a little fly (order Diptera) on the stem below - maybe a Freeloader Fly (family Milichiidae)?

My camera has a [+/-] button which I use a lot to nudge a photo from the exposure it thinks is right. I push the button and rotate a little wheel with my thumb to change it. Damn, I rolled the wheel the wrong way again! This photo is overexposed and maybe over-corrected. I'm an old-time film photographer. I like to get the photo right from the start.
Locality:
family Milichiidae)?

File.Name: d914b_d914-032.jpg
Notes:
9753252732 These are interesting new galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The mushroom-like overlapping base of the gall distinguishes them from similar galls. They are growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2013)

These galls produce real "oak honey" on their surface - I've tasted them. I guess the galls produce honey to attract ants which will protect them from other wasp predators. It's curious that there are no ants visible in this photo. Maybe I scared them away putting my tripod in place?
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: d914c_d914-028.jpg
Notes: 9753304535 These are more interesting galls on leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe they are the galls of the Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras timaculosum aka Atrusca trimaculosa) in the Cynipidae family of the Hymenoptera. I found lots of them today. Unlike most galls, they seem to shrivel up the leaves that feed them. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2013)

File.Name: d914d_d914-031.jpg
Notes: 9753217673 This sure looks like an "oak apple" gall, but it's yellow and lumpy instead of red and smooth - maybe it's an "oak lemon"? It's an inch or more in diameter, on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I look close for new galls in late summer, and I don't believe I've ever seen this one before. There's nothing like it in my book "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (Ron Russo, UC Press, 2006). (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2013)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Field Guide to Plant Galls

File.Name: d914e_d914-021.jpg
Notes: 9752994643 This is a small diurnal (daytime) moth (Lepidoptera) on a spent plant of weedy non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. It's quite pretty and has long mouthparts like a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae). I usually find them in winter/spring, so this is a new one for me. See this photo and this one at Fotolog. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2013)

File.Name: d914f_d914-046.jpg
Notes:
9752764954 This is another Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working new yellow composite flowers of Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family - now renamed Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii). See my photos yesterday of yellow composites flowering now. It's a handsome butterfly on handsome flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2013)

This plant is growing near Knapp's Castle, the lowest elevation that I've found it. There used to be another plant here that died a couple of years ago, and this seedling has taken its place - not quite in the same place.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Bush Groundsel; Douglas' Groundsel
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Senecio douglasii; Asteraceae; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii

File.Name: d915a_d915-006.jpg
Notes:
9766614734 This is a different Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera sinking its fearsome proboscis into a fly (order Diptera). This action is happening on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's not easy to see, but I'm pretty sure the unfortunate fly is a Black Scavenger Fly or Ensign fly, genus Sepsis in the family Sepsidae. Its head is twisted sideways, which I've seen before. It's a wonder that these slow-moving insects can catch such a fast-moving prey, but they do. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2013)

Today's photos are all assassin bugs and lizards. Life is good!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; fly; Wild Blackberry; Black Scavenger Fly; Ensign fly
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Sepsis; Sepsidae

File.Name: d915b_d914-003.jpg
Notes: 9766534813 I'm pretty sure this is the same adult Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera as my [Previous] photo. This bug is perched on the same dusty leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I guess this one is newly emerged from its final molt into adulthood, and its wings haven't had time to harden yet - like a soft-shelled crab. I got this photo yesterday. Compare the two photos, and look at the differences in the wings. (San Marcos Pass, 14 September 2013)

File.Name: d915c_d915-002.jpg
Notes:
9766136772 This is a fully adult Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. I found it today on same dusty leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family as the critter in my [Next] photo, taken yesterday. I'm pretty sure they are the same insect, but this one has had time for its wings to harden. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2013)

They are striking insects, with a long predator-proboscis to suck prey. They look fast, but they are really *s*l*o*w* moving. This one hasn't moved since yesterday. It's a wonder that they capture any prey, but they do - see

File.Name: d915d_d912-007.jpg
Notes:
9765833882 This is the same little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) as my [Previous] two photos. It's posed on an interesting fossil that is part of the border of our small cactus garden. I'm pretty sure from the shape that it is a Mussel from times long past. I couldn't ask for a better pose. This lizard had a thing for fossils! (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2013)

The fossil is about 4 inches long, so I reckon the lizard is about 3 inches long. It's just an adolescent, born this year. The little ones have the advantage that they warm up quicker in the morning, so they get a head start on the adults when it comes to hunting.

Just after I got this photo, I noticed an interesting Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens) nearby. It flew away about 10 feet - right into the jaws of an adult lizard! Nature is tough for the flies, but good for the lizards!
Locality: Hermetia illucens) nearby. It flew away about 10 feet - right into the jaws of an adult lizard! Nature is tough for the flies
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Mussel; Black Soldier Fly

File.Name: d915e_d912-014.jpg
Notes:
9765846065 This is the same little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) as my [Previous] photo. It's perched on a whale bone fossil embedded in a rock of local Monterey Shale. You can see the matrix of the bone, turned by time into hard agate-like stone. This fossil rock is part of the border of our small cactus garden. I couldn't ask for a better pose! (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2013)

Before I caught the photo bug, I used to collect local fossils. I have 100s of them - the larger ones like this doing honest work in the garden. Sometime I'll get some photos. I'm no longer sure of locations, but I'm sure I found this one on the dry wash of the Santa Ynez River.

File.Name: d915f_d912-011.jpg
Notes: 9765550572 This is a perfect little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) on a fossil rock that is part of the border of our small cactus garden. This lizard is adolescent, part of this year's hatch. It's only about 3 inches long. See my [Next] photos. Hah - this lizard has a cactus spine stuck in its nose! It must be tough when you don't have hands to remove such a distraction! (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2013)

File.Name: d916a_d916-035.jpg
Notes:
9779867676 I found this sweet little Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) along Paradise Road on my way back from my photo shoot today. I guess it's a yearling doe. It has a wry smile - maybe thinking that the autumn equinox is just around the corner so we might get some rain again? I know, it's not quite sharp. I stopped my truck and reached for my camera, tripod still attached uselessly, and got the photo hand-held without checking camera settings. It's a sweet photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 September 2013)

I don't know how hunters can understand the damn hunting laws. They seem really obscure. As far as I can figure, hunting season is on for another week. This doe should be safe, but who knows?

File.Name: d916b_d916-038.jpg
Notes:
9779713624 This is yet another beautiful Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working new male staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2013)

Insects are scarce in this drought year, but I have seen a few other butterflies - a Western Tiger Swallowtail on the porch this afternoon, some kind of Lady on the Butterfly Bush in the garden, and an uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) by the river a few days ago. I'll keep looking.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Coyote Bush; Western Tiger Swallowtail; Lady; Fatal Metalmark
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Calephelis nemesis californica
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Calephelis nemesis californica

File.Name: d916c_d916-008.jpg
Notes: 9779690113 It's almost autumn, but here are still new flowers in our summer-dry woods. These are new yellow composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's "stump-sprouting" from a burned stem in a place that was burned last May by the "White Fire". It's nice to see some color in this sere landscape. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 September 2013)

File.Name: d916d_d915-013.jpg
Notes:
9779529713 These are tiny but pretty flowers of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains - with seeds and seedpods. The seeds develop under the flowers in an inferior ovary that splits open to release seeds. It's related to wetland plants, but this one picks the bone-dry late-summer soil in our mountains to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2013)

I'm intrigued by these late-summer flowers that pick the harshest time of the year to flower, especially in a drought year. These are survivors!

File.Name: d916e_d915-019.jpg
Notes: 9779075111 Here are a pair of composites in the Asteraceae plant family that grow as weeds in waste places and along mountain roads. On the left is Horseweed (Conyza canadensis), and on the right is Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora). The real surprise is that both plants are California natives! We do have native weeds that are adapted for disturbed areas, and it's good that they flower in late-summer when the insects need some help. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2013)

File.Name: d916f_d916-015.jpg
Notes: 9778928651 I found this toasted succulent in the Crassulaceae plant family on the "river trail" today, in the direct path of the "White Fire" that burned through here last May. I didn't have my pocket knife with me, so I put my watch in the photo for scale. The crystal is about 1-1/4 inches across, which makes the stem of this succulent pretty fat - arm or ankle size. I believe this is native Chalk Lettuce (Dudleya pulverulenta). I've never found it along the river before. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 September 2013)

File.Name: d917a_d917-016.jpg
Notes:
9793415653 This is a tiny Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) on spent flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. The flowers are already turning rusty brown as autumn approaches. I'm sorry to see it go. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2013)

This damselfly really is tiny, less than 2 inches long, like a flying needle. They fly like little cargo helicopters - or little fish! The air is so thick for these tiny insects that it's like swimming through water! It must be miles away from any creek water in this dry year, so it has come some distance to be here.

File.Name: d917b_d917-024.jpg
Notes: 9793232235 This is a very pale little "True Bug" in the family Pentatomidae in the old order Hemiptera. It's hiding in an old flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - see my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2013)

File.Name: d917c_d917-027.jpg
Notes:
9793106185 Here are odd new "flowers" of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - now moved to the Orobanchaceae. Yep, the white petals show that these are really flowers, and insects like them. You can see where insects have chewed holes trough the base of the flowers to get in. They are common, though you have to hunt for the white petals. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2013)

I love these tough late-season plants that pick our bone-dry late-summer to flower!

File.Name: d917d_d917-031.jpg
Notes:
9792901934 These are new composite flowers of native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's another of the late-season composite flowers that grows along the river and on rocky banks in the mountains. These late-season composite flowers keep the late-season insects alive. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2013)

I love these tough late-season plants that pick our bone-dry late-summer to flower!

File.Name: d917e_d917-033.jpg
Notes: 9792844843 This is a native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It still has the erect dreadlock-like male catkins, which distinguish it in the keys from the oaks with droopy catkins. The acorns are all gone now, though you can make out an undeveloped acorn if you look close. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2013)

File.Name: d917f_d917-011.jpg
Notes:
9792500363 This is a new weed in our mountains. I believe it is non-native Skeleton Weed (Chondrilla juncea) in the Asteraceae plant family. This photo shows flowers, seeds, and seed heads. I found this colony of them along East Camino Cielo Road in the mountains. I first found it last year, and it's still in the same place - not spreading as near as I can tell, but look at those seeds. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 September 2013)

This is considered a type "A" noxious weed in California - "A pest of known economic or environmental detriment and is either not known to be established in California or it is present in a limited distribution that allows for the possibility of eradication or successful containment." (CDFA Encycloweedia)

I alerted David Chang at the County of Santa Barbara's Agricultural Commissioner's Office about this infestation last year. He said it's known from Live Oak Campground and from the railroad tracks in Carpinteria, and this is a new location. He adds "So many weeds, so little time & money." I understand. The weeds are still here and making seeds a year later.
Locality:
CDFA Encycloweedia)

File.Name: d918a_d918-043.jpg
Notes:
9811076363 This fly has the iridescent green colors of a common Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera) in the genus Lucilia - trash flies - and maybe it is. But I found this one in the woods, working flowers of native native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I don't think it's a trash fly. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2013)

It sure looks like the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria) fly to me. See this photo and the photos at BugGuide. These fly are mostly blue, and have a yellowish beard - but it's hard to judge colors with these iridescent critters. I don't know about the beard.

If I'm right, this fly is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. The fly is native to North America. Its larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue. But it is the "secondary" screwworm that only feeds on already dead tissue. It actually has some medical uses. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And they have been used "in some cases as surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds. There's more info at Wikipedia.
Locality: Cochliomyia macellaria) fly to me. See this photo and the photos at BugGuide. These fly are mostly blue
CommonNames: Blow Fly; Scale Broom; Secondary Screwworm
