File.Name: d918b_d918-027.jpg
Notes:
9810847276 There are still new flowers on the native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family growing along the river. If you look close, you can see a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with prey and an interesting fly (Diptera) on the left flower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2013)

The pretty flowers are remarkable because they've been going since (at least) March! See this photo.

File.Name: d918c_d918-001.jpg
Notes:
9810575955 I found this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on some kind of pale non-native Aster (genus Aster, Asteraceae plant family) flower growing in a wet place in the garden. I think we got this plant from my Mom many years ago. It's a leggy plant that always flops over with stems 6+ feet long. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2013)

I got this photo in the garden at home. All the rest of my photos today are from Paradise, by the Santa Ynez River.

File.Name: d918d_d918-038.jpg
Notes: 9810471435 These Honey Bees (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) are working new female pistillate flowers of native Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. This shrub has willow-like leaves, but composite flowers. The bees are a good reminder that these really are flowers! Apparently the plant gets its charming common name because it provides good forage for livestock. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2013)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: d918e_d918-022.jpg
Notes:
9810341874 This is a new flower of Calabazilla aka Coyote Melon or Buffalo Gourd (Cucurbita foetidissima) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. You can see an incipient gourd at the base of the flower. The species name tells how it smells - it really does stink, and it's hard to get the smell off your hands. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2013)

The apple-size gourds were used by Chumash native Americans for soap, tools, and rattles. These native gourds are not common. This colony near Paradise Camp has been here as long as I can remember. I wonder if it dates back to the old days.

File.Name: d918f_d918-009.jpg
Notes:
9810041754 This is a new flower of magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family on a dry flat by the river. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. The flowers are very large, probably bigger than it appears on your monitor. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2013)

Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!

File.Name: d919a_d919-006.jpg
Notes:
9828709216 This is a beautiful Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) working the late-season flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden - captured at the moment that it spread its wings. Look how spotted it is! This is a "tele-macro" photo with my long lens, and then heavily cropped. My long lens is ailing after a few crashes, and needs help. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2013)

You'd think Monarchs would be common here, since tens of thousands of them overwinter a dozen miles away in trees by the coast... but they are actually rather scarce, and I only see the in the fall.

Tomorrow is a busy day. My daughter (in-law) is flying down from Portland, Oregon late tonight. And the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society is hosting a screening of Michael Love's new movie
Locality: in-law) is flying down from Portland

File.Name: d919c_d919-001.jpg
Notes:
9828141413 I'm believe this is a real Western Conenose Bug (Triatoma protracta) in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. I'm sorry to say that Julie found it on the couch in our house this morning. Yipes! (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2013)

Julie had it in a jar, so I dumped it out on the porch railing for a photo, expecting it to be slow moving like other Reduviids. Hah - it immediately took off trying to get away! I managed to corral it with my hand, but it still moved too fast for a photo. I was lucky to get this one. I put it in the refrigerator for a few hours to slow it down... and it took off again right away when we let it go and escaped over the edge of the porch.

Apparently, they live and feed in Dusky-footed Woodrat (Neotoma fuscipes) nests, but they can move also into houses. I'm not surprised - the woodrats have moved into our house too in the past!

It's a problem because the insect is a potential host of Chagas disease, caused by the parasite Trypanosoma cruzi. My book (Charles Hogue, The Insects of the Los Angeles Basin, 1974) says that "The Western Cone-Nose Bug often harbors a trypanosome parasite apparently identical to the one found in the disease. It may be a non-virulent strain."

But I've read that Chagas Disease has grown in Southern California because of increased immigration from Mexico and Central America, so the chances of infection must be greater. The CDC says that "Although there are triatomine bugs in the U.S., only rare vectorborne cases of Chagas disease have been documented."

Sounds good, but we'll keep them out of our bed!
CommonNames: Western Conenose Bug; Dusky-footed Woodrat; Chagas disease; The Insects of the Los Angeles Basin
ScientificName: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosoma cruzi
associatedTaxa: Triatoma protracta; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Neotoma fuscipes; Trypanosoma cruzi

File.Name: d919d_d919-016.jpg
Notes: 9827861194 This is a single native ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. This plant is a notorious weed in our mountains, but we always keep a few for the butterflies - and ants. It does flower in the late summer when insects need some help. We try to remember to keep the seed heads trimmed. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2013)

File.Name: d919e_d919-012.jpg
Notes: 9827777416 This is the late-instar nymph of a Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera on a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. We hear the adults singing every night, but I guess there are still a few late nymphs like this waiting to join the chorus. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2013)

Notes: 9827614434 This is a rather large Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for action on a spent flower head of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family, with leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family in the background. Those jaws...eyes...claws! The spiders know - these odd flowers DO attract insect pollinators. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2013)

File.Name: d920a_d918-050.jpg
Notes:
9857327446 Here is a wall of dried-out Cattails (family Typhaceae) growing along the mostly dry bed of the Santa Ynez River. There would normally be water in this place, but not in this drought year. I can't see a single flower/seed head in that mass of dry leaves. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2013)

No time to post new photos yesterday. My daughter (in law) is visiting and I had a meeting in the early evening. It was a crummy day for photos anyway. The marine layer fog spilled over the mountains, and it was gloomy and breezy all day.
Locality: in law) is visiting and I had a meeting in the early evening.  It was a crummy day for photos anyway.  The marine layer fog spilled over the mountains

File.Name: d920b_d918-047.jpg
Notes: 9857140866 This is the dry bed of the Santa Ynez River. There would normally be water in this place, but not in this drought year. That is a wall of Cattails (family Typhaceae) in the background. See my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2013)

File.Name: d921a_d921-036.jpg
Notes:
9865821594 I was trying to get a photo of this native Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) flower in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm sorry to say that I didn't notice the little caterpillar hanging (like the Hanged Man) right next to it until I got the photo on my computer. It was breezy. Not the best photo of the flower, but not a bad one of the caterpillar! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 September 2013)

Compare at my last few photos. These Stephanomeria virgata and S. exigua and S. cichoriacea flowers all share a family resemblance. All have only ray flowers, with striped pistils and beautiful stamens that looked like they've been dippped in sugar. The plants may look weedy, but the flowers are nice.

Today is the last day of summer. The autumnal equinox happens tomorrow afternoon at 1:44 pm PDT. It's fitting that we had our first winter storm move through today. No rain, but there were real clouds in the sky that were different from the usual fog. I hope it's an omen.

File.Name: d921b_d921-041.jpg
Notes:
9865550836 This is a small part of the inflorescence of a native Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family, with erect phyllaries. It has more petals and shorter peduncles than the Small Wirelettuce (S. exigua) in my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 September 2013)

You can see why this plant to so hard to photograph. The stems and flowers so widely spaced that's it's hard to get any kind of a natural grouping.

File.Name: d921c_d921-027.jpg
Notes: 9865150984 These are new flowers of native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a small plant, less than one foot tall - but it is rather pretty with those violet stamens and pistils. The flowers are almost identical to the Stephanomeria virgata in my [Next] two photos, but it has fewer petals and much longer peduncles aka flower stalks. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 September 2013)

File.Name: d921d_d921-025.jpg
Notes: 9864906036 Here are more pretty new flowers of the late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. Notice that the flowers are all ray flowers with pretty tips, and compare my next few photos. They all share a family resemblance. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 September 2013)

File.Name: d921e_d921-029.jpg
Notes: 9864586165 Here is a bit of the Santa Barbara backcountry, with dramatic lighting. This is looking up Oso Canyon, the site of the "White Fire" last May. (See this photo taken from the same place.) Across the top is (left-to-right) Mission Pine Ridge, then Little Pine Mountain, then Big Pine Mountain. The Santa Ynez River is at the bottom of my photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 September 2013)

File.Name: d921f_d916-010.jpg
Notes: 9864429165 Don't get me wrong from my [Previous] couple of photos. There is still water and green Cattails (family Typhaceae) growing along the Santa Ynez River. This pool is spring fed by a spring. You just have to know where to look! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 September 2013)

File.Name: d922a_d922-009.jpg
Notes:
9885597776 Here are the first new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. They are the first new flowers of any native plant I've seen in this rain year, so it's appropriate that I found them on the first day of autumn. We haven't had a bit of rain since I can't remember when, but here the are! Last year they kept flowering until March. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2013)

For 30 years, we've had one of these shrubs in our garden that always had the first new flower of the rain year. It finally died last spring, from too much summer water or just old age. I went hunting in the woods for a new early-flowering shrub, and found it!

The autumnal equinox happened today at 1:44 pm PDT. The path of the sun follows the ecliptic today. The path of the sun changes from 23.5 degrees above its path today in summer to 23.5 degrees below in winter. I'm already noticing the change. We lose the sun almost completely in our north-facing house, but that's when the mushrooms come out!

We had our first winter storm pass through last night. It didn't deliver a drop of rain, but it cleared the air and brought lots of wind blowing over the pass towards Santa Barbara. It was in the 80s in town today, but only in the 70s up here in the mountains. These "sundowner winds" are one time when it's warmer in town.

File.Name: d922b_d922-020.jpg
Notes: 9885360495 This is a seed head of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family. This is from one of the same wonderful flowers I show in this photo from last June. It's full of flat seeds, ready to spill out. It was quite windy when I got this photo, and I took many photos to get this one keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2013)

File.Name: d922c_d922-014.jpg
Notes: 9885153076 Here are new flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "taco plant" because of the folded leaves, like hard taco shells. The buds wait on the plant for months to flower, until next March or April. They show hope for the new rain year, which seems appropriate for the first day of autumn. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2013)

File.Name: d922d_d922-015.jpg
Notes:
9884755694 I guess this is a blue-legged Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes). It's a Short-Horned Grasshopper - family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera. This one is perched on a dead-looking twig of Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) in the Lamiaceae plant family. This photo looks like I used flash, but it's natural light, like almost all my photos. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2013)

Grasshoppers have been scarce in this drought year - or maybe it's just that I'm not so good at bending low lately because of my bad back.
CommonNames: Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper; Short-Horned Grasshopper; Pitcher Sage
ScientificName: Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae

File.Name: d922e_d922-006.jpg
Notes:
9884517063 Here are pretty new berries of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family on the edge of our garden. The tricolored berries start yellow, turn red, and finally become almost black when they are ripe. The birds like them dead ripe. I've seen Bushtits, Wrentits, Towhees, and even the newly arriving Warblers take the berries. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2013)

I've never actually tried one. This plant is related to native Cascara Sagrada (Rhamnus purshiana) which has a strong laxative effect. That's a good way to spread seeds though!

File.Name: d922f_d921-043.jpg
Notes: 9884268856 Here is one more photo of a pretty flower of native Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family. See my [Previous] two photos. The plant looks kind of weedy from a distance, but up close the pistils and stamens are gorgeous, like they've been dipped in sugar. It is taller, and has more petals and shorter stems than the very similar Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua). (Santa Ynez Mountains, 21 September 2013)
ScientificName: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria exigua
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria exigua

File.Name: d923a_d923-043.jpg
Notes:
9906805313 This is an interesting fly (Diptera) getting sweet honey from the gall of a Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. This is happening on a twig of on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I guess it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae with those bristles on its abdomen and paddle-like antennae, but I won't try to guess beyond that. There's another tinier fly to the right. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2013)

Today's photos are all interesting galls on oaks. It was a beautiful day, though breezy in the morning when I was out getting photos. The wind has since calmed, which bodes well for tomorrow.

File.Name: d923b_d923-015.jpg
Notes: 9906538224 I believe these are the galls of the Woollybear Gall Wasp (Sphaeroteras timaculosum aka Atrusca trimaculosa) in the Cynipidae family of the Hymenoptera growing on a leaf of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. There's a small parasitic wasp on the gall at the bottom of my photo thinking about the wasp larvae inside. It must be hard to lay eggs through such bristly hairs! (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2013)

Notes: 9906354855 These are galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These remarkable galls form on the undersides of the leaves. Most of the galls are turning dull brown by now, but these still have some color. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2013)

Notes: 9906032064 This is a real "oak apple" gall - though maybe "oak pear" would be a better name! It's the gall of a California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It's about the size of a golf ball. There are critters living in there! (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2013)

File.Name: d923e_d923-030.jpg
Notes:
9905940513 This is an odd "fuzzy gall" on leaves of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It sure looks like the gall of a Yellow Wig Gall Wasp (Andricus fullawayi) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but I usually find those on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) - a "white oak." Maybe the wasps cross over to this "intermediate oak" - or maybe it's something else? (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2013)

Remember - this gall is part of the oak shrub. It's also a wasp nest. The wasp induces the growth and there are wasp larvae inside, but it all comes from the oak.

Notes: 9905682516 I believe the swelling in this oak twig is the gall of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). It's growing on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. This gall doesn't seem to be interfering with leaf growth beyond. It has odd white bumps on the surface. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2013)

File.Name: d924a_d924-038.jpg
Notes:
9925876044 This is a tattered Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry wash by the river today. The wings are so worn that they are transparent. See this photo for a better idea of what they are. Tattered, but it was still a treat to see it. I usually find a few in the fall, but not every year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 September 2013)

It was a good day for Lepidoptera at the river today, and I still have that elusive Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly to capture!

File.Name: d924b_d924-046.jpg
Notes: 9925672394 This is a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry wash by the river today. These butterflies are usually pretty common, but they've been scarce in this drought year. This one kept turning on-edge to my camera. There's nothing to do about it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 September 2013)

File.Name: d924c_d924-032.jpg
Notes: 9925690873 This is a handsome Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry wash by the river today. These butterflies are usually common, but they've been scarce and camera-shy in this drought year. This specimen is rather small, so maybe it's a male. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 September 2013)

File.Name: d924d_d924-019.jpg
Notes:
9925323486 This is a handsome female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new yellow composite flowers of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). I mostly find these butterflies on these flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 September 2013)

(The wonderful short movie ZÈro de Conduite (Jean Vigo, 1933) is done. The scene with feathers in the air is ecstatic, as good as I remembered.)
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Goldenbush; Coastal Goldenbush; Desert Isocoma

File.Name: d924e_d924-042.jpg
Notes:
9924635864 This is another pretty Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry wash by the river today. These are by far the most common butterflies in the woods right now. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 September 2013)

This photo will have to stand in for the more interesting Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly that I missed today. I know they're out there. I'll keep trying.

(I'm going to take a time-out from posting to watch the wonderful movie ZÈro de Conduite made by the French director Jean Vigo in 1933, now showing on TCM. As a long-time private school teacher, this film resonates with me!)

File.Name: d924f_d924-003.jpg
Notes:
9924643973 This is a fairly large and hefty diurnal (daytime) moth (Lepidoptera) working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry wash by the river today. It has pretty green eyes. I don't know my moths very well, but I know one when I see it. It was odd to find this one working flowers on the day shift. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 September 2013)

Thanks Lynn - this may well be the Darker-spotted Straw Moth (Heliothis phloxiphaga) in the family Noctuidae. And it may be that I have a photograph of the caterpillar here on another composite. (See Jeffrey Caldwell's comment.)

File.Name: d925a_d925-021.jpg
Notes:
9942480074 This is an interesting Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae - interesting because it is straw-yellow, not green! Maybe it's some other kind of spider, but I'm sure the family is right. This one is perched on its egg case on a native shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2013)

Today started breezy, and worked into a pretty good sundowner wind event by the evening. It was too windy for photos today, but I got a few early in the day.

File.Name: d925b_d923-003.jpg
Notes:
9942310984 This is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for action in a little petal-formed cave on the flower of a non-native Aster (genus Aster, Asteraceae plant family) flower growing in a wet place in the garden. I think we got this plant from my Mom many years ago. It's a leggy plant that always flops over with stems 6+ feet long. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2013)

Note the tiny ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) in the flower head. I guess these spiders don't bother with ants.

File.Name: d925c_d925-004.jpg
Notes:
9941974515 This is an adult female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera on a thorny pad of "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia) cacti in the Cactaceae plant family. I believe the cactus is a Coastal Pricklypear (Opuntia littoralis), probably one we collected years ago in the Santa Monica Mountains. You can tell the cricket is a female by its narrow wings and ovipositor. The long antennae go on and on. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2013)

The males of these crickets sing at night. I have the notion that these brown crickets have a continuous trill, while the whiter "snowy" ones pulse with the temperature - but I don't know.

File.Name: d925d_d923-006.jpg
Notes: 9941873605 This is the beautiful late-instar nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. It's perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. This is the second hatch of these pretty bugs that I've seen in a few weeks. See this photo of the adults. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2013)

File.Name: d925e_d924-045.jpg
Notes:
9941737996 Here is another Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae, Hemiptera) - probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) - waiting for prey on a flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry wash by the river. These tiny predators have remarkable "ninja suits" of armor. I haven't noticed them for a while. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 September 2013)

This photo looks like I had Vaseline on my lens. There's something about having too much yellow in a photo that throws everything off.

Notes:
9941617626 The one that got away...

This is another pretty Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) taking wing just as I took the picture. File.Name: d926a_d926-006.jpg
Notes:
9973040116 This is a handsome male Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working new yellow composite flowers of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). I mostly find the butterflies on these flowers, probably because its host plant Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus, Malvaceae) grows nearby. See this photo. It curious that I have never found the butterflies on the mallow flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2013)

I had this photo and my two [Previous] all ready to upload when Flickr went down last night. Oh well, that sticky brown stuff happens.
CommonNames: Northern White-Skipper; Goldenbush; Coastal Goldenbush; Desert Isocoma; Chaparral Mallow
ScientificName: Heliopetes ericetorum; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae

File.Name: d926b_d926-021.jpg
Notes: 9973007043 I guess this is a Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. It's a handsome little fly with a short proboscis, so it has to stick its head right into the flower. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2013)

File.Name: d926c_d926-018.jpg
Notes: 9972931533 This is an interesting fly (Diptera) working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. I reckon it's a it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae with that bristly abdomen. I don't know anything else about it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2013)

File.Name: d926d_d926-007.jpg
Notes:
9958841146 These are new stump-sprouting shrubs of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. How quickly they come up and flower! I depend on this shrub for fall flowers that attract many insects, so I'm happy to see these stump-sprouts. Since these are just starting to flower, I reckon they'll be around for a while. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2013)

Well I tried to post my usual 6 photos last night, but Flickr went to a "panda screen" just as I entered this comment. No time to post them right now - I have new photos to get!

File.Name: d926e_d926-024.jpg
Notes:
9958625715 I believe this is native Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) in the Asteraceae. It's a leafy shrub that grows 5-8 feet tall with yellow composite flowers that have ray-flowers. The place where I always used to find this shrub was burned by the White Fire last May. It does NOT seem to stump-sprout! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2013)

This is another native shrub with yellow composite flowers. See this photo, and the photos [Previous] to that.

File.Name: d926f_d926-017.jpg
Notes: 9958636163 These are large clumps of Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite high in a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family by the river. This is near where I found the tattered Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly a few days ago, and mistletoe is the host plant for that butterfly. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2013)
CommonNames: Big-leaved Mistletoe; Western Sycamore; Great Purple Hairstreak
ScientificName: Phoradendron macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Atlides halesus
associatedTaxa: Phoradendron macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae; Atlides halesus

File.Name: d927a_d927-022.jpg
Notes:
9974280395 This is a stunning Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on the fading flowers of a native shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It must be newly emerged with those blue colors on its forewings - and even on the veins of its wings. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2013)

Today was a beautiful autumn day - not too hot, even a hint of chill in the air, and mild winds. Now when does the rain start?

File.Name: d927b_d927-003.jpg
Notes:
9974173543 This is a still-flowering native perennial Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. There's a tiny Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) on the right, and a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on the left - both on the wing. This is a native plant in our garden that gets regular summer watering and is doing well. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2013)

I know that California natives shouldn't get summer water, but this one seems to be an exception. It's thriving!
CommonNames: Branching Phacelia; Bee Fly; Wild Bee
ScientificName: Phacelia ramosissima; latifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: d927c_d927-007.jpg
Notes:
9973800694 This is the same adult female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera that I showed a couple of days ago in this photo. It didn't move for two days, so I figured something was wrong.

This cricket has been impaled by the the small hair-like thorns of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia) cacti in the Cactaceae plant family. It's quite dead now. You can see some black discoloring on its abdomen, and I suspect that oddly-shaped spot on the cactus pad is caused by its leaking body fluids. I've been stricken by these tiny thorns myself. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2013)

This cricket isn't the first victim of these cactus spines. See this photo (from Fotolog). I believe the cactus is a Coastal Pricklypear (Opuntia littoralis), probably one we collected years ago in the Santa Monica Mountains.
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Prickly Pear; Coastal Pricklypear
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Opuntia littoralis
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Opuntia; Cactaceae; Opuntia littoralis

File.Name: d927d_d927-012.jpg
Notes:
9973592864 Look close! This is an adult male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera on a ripening fruit of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia) cacti in the Cactaceae plant family. They like to "hide" face down until they come out at night to sing. Only the males with broad wings like this one sing. Compare this photo of a female, and see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2013)

This is a native cactus, maybe one we collected in Baja many years ago? See this photo of our garden. This is one of the cacti on the left with pink flowers.

File.Name: d927e_d927-017.jpg
Notes:
9973402664 This is the same interesting Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae that I showed a couple of days ago. It's perched on its egg case on a native shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's interesting because it is straw-yellow, not green, though I can see a bit of green on its abdomen. If you compare the images at BugGuide, you will see that this happens. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2013)

For some reason, I've had 1800+ hits on that previous photo. Go figure!
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: d927f_d926-002.jpg
Notes:
9973097215 This sure looks like the Carmel Valley Malacothrix (Malacothrix saxatilis var. arachnoidea), but I'm not sure what it's doing here in Santa Barbara County. It looks like the common Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia except that the leaves are white-tomentose. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2013)

I found this plant last year too. There are several records of this variety in Santa Barbara County, including a collection by Clifton Smith on the "S face of little Pine Mtn" which may well be the same place I found it.
ScientificName: Malacothrix saxatilis; arachnoidea; Malacothrix saxatilis; tenuifolia
associatedTaxa: Malacothrix saxatilis; arachnoidea; Malacothrix saxatilis; tenuifolia

File.Name: d928a_d928-042.jpg
Notes:
9989708714 This is just what I've been dreading in this drought year. It's a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family showing signs of extreme water stress. Well it's not gophers! Note that the trees on either side look better, though they also have some dead needles. If you look very close you can see a few of the large cones hanging from this dying tree. The oaks look better. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2013)

Santa Barbara and San Luis Obispo counties have just been elevated to "D3 ... extreme drought" condition at the U.S. Drought Monitor webpage. The National Weather Service has a good report, thought I don't know how long the link will remain active.

It's almost October, when we often get our first real rain after months without. I hope it happens this year.

File.Name: d928b_d928-003.jpg
Notes: 9989607014 Here are pretty late-season flowers of Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. The new flowers are white, but they develop these pretty rose colors as they mature. This photos show just how they grow - in tangles. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 September 2013)

File.Name: d928c_d928-008.jpg
Notes: 9989425155 There are still a few new flowers of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains. Here is one next to a seed head. They have been making flowers since June. These summer flowering plants are tough! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 September 2013)

File.Name: d928d_d928-017.jpg
Notes: 9989324464 I suspect this is another blue-legged Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes). It's a Short-Horned Grasshopper - family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera. This one is posed above new flowers of Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka Senecio douglasii). Grasshoppers have been scarce in this drought year. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 September 2013)
CommonNames: Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper; Short-Horned Grasshopper; Douglas' Groundsel
ScientificName: Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Senecio douglasii
associatedTaxa: Melanoplus cinereus; cyanipes; Acrididae; Orthoptera; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Senecio douglasii

File.Name: d928e_d928-023.jpg
Notes: 9989243403 These are pretty new yellow composite flowers of Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family - now renamed Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii). There's a handsome little Behr's Metalmark (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) butterfly on the flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 September 2013)
CommonNames: Bush Groundsel; Douglas' Groundsel; Behr's Metalmark
ScientificName: Senecio douglasii; Asteraceae; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti
