File.Name: d928f_d928-037.jpg
Notes: 9989145353 These are mostly-spent yellow composite flower heads of Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. The shrubs are leafy with green leaves and no ray flowers. It only grows in the higher mountains and is just about done for the year, though this specimen still has some real flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 September 2013)

File.Name: d929a_d929-004.jpg
Notes:
10007063364 This is a fully adult Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. It's sprawled across a leaf of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. We have both nymphs and adults in the woods right now. In fact we seem to have more predators than prey! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2013)

Just two photos today. Julie and I are soon heading out to beautiful Hollister Ranch for a mini-reunion of former teachers at Dunn Middle school. And maybe I'll get a few photos!

File.Name: d929b_d929-008.jpg
Notes: 10007043246 This is a handsome little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for action on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2013)

File.Name: d930a_d929-019.jpg
Notes:
10029829796 Here are interesting new flowers and seedpods of native Bladderpod (Peritoma arborea var. globosa) in the Cleomaceae plant family, formally Isomeris arborea, Capparaceae. (Little Drake's, Hollister Ranch, Santa Barbara County, 29 September 2013)

It's especially interesting because of where I found it - near the beach cabaÒa at Hollister Ranch near Point Conception. I expect to find this ill-smelling native shrub at Point Mugu and Joshua Tree in the Mojave Desert, but this was a surprise. There are a couple of collection records from Santa Barbara County, but mostly from Santa Rosa Island. It's an interesting native with an interesting distribution!
ScientificName: Peritoma arborea; globosa; Cleomaceae; Isomeris arborea; Capparaceae
associatedTaxa: Peritoma arborea; globosa; Cleomaceae; Isomeris arborea; Capparaceae

File.Name: d930b_d929-028.jpg
Notes:
10029658843 I got this photo at beautiful Hollister Ranch last evening as the sun was going down. I love the layers of color in the ocean, especially the kelp beds offshore. I wanted to keep shooting photos as the sun went lower and lower, but the group was ready to leave. (Little Drake's, Hollister Ranch, Santa Barbara County, 29 September 2013)

The cars on the beach are something you just have to accept on Hollister Ranch. The locals are very protective of that.

File.Name: d930c_d929-023.jpg
Notes:
10028829044 I don't usually post "people photos" or photos this large, but these are all good friends at our mini-reunion of former teachers from Dunn Middle School meeting at beautiful Hollister Ranch last evening. I'll make an exception for these good friends! From left to right, here is me, Genevieve, Amelie, Jacob, Orin, Doug, Linda, Julie, Monte, Cynthia, Donna, and Jonathon. (Little Drake's, Hollister Ranch, Santa Barbara County, 29 September 2013)

Members of this group were the teachers at DMS in the previous decade - Linda (Director), Cynthia (Language Arts), Genevieve (Social Studies), Donna (Math), Julie (Electives), and me (Science). We made a good team. I was the science teacher at DMS for almost 30 years, until I finally retired a few years ago. I take some pride that I made my own program and never used a textbook. Jacob was my former student!
Locality: Science).  We made a good team.  I was the science teacher at DMS for almost 30 years

File.Name: d930d_d930-003.jpg
Notes: 10028610985 I guess these are little insect or spider egg cases on a different columnar cactus (Cactaceae) in our garden. They look like little bicycle tire patches, with a blister in the center. I noticed them last year too, and never did see what comes out of them. Whatever it is, it doesn't emerge until spring. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2013)

File.Name: d930e_d929-011.jpg
Notes: 10028481353 This is another adult female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera "hiding" face down on the ripening fruit of a "Prickly Pear" (Opuntia) cacti in the Cactaceae plant family. Only the males with broader wings sing at night. This is a safe hiding place, but be careful of those deadly small bristles! See this photo, and also this photo of a male. They like these cactus fruit! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2013)

File.Name: d930f_d930-001.jpg
Notes:
10028177334 Hot stuff in the garden!

These are new HabaÒero Chilis (maybe Capsicum chinense, Solanaceae) in our garden. They are seriously hot! They are also seriously beautiful, and Julie found our first brilliant red ripe one a few days ago. Despite the drought, these chilis are having a good year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2013)

File.Name: da01a_da01-005.jpg
Notes:
10046794103 I believe this is a little black Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera. Looking at its pollen-dusted face, I can guess where it's been! I got this photo on flower heads of yellow composite flowers of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)

I was expecting winds today, but it wasn't too bad. There are major winds in the forecast for Thursday night, but I don't know that I believe these forecasts days in advance.
CommonNames: Blister Beetle; Goldenbush; Coastal Goldenbush; Desert Isocoma
ScientificName: Meloidae; Coleoptera; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia
associatedTaxa: Meloidae; Coleoptera; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia

File.Name: da01b_da01-003.jpg
Notes:
10046585795 This is a new seed pod of magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. It's still shedding the brown skin of its old flowers. The leaves smell bad, but the seed pods are wonderful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)

Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!

File.Name: da01c_da01-039.jpg
Notes:
10046505643 This is a new unwinding flower of magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. See my [Previous] photo. These are probably "photo clichÈs" by now, but it is still beautiful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)

Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!

File.Name: da01d_da01-034.jpg
Notes:
10046435753 Here are remarkable new flowers of magical Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. The flowers are very large, probably bigger than it appears on your monitor. You can see the interesting new flower buds below the open flowers. See my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)

Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!

File.Name: da01e_da01-021.jpg
Notes: 10046201854 These are ripening red berries of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. It's curious to find them now, since I also found new berries back in June. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)

File.Name: da01f_da01-015.jpg
Notes: 10046054544 Well, I'll be darned. These are new glandular flowers of native California Four O'Clock aka Wishbone Bush (Mirabilis californica in the Nyctaginaceae plant family. The flowers normally open in the afternoon (around 4:00 pm?) and close in the morning. I was there around noon, which is why the flowers are shut down for business. The wonder is that they're here at all at the end of this drought year! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)

File.Name: da02a_da02-024.jpg
Notes:
10061657706 Here are a mating pair of "True Bugs" in the old order Hemiptera. Maybe in the family Lygaeidae? They look like the Redcoat Seed Bug (Melanopleurus belfragei) at BugGuide. (Thanks Susan!) They are having their nuptials on a late-season staminate flowering plant of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. Their "private parts" look complicated! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 October 2013)

It was a crummy day for new photos today, but I got a few. It was cool and gloomy, with the "fog winds" that happen at the upper layer of the temperature inversion. The forecast is for Sundowner/Santa Ana winds tomorrow evening, but it doesn't seem likely right now.
CommonNames: True Bugs; Redcoat Seed Bug; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Lygaeidae; Melanopleurus belfragei; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Lygaeidae; Melanopleurus belfragei; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae

File.Name: da02b_da02-012.jpg
Notes:
10061400384 These are honey-producing galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Look close to see the tiny ants (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera) that are tending the galls. I reckon the galls produce honey to attract the ants which will protect the larvae inside from other predatory wasps. Nature is complicated like that. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2013)

Yeah, this photo is not quite sharp - none of my photos today is. The marine layer clouds blew over the mountains this morning. It was cool and gloomy, with the "fog winds" that happen at the upper layer of the temperature inversion. I did the best I could, but I wish I had kicked up the ISO another notch.
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; ants
ScientificName: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: da02c_da02-014.jpg
Notes: 10061252825 I believe this is an isolated gall of a Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Usually these galls grow together and form a mass, but sometimes I find a singleton like this. Compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da02d_da02-009.jpg
Notes: 10061212496 These are mature "oak apple" galls of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks like some critter - maybe a bird? - tried to dig into the galls to get the wasp larvae inside. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da02e_da02-028.jpg
Notes: 10061094365 There are still new composite flowers of native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's another of the late-season composite flowers that grows on rocky banks in the mountains and along the river. The flowers look like they belong in the ocean. These late-season composite flowers keep the late-season insects alive. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 October 2013)

File.Name: da02f_da02-015.jpg
Notes: 10060906855 This is a seed head from last spring of native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primulaceae plant family. Beautiful flowers, beautiful seed heads! The seeds are mostly gone, but notice that the little pod in the lower right is still wearing its cap. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2013)

File.Name: da03a_da03-025.jpg
Notes:
10077701283 This is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for action on a new pistillate (female) flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. For some reason, this one particular shrub was full of crab spiders - at least eight! See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2013)

No particular winds yet, but the National Weather Service is still saying that Santa Ana/Sundowner winds are coming later tonight until Sunday. We're under a Red Flag Warning because of wind and low humidity. The NWS said tonight, in all caps as usual, but it looks like shouting:

"A STRONG SANTA ANA WIND EVENT WILL DEVELOP OVER THE AREA TONIGHT THROUGH FRIDAY MIDDAY THEN WEAKEN SATURDAY AFTERNOON THROUGH SUNDAY...BRINGING THE MOST SIGNIFICANT FIRE WEATHER THREAT IN THE PAST 5 YEARS."

Yipes!

File.Name: da03b_da03-021.jpg
Notes: 10077459585 This is another handsome little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for action on a new pistillate (female) flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I think I like this photo best of my three spider photos today because it shows the flower almost in focus. The "toenails" on its left forelegs are also pretty neat. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2013)

File.Name: da03c_da03-023.jpg
Notes: 10077420326 This is a handsome little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for action on a new pistillate (female) flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. For some reason, this one particular shrub was full of crab spiders - at least eight! See my [Next] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2013)

File.Name: da03d_da03-039.jpg
Notes: 10077324795 Not all of our late-summer composite flowers are yellow or white. These are pretty new flowers of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. Hooray, it's got it proper name back in Jepson, and I'm glad. (Just wait, and all will be made right!) I love the way it's name sounds - "cor-e-thro-gyn-e-fil-ag-in-i-fol-ia" - it kind of rolls off the tongue. It's a tough plant that flowers despite drought, sometimes into spring. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2013)

File.Name: da03e_da03-013.jpg
Notes: 10077149475 Here are pretty new flowers of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) - in the Onagraceae plant family in the canyon. They keep the hummingbirds going during late summer and autumn. Very beautiful, and surprising after a hot dry summer. They are not enjoying this drought year, but there are a few. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2013)

File.Name: da03f_da03-054.jpg
Notes: 10077012424 This is a pretty flower of native Scarlet Monkeyflower (Mimulus cardinalis) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family growing with some kind of native Horsetails (genus Equisetum) in the Equisetaceae plant family. It's growing in our "water garden" - just an old fiberglass tub filled with water and plants, not to mention frogs and worms. I can't remember where we collected it, probably a rescue from a slide along some creek. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2013)
ScientificName: Mimulus cardinalis; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Equisetum; Equisetaceae
associatedTaxa: Mimulus cardinalis; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Equisetum; Equisetaceae

File.Name: da04a_da03-044.jpg
Notes:
10091989404 Here are photos of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a "dioecious" shrub with separate male and female plants. That's the staminate male pollen-bearing plant on the left, and the female pistillate seed-bearing plant on the right. The males have a distinctly yellowish cast, and the females are distinctly whitish. You can recognize males and females at glance when you see them driving by. I depend on these shrubs for late-season insect photos. These are "weedy shrubs" that thrive on disturbed ground, though I also find in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2013)

Today's photos are all weeds in the sense that they grow in disturbed places, but they are not weeds in the sense of "plants that grow where they shouldn't". This is just where they should grow.

The forecast was for big Santa Ana/Sundowner winds today, but it didn't happen. I'm glad. I went to the river today in the national forest, and river access was "Closed Due To Government Shutdown". Damn - this is getting personal!

File.Name: da04b_da04-014.jpg
Notes:
10091975923 These are new "flowers" of native Cocklebur (Xanthium strumarium) in the Asteraceae plant family. The male staminate flowers form on top and the bristly female flowers form below. They are hardly recognizable as flowers, but they are. You can make out the purplish stigma of the female flower in the upper-left, with receptive hairs. It more typically grows along the river, but I found these along East Camino Cielo road in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2013)

These plants are native, but they grow like weeds in disturbed places like this. We've all had to pick the seedheads from our socks and shoe laces. It`s been called the original inspiration for velcro - or is that Burdock Thistle (genusArctium, family Asteraceae)?
ScientificName: Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae; velcro; Arctium; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Xanthium strumarium; Asteraceae; velcro; Arctium; Asteraceae

File.Name: da04c_da04-009.jpg
Notes:
10091645955 These are new flower stalks of native Horseweed (Conyza canadensis aka Erigeron canadensis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I added a close shot of a flower in the upper-right. It grows in waste places and along roads in the mountains. It's another native that gets weedy. The new plants with leafs are rather attractive. Every year a few of these sneak through our weed patrols in the garden! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2013)

If you look close, you can see a few tall native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) plants with flowers in the Onagraceae plant family growing with them. These late-season natives in a drought year are amazing!
ScientificName: Conyza canadensis; Erigeron canadensis; Asteraceae; Epilobium brachycarpum; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Conyza canadensis; Erigeron canadensis; Asteraceae; Epilobium brachycarpum; Onagraceae

File.Name: da04d_da04-005.jpg
Notes: 10091467585 This is a new flower and seed head of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a tall, skinny plant that grows as a weed in disturbed places. It's another native plant that gets weedy. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2013)

File.Name: da04e_da01-030.jpg
Notes: 10091441313 These are new "flowers" of native Western Ragweed (Ambrosia psilostachya) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a composite related to sunflowers, though you have to study the flowers under magnification to see the relationship. I also find it along the roads in the mountains near home. It's another native that gets weedy. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)

File.Name: da04f_da01-025.jpg
Notes: 10091305606 This is native Dove Weed aka Turkey Mullein (Eremocarpus setigerus aka Croton setiger) in the locally uncommon Euphorbiaceae plant family, with flowers. This is nobody's favorite wildflower, but it is a late-season native, and here it is growing on on a sandy flat by the river. It's a weed, but a native weed - like all of today's photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)

File.Name: da05a_da05-003.jpg
Notes:
10108362286 This is yet another Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) in the woods. This one is working a still-flowering native composite Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. Butterflies are scarce in our autumn woods, and these are the ones I find. I'm glad there are interesting galls to fill the gap between summer insects and winter mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)

This is day 2 of our Red Flag Warning fire weather, but the expected Santa Ana/Sundowner winds haven't happened yet. It's been just normally breezy in the afternoon. I'm glad.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Sawtooth Goldenbush
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: da05b_da05-027.jpg
Notes: 10108235835 This is a remarkable gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They look like little mushrooms growing from the oak leaf - I can even see rounded "gills", like a Chanterelle! They are really the "nests" of tiny wasps - made by the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da05c_da05-030.jpg
Notes: 10108028485 Here are a pair of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family that have been galled by the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. For some reason, most of these wasp galls form on the undersides of the leaves. These galls are quite extraordinary. They form as individual capsules, but they grow together to make a mass. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: da05d_da05-007.jpg
Notes: 10107791784 This is an odd "fuzzy gall" on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It has longer hairs than the gall of a Woollybear Gall Wasp, and it's growing on a leaf rather than a stem, so it's not the gall of a Fuzzy Gall Wasp or a Round Gall Wasp. I'm not sure what it is. I'm not sure what it is, but I have no doubt it's the gall of a Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Woollybear Gall Wasp; Fuzzy Gall Wasp; Round Gall Wasp; Gall Wasp

File.Name: da05e_da05-019.jpg
Notes: 10107658065 These are new flower buds of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. The leaves are furry-tomentose underneath. It's good to see these early-flowering shrubs acting normal after two drought years. Last year they were flowering in December. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)

File.Name: da05f_da04-001.jpg
Notes: 10107592993 This is the announcement that's been posted at all campgrounds, picnic areas, and river access points at Paradise on the Santa Ynez River. Access to the upper river beyond the first crossing has been closed. I depend on this place for autumn photos. Damn - this foolishness is getting personal! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 October 2013)

File.Name: da06a_da06-030.jpg
Notes:
10129223574 This sure looks like a different Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly than the usual Behr's Metalmark (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) I've been showing lately. (See this photo taken yesterday.) Maybe it's just local variation, but maybe it's Apodemia mormo ssp. mormo or some other subspecies? (See this page at BugGuide. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 October 2013)

It sure looks different, but it was working the same flowers of Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka Senecio douglasii) as other normal looking Behr's Metalmark (Apodemia virgulti). Is it really a separate population? So many butterflies, so little time!

Today is the last day of our Red Flag Warning fire weather, but the expected Santa Ana/Sundowner winds still haven't happened. Hah - there's a 30% chance of rain on Wednesday. This will be our first rain since May. Bring it on!
Locality: Apodemia virgulti). Is it really a separate population? So many butterflies
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Douglas' Groundsel; Behr's Metalmark
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Apodemia mormo; mormo; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Senecio douglasii; Apodemia virgulti
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Apodemia mormo; mormo; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Senecio douglasii; Apodemia virgulti

File.Name: da06b_da06-022.jpg
Notes: 10129113585 These are ripe seeds of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family. It's great fun to whack the seed clusters with a walking stick and watch them "helicopter" down to earth. Note that there are few leaves in the trees behind. This is one of my favorite "fall color" trees, but there is little color in this drought year, at least on this tree in the open. The leaves are just turning brown and dropping, and I hope the trees are still alive. Other trees in the deeper canyons are just starting to show real fall colors. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 October 2013)

File.Name: da06c_da05-015.jpg
Notes: 10128864315 This is a ripening fruit of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. They sure look like little avocados, in the same family! The fruit taste very bitter unless they are dead ripe, but the nuts are delicious, almost like chocolate when they are prepared right. See this YouTube video. The trees are not producing many fruit in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)

File.Name: da06d_da05-049.jpg
Notes: 10128748146 This is a new cone on a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family, with another in the background. It's a three-needle pine. The huge cones can weigh as much as 10 pounds, according to Wikipedia. I don't recommend camping under one of these trees! (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)

File.Name: da06e_da05-044.jpg
Notes: 10128425606 This is white-stemmed Coastal Sagebrush (Artemisia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's another one of those summer composites with flowers that don`t look like flowers. Here it is sandwiched between California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) in front, and native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus, Rhamnaceae) in the background. I like the colors of this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)
CommonNames: Coastal Sagebrush; California Buckwheat; Greenbark Ceanothus
ScientificName: Artemisia californica; Asteraceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Artemisia californica; Asteraceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: da06f_da05-037.jpg
Notes: 10128353313 This is pretty foliage of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family, showing its fall colors. Many plants have already lost their leaves without much show, but this plant hung on to the end. I don't get poison oak, but even people who do must appreciate the beauty. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)

File.Name: da07a_da07-009.jpg
Notes:
10147653313 This is another photo of a impressive bristly Spiny Tachina Fly - Paradejeania rutiloides ssp. nigrescens, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. It's quite large for a fly, almost the size of a small bumblebee. I reckon my [Previous] photo shows the fly better, but this one is more dramatic. I like how its front legs are casually crossed! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2013)

It seems like the ancient "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the equally bristly "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae. I first noticed this one "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden. Maybe it's hunting for caterpillars? It flew off to this nearby leaf of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii,Scrophulariaceae). I usually see them working pistillate (female) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family, a place that is difficult to photograph. This was a lucky encounter.

Today was another beautiful day in paradise - not too breezy or too hot. The chance for a bit of rain on Wednesday has been bumped up to 40%. We need it!
CommonNames: Spiny Tachina Fly; Butterfly Bush; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; nigrescens; Tachinidae; Diptera; Arctiidae; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Paradejeania rutiloides; nigrescens; Tachinidae; Diptera; Arctiidae; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: da07b_da07-007.jpg
Notes:
10147286215 This is an impressive bristly Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. I believe it is ssp. nigrescens due to "black 5th tergite and the black median vitta on the anterior tergites" - meaning it has a black butt. It's quite large for a fly, almost the size of a small bumblebee. It seems like the ancient "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the equally bristly "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2013)

I first noticed this one "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden. It flew off to this nearby leaf of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii,Scrophulariaceae). I usually see them working pistillate (female) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family, a place that is difficult to photograph. This was a lucky encounter.
CommonNames: Spiny Tachina Fly; Butterfly Bush; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; nigrescens; Arctiidae; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; nigrescens; Arctiidae; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: da07c_da07-016.jpg
Notes:
10146881404 I believe this is a small atypical Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera, maybe genus Cylindromyia. You can see it's bristly abdomen. It's working new pistillate (female) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. These flies are quite camera shy. This is a 800x800 crop from my original 4800x3200 photo, as close as I could get. I'm glad my camera has good pixels! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2013)

Adult Tachinid Flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize!
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Cylindromyia; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: da07d_da07-004.jpg
Notes: 10146773406 This is a small Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, order Diptera) "puddling" - taking moisture - on damp soil in our garden today. These little flies look almost black when you see them, but they are quite brown up close like this. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2013)

File.Name: da07e_da07-014.jpg
Notes: 10146422314 Here is another yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on new pistillate (female) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It can sit perched like this for days if need be and snap shut like a mousetrap when a little fly or bee lands. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2013)

File.Name: da07f_da07-020.jpg
Notes: 10146373485 There are *still* new flowers of Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in our summer-dry woods. The plants look mostly dead, but there are still a few green parts with these pretty blue flowers. The blue pollen is striking. This is one of the few late-summer flowering plants that has spring-like flowers. The flowers are tiny, and the plant is not one you notice unless you hunt for it. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2013)

File.Name: da08a_da08-002.jpg
Notes:
10163319116 Hot stuff in the garden! These are ripening fruit of HabaÒero Chilis (maybe Capsicum chinense, Solanaceae) in our garden. They are seriously picante! They are also seriously beautiful. They seem to glow with an inner light of their own. Of course some backlight helps! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2013)

It was cool and breezy today. There is still a 40% chance of real rain tomorrow, but it doesn't sound like it will amount to much.

File.Name: da08b_da08-013.jpg
Notes: 10163079204 Here are new flowers of a native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. They are the first new flowers of any native plant I've seen in this rain year. We haven't had a bit of rain since last May, but here the are! Last year they kept flowering until March. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2013)

File.Name: da08c_da08-018.jpg
Notes: 10163057744 This is another surprisingly beautiful grape-sized gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It is growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It might be our most beautiful gall. They've been kind of scarce in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2013)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: da08d_da08-016.jpg
Notes:
10163199813 Here is a beautiful mature "oak apple" gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks like its been dipped in varnish. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2013)

I'm a little confused about the timing of these galls. This one seems fully mature, but I'm still finding fresh ones.
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da08e_da08-007.jpg
Notes:
10162782114 This is all that's left of the once pretty leaf galls on a Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. Compare this photo from last May. The galls are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp - the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera). They don't seem to do the leaves any good. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2013)

That's the interesting "life trail" of a tiny leaf-miner insect in the upper-left.
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: da08f_da08-021.jpg
Notes: 10162799046 I believe these are "cleistogamous" flowers of native pistillate Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are flowering without opening in these dry scarious heads, something they do in a very dry year like this. Some of the dry flowers actually have seed heads, but I doubt if they are fertile. This is not good for the insect pollinators - see my [Previous] few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2013)

File.Name: da09a_da09-017.jpg
Notes:
10179344584 Here are falling raindrops on the leaves of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family! I got this photo from under cover on our front porch. Rain and Madrones seem to go together, and the tree is digging it! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2013)

We had our first bit of real winter rain today! The National Weather Service kept their word - both that we would get some rain, and that it wouldn't amount to much. We ended up with 0.08 inches in our rain gauge. Not much, but it's a start.

File.Name: da09b_da09-012.jpg
Notes:
10179273496 We finally got a bit of real rain today! Not much, but it's a start. See my [Next] photo.

Here are raindrops on leaves of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. This plant catches and holds raindrops like few others, there's something about the leaves... (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2013)

File.Name: da09c_da05-046.jpg
Notes: 10179011636 This is a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family showing signs of extreme water stress in this drought year, our second in a row. It's a large tree that's growing in a disturbed place where people park their cars, and the resulting root damage may have something to do with it. It sure looks dead. Notice that the trees in the background (where no one parks) are also showing brown needles. (San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2013)

File.Name: da09d_da01-019.jpg
Notes:
10178644084 This is a drought-stressed Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family growing in the woods by the river. I can still see some green in the canopy, but it's mostly brown. The trees on either side still look good, and this is the first oak I've seen in trouble. I'm glad we finally got a bit of real rain today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)

The Forest Service did a clearing this summer on one side of the base of this tree, and maybe that's why it's in stress? This is an indication of how close the woods are to real danger. I got this photo and the [Previous] one just before the Forest Service shut down access to the river due to the ongoing government shutdown. Damn, this is just where I want to be at this time of year!

File.Name: da09e_da01-024.jpg
Notes: 10178693576 These are large clumps of Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite high in a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family by the river. But look how small and few the sycamore leaves are. It seems too early for the leaves to be showing fall color. Compare this photo from November last year. I suspect this is a sign of water stress in this drought year. I'm glad we finally got a bit of real rain today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2013)
ScientificName: Phoradendron macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Phoradendron macrophyllum; Viscaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: da09f_da03-046.jpg
Notes:
10178509643 This is a drought-stressed shrub of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family, growing in our canyon. Notice that there are still some green leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2013)

This drought year has been a tough time for the native plants, but this is what they are adapted for. I'm glad we finally got a bit of real rain today.

File.Name: da10a_da10-004.jpg
Notes:
10199070524 This is a little Snowy Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) perched on an old flower of of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I tried to nudge it into a better position to show its wide wings, but it hopped away and I lost it. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2013)

I believe this is one of our "thermometer crickets" that sings with a pulsing call that speeds up on warmer nights. Sometimes they create beautiful jams, but mostly they stay in synch with each other. Now how do they do that?

The weather has been cool lately - mostly in the low 50s, though it warmed up a bit in the afternoon. We had our first winter rain yesterday, and there was breeze today in its aftermath. I got a few new photos. "Perseverance furthers.
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: da10b_da10-014.jpg
Notes:
10199006333 Here are old seed heads of the unusual (in our mountains) native Rough-leaved Aster Aster radulinus in the Asteraceae plant family. This is the epitome of fall colors - not drought, because they always turn brown like this at this time of year. See this photo for the not-so-pretty flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2013)

I only know this native plant from this one place near home. It's a fire-follower plant, but in this case clearing the chaparral down to bare ground a few years ago was good enough to give them a place.

File.Name: da10c_da10-003.jpg
Notes: 10198693616 Here is a old frond of native Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family, showing fall colors. Note that it still has a bit of green. This is the only summer fern in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2013)

File.Name: da10d_da09-019.jpg
Notes: 10198539685 These are beautiful plants of the native grass Purple Needlegrass (Stipa pulchra aka Nassella pulchra) in the Poaceae plant family. These are growing in the garden, from local seed. They do get some summer water, and seem to do fine. I got this photo yesterday, after our first winter rain of the year. Everything looks better after a bit of rain! (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2013)

File.Name: da10e_da03-026.jpg
Notes: 10198153724 This is a nice clump of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) in the Poaceae plant family growing in the canyon. It's still green after this drought summer. You probably wouldn't want it for a lawn, but it's a real native grass that sometimes forms a grassy understory in our woods. Native grasses are uncommon. I like them a lot. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2013)

File.Name: da10f_da10-009.jpg
Notes: 10198112945 This is non-native Smilo Grass aka Rice-Millet or Millet Mountain Rice (Stipa miliacea aka Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family. The stems are tough and wiry, but at least the seeds don't stick in your socks. It's impossible to pull by hand from dry ground. I reckon this has become the worst invasive weed in our mountains. It grows along roads like other weeds, but it's also getting established in the woods and along the river. I've watched it get worse over the years. Remove it if you can. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2013)
CommonNames: Smilo Grass; Rice-Millet; Millet Mountain Rice

File.Name: da11a_da11-013.jpg
Notes:
10217493635 This is a large white female Crab Spider (genus Misumena, Thomisidae) on a pistillate (female) flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Beautiful. But what gives with that extra pair of hands/legs embracing the bottom of her abdomen? See my [Prevous photo]! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2013)

I'm heading off for the weekend. I'll be back Sunday night or maybe Monday.

File.Name: da11b_da11-016.jpg
Notes:
10216935035 Wow - talk about sexual dimorphism!

This is a large white female Crab Spider (genus Misumena, Thomisidae), with the male spider riding the hump of her back. The male has a ridiculously small body and ridiculously large front legs. I found them today on pistillate (female) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2013)

I first noticed the female with male entwined in my [Next] photo. I nudged them a bit into this pose. I tried to nudge them again into a better position, and the male dropped away. I apologize for interrupting!
ScientificName: sexual dimorphism; Misumena; Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: sexual dimorphism; Misumena; Thomisidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: da11c_da11- 002.jpg
Notes:
10216693444 This is another adult male Snowy Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Maybe it's part of the Oecanthus niveus group. It has beautiful patterns on its wings. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2013)

I believe this is one of our "thermometer crickets" that works the night shift and sings with a pulsing call that speeds up on warmer nights. Sometimes they create beautiful jams, but mostly they stay in synch with each other. Now how do they do that?
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Oecanthus niveus
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Oecanthus niveus

File.Name: da11d_da11-029.jpg
Notes:
10216472134 This is a tiny net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera. I believe it is genus Neaethus in the family Issidae. It's perched on the edge of a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It looks like a cartoon character! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2013)

I did two things I don't like to do to get this photo. I bumped the ISO up to 560 which results in some digital noise, and I used my on-camera flash. Well, it was breezy in low light. It was that or nothing.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Neaethus; Issidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Neaethus; Issidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: da11e_da11-004.jpg
Notes:
10216216844 This is the tiny gall of a "Pumpkin Gall Wasp" (Dryocosmus minusculus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It does look a bit like a tiny pumpkin or gourd. Notice that this gall does cause a color change in the leaf. I guess it is a "nutrient sink". (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2013)

How tiny is it? I'm glad you asked! This is a 1:1 800x800 crop from my original 4800x3200 photo (in DX mode) taken at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The gall is about 300 pixels wide in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the gall is 23.4x300/4800 = 1.5 mm, about 1/16 inch. Like I said, this gall is tiny!
Locality: in DX mode) taken at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens.  The gall is about 300 pixels wide in my original photo
CommonNames: Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da11f_da11-019.jpg
Notes:
10216188605 So that's why they call them coffee berries!

This is a single seed of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family that I found balanced on a leaf of the same shrub today. There are a pair of them inside each berry - see this photo. They really do look like unroasted coffee beans. I have no idea how this one got onto the leaf. It doesn't look like its been pooped from a bird. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2013)

File.Name: da13a_da13-049.jpg
Notes:
10259963666 These are new flowers of native Hayfield Tarweed (Hemizonia congesta ssp. luzulifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. This photo shows how glandular they are - probably the only reason they still survive in this cow pasture! It's an unusual (for us) late summer flower that seems to prefer serpentine soil. I've also found it flowering in the fall on serpentine soil at the "Indian mounds" near Midland School in the Santa Ynez Valley. (Perozzi Ranch, San Luis Obispo, 12 October 2013)

I was away last night at a "doin's" - secret stuff! Photo opportunities were limited, but I got a few.

File.Name: da13b_da13-044.jpg
Notes:
10259544426 I believe this is the Hayfield Tarweed (Hemizonia congesta ssp. luzulifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a remarkable native plant - still flowering at this time of year, in a cow pasture! I guess the cattle don't like it because it is quite glandular. See my [Next] photo. (Perozzi Ranch, San Luis Obispo, 13 October 2013)

This one is growing on serpentine soil. I've also found it flowering in the fall on serpentine soil at the "Indian mounds" near Midland School in the Santa Ynez Valley. I guess it like serpentine.

File.Name: da13c_da13-026.jpg
Notes: 10259383443 This is a closer photo of the "Prickly Pear Cactus" (Opuntia) in the Cactaceae plant family that I show two photos [back]. It has nasty long spines. I guess it is Opuntia phaeacantha, but there were no flowers and no fruit I could get to. It looks a lot like the yellow-flowered cactus in our garden. I wonder if it also has yellow flowers? (Perozzi Ranch, San Luis Obispo, 12 October 2013)

File.Name: da13d_da13-002.jpg
Notes: 10259045985 This is looking up into a grand Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family. They make great climbing trees is you can get past the first section of trunk! (Perozzi Ranch, San Luis Obispo, 12 October 2013)

File.Name: da13e_da13-010.jpg
Notes: 10258447716 This is the austere landscape near our event yesterday on the Perozzi Ranch near San Luis Obispo. At this time of year, it's a pretty barren landscape with cacti and Western Sycamore trees following the dry creek bed. There are a few native shrubs - Coyote Bush and Coffeeberry. You can see a few Coast Live Oak trees in the background. Those scraggly plants in the foreground are the interesting Silky Tarweed (Hemizonia congesta ssp. luzulifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. I'll show them after a few more photos. (Perozzi Ranch, San Luis Obispo, 12 October 2013)

File.Name: da13f_da13-038.jpg
Notes: 10258022634 I was at an event yesterday that happened on the Perozzi Ranch near San Luis Obispo. Some of our event happened in this grand old barn, with a horny chandelier! I like the back wall, with the sun behind. Knots, woodpeckers, bullet holes? (Perozzi Ranch, San Luis Obispo, 12 October 2013)

File.Name: da14a_da14-026.jpg
Notes:
10281987506 Butterflies first, though this is not my favorite photo today. (See this photo!)

Here's a twofer of Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterflies on late flowers of the non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii,Scrophulariaceae) that still has a few flowers in the garden. These butterflies look very different, but they are both the same in all the important ways. I guess that's a newly emerged one on the right and an older worn one on the left. Both have five eyespots on their lower wings, and more white on their forewings. This is a "tele-macro" photo with my long lens, and then heavily cropped. My long lens is ailing after a few crashes, and needs help. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2013)

"Try our new Photo Experience"

Offhand, it sucks. At least you can opt out (so far) if you make the mistake of clicking on the screen button. Flickr seems to be following the path of Fotolog, which drove me away last spring after almost 10 years. I hope this doesn't happen again.
Locality: so far) if you make the mistake of clicking on the screen button. Flickr seems to be following the path of Fotolog

File.Name: da14b_da14-012.jpg
Notes: 10281880135 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a pretty new flower of native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. You have to get down on your hands and knees to appreciate the beauty of this flower, but it is quite attractive. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2013)
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria exigua; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria exigua; Asteraceae

File.Name: da14c_da14-015.jpg
Notes:
10281314184 I was getting a photo of the "oak apple" gall on the right of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. I didn't notice until I got the photo up on my computer screen that there's also a tiny gall on the left, with a tiny Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) below it on the stem. I'll add notes to the photo to make them easier to find. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2013)

What a difference in size between the two galls, though both will hatch tiny wasps about the same size as the wasp below. Different strategies can both work.
CommonNames: California Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: da14d_da14-014.jpg
Notes:
10280896233 This is another strange little wasp gall (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on the topside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks like one of those old bottles of Chianti wine. It's only about 1/8 inch high, with an exit hole where the wasp came out. I don't know what it is. I'm just the photographer! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2013)

I think I've found this gall before in this photo, also with an exit hole. On that one, I thought it might be the gall of the Red Cone Gall Wasp (Andricus kingi), though it seems too small, the shape is wrong, and it's not red. Close, but no banana.

Maybe it's the gall of the Bullet Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis mamillana?
CommonNames: wasp gall; Scrub Oak; Bullet Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Red Cone Gall Wasp; Andricus kingi; Disholcaspis mamillana
associatedTaxa: Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Red Cone Gall Wasp; Andricus kingi; Disholcaspis mamillana

File.Name: da14e_da14-019.jpg
Notes: 10280315026 This strange little gall looks a bit like the "pinched form" of the Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed Cynipidae in the Hymenoptera family) growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae family, but the halves seem to be fused together into one piece. This gall is tiny, only about 3 mm across, about 1/8 inch. I don't know what it is. I'm just the photographer! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2013)
CommonNames: Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: da14f_da14-004.jpg
Notes: 10280164626 Here are more gall masses of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. These are on leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are made up of individual capsules and only form on the undersides of the leaves. They start bright red, then turn rusty brown like this, and finally dull brown. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da15a_da14-021.jpg
Notes:
10301354773 This is a mature acorn of a different Scrub Oak - Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It grows into mighty trees in the canyons on the north slopes of our mountains, but it grows as a scrub oak closer to home. You can see the golden fuzz on the acorn cup that is its namesake. This scrub oak has squat acorns with thin scales, and mostly entire leaves that sometimes have gold fuzz on their undersides. It also hosts a pretty good assortment of galls. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2013)

This is the photo from yesterday that sent me looking for acorns on other kinds of scrub oaks today. See my [Previous] photos. I should do the same thing for tree oaks, maybe tomorrow. It's good to have a photo quest!
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak

File.Name: da15b_da15-023.jpg
Notes: 10301114425 This is a mature acorn of a different Scrub Oak - Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens in the Fagaceae plant family. The cup scales are flat and thin, and the leaves are shiny underneath with mostly smooth margins, quite different from the more common Quercus berberidifolia in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2013)
ScientificName: Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; Quercus berberidifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; Quercus berberidifolia

File.Name: da15c_da15-006.jpg
Notes:
10300773045 This is a mature acorn of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The acorn cups are "tubercled" or bumpy, the leaves are spiny and whitish underneath. It's our most common scrub oak by far, and usually the most productive in terms of acorn production. It's an important shrub for wildlife. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2013)

This is also our most productive shrub for insect galls - see my [Previous] few photos and lots more in the archives.

File.Name: da15d_da15-015.jpg
Notes: 10300589605 This is the green form of a gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Well, it is slightly greenish, though the red is coming through. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2013)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: da15e_da15-017.jpg
Notes:
10300451125 This is a new gall of (I think) the "Dried Peach Gall Wasp" (Disholcaspis simulata) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera). It's growing on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2013)

I don't make up these names, but maybe Ron Russo does. He's the author of my book on Plant Galls of California and Other Western States (UC Press, 2006). They are mostly apt. This one really does look like a small peach!
CommonNames: Dried Peach Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Plant Galls of California and Other Western States
ScientificName: Disholcaspis simulata; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis simulata; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da15f_da15-014.jpg
Notes: 10300333225 This is another strange wasp gall (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It just looks like a lump of tissue, but it has a neat "alligator skin" pattern. I don't know what it is, but I'm sure it's the home of a Cynipid wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2013)

File.Name: da16a_da15-013.jpg
Notes:
10320129335 This is a new acorn on native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The leaves are cupped. Acorns have been scarce in this drought year, but these are the dominate tree in our woods on San Marcos Pass. These acorns have thin scales. Some years are good acorn years, other years aren't. There's a really interesting (and readable) article on "The Mystery of Masting in Trees" by Dr. Walt Koenig of the Cornell Lab of Ornithology available here. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2013)

I did go looking for other oaks today, but I found no acorns. We also have Q. Dumosa along the coast and Q. durata and Q. kelloggii on Figueroa Mountain, but not up here on the Pass.
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Q. Dumosa; Q. durata; Q. kelloggii
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Q. Dumosa; Q. durata; Q. kelloggii

File.Name: da16b_da16-024.jpg
Notes: 10319995603 This is the most unusual oak in our woods. I believe it's an unusual "Shreve Oak" (Quercus shrevei) - though it's also been called Q. parvula; or Q. parvula var. shrevei; or Q. wislizenii. It's part of the Fagaceae plant family. There is a nice copse of these trees at the sharp bend along West Camino Cielo Road, and a few more once you notice them. I did an insert of the leaves - the upper surface on the left, and the lower surface on the right. Both sides are smooth and shiny. There don't seem to be any acorns this year, at least none that I can reach with my tripod. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2013)
ScientificName: Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; Fagaceae

File.Name: da16c_da16-008.jpg
Notes: 10319555936 This is a mass of "oak apple" galls of California Gall Wasps (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. They are growing on twigs of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in the Fagaceae plant family. I didn't find any acorns on the trees today, but these galls will do. The leaves of this white oak look like real "oak leaves". (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 October 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae

File.Name: da16d_da16-019.jpg
Notes: 10319433816 This is foliage of native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's not a common tree in our area, but there are a few nice groves on the hills along the Santa Ynez River. I was looking for acorns, but there don't seem to be any in this drought year. These trees are deciduous, and the leaves have a distinctly blue-green color. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 October 2013)

File.Name: da16e_da16-023.jpg
Notes:
10319202884 Despite the closure of river access due to the government shutdown, there is still one place where you can "legally" get to the river. This is a line of river trees - willows and cottonwoods and a few sycamores. There's only a bit of water. The trees are starting to show some fall colors. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 October 2013)

I'll keep the location to myself. I want to go back tomorrow, whether the river is officially opened or not.

File.Name: da16fe_da16-011.jpg
Notes:
10318748585 This the announcement that the forest service has posted at the first river crossing at Paradise. Note that this order forbids any access to the national forest along the river, with the threat of $5,000 or 6 months in stir! There was a report on the Santa Barbara Edhat a couple of days ago that *someone* was actually handing out citations. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 October 2013)

Maybe this nonsense is finally done? I've heard rumors that "They-Who-Must-Not-Be-Named" have finally agreed to a settlement, at least until the new year. I hope the river opens again tomorrow, though I'm not holding my breath. And I found a backup - see my [Next] photo!

File.Name: da17a_da17-028.jpg
Notes:
10337503174 These are leaves of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family showing some fall colors in the canyon by Cold Springs Tavern. But note that many leaves are skipping the fall color phase and just turning brown. That's how it is in this drought year. I've seen this on native sycamores too, see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2013)

Today was another beautiful autumn day, not too hot or windy. I went back to the river today, but it's still officially "Closed Due To Government Shutdown" even though a budget agreement was signed. I guess it takes a day or two to get the machinery working again. I went to my "secret place" on the river, and I got this photo on my way home.

File.Name: da17b_da17-018.jpg
Notes:
10337076405 This is a nasty invasion of non-native Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) plants in the Fabaceae plant family on the dry wash of the river. The plant has a Cal-IPC rating of high, which means its a bad invasive weed. You can see native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) trees in the background, against a cliff of Monterey Shale. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2013)

Julie and I used to visit a secret swimming hole at the base of those cliffs a dozen years ago. (See this photo from Fotolog in 2003, one of my first uploaded photos!) I went back today, and I was astonished to see this invasion of Spanish Broom which has happened in the last decade. This is on the property of Rancho San Fernando Rey. I saw some evidence that the forest service has kept it from spreading across the property line. Maybe the next good rain will wash it all away? But maybe not...
ScientificName: Spartium junceum; Fabaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Spartium junceum; Fabaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: da17c_da15-003.jpg
Notes: 10337119653 These are glandular new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. They are the first new flowers of any native plant I've seen in this rain year. May there be many more! They are beautiful and full of hope for the new year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2013)

File.Name: da17d_da15-010.jpg
Notes:
10336740566 Here are new ripening berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. They are not ripe yet, but they are showing some color. The plants are also called Christmas Berries. These might be early for Christmas, but other plants in the mountains are still flowering. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2013)

The ripe berries are edible, but astringent. If you make an infusion in boiling water and add a bit of honey, they make a delicious drink.

File.Name: da17e_da15-021.jpg
Notes: 10336624184 Here are new ripening berries of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. Most of the new berries are too high to photograph, but this tree was within reach. The berries are edible, though a bit mealy. They have an interesting bumpy texture. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2013)

File.Name: da17f_da17-008.jpg
Notes:
10336391296 There are still late-season composite flowers of native Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash along the river. The river is still officially "Closed Due To Government Shutdown" even though an agreement was signed. I guess it takes a day or two to get the machinery working again. I got this photo at my "secret spot" that has no signs posted. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 October 2013)

I didn't see any insects visiting today. Maybe the insects are just about done - though I did spot a new-looking Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly in the garden this morning!
ScientificName: Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides; Asteraceae; Vanessa atalanta
associatedTaxa: Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides; Asteraceae; Vanessa atalanta

File.Name: da18a_da18-019.jpg
Notes:
10353755113 This is another photo of the wonderful nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera that I found today in the woods. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. It was perched of a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family today. I was surprised to find it. All insects - predators and pollinators alike - are scarce now. I wonder what prey it will find? (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2013)

This critter didn't like being photographed. It looks like a threatening pose, but I think its just turning around from the view in my [Previous] photo. After I got this photo, it disappeared down the plant stem. I found quite a few of these predators a month ago, but this is a late one.

Today was another beautiful day in paradise, not too hot or windy. I keep think today will be the day when I can't find anything new to photograph, and new things keep appearing.

File.Name: da18b_da18-018.jpg
Notes:
10353338615 This is the wonderful nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. It looks bejeweled. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. I found it perched of a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family today. I was surprised to find it. All insects - predators and pollinators alike - are scarce now. I wonder what prey it will find? (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2013)

This critter didn't like being photographed. After I got this photo, it turned around to face me and then disappeared down the plant stem. See my [Next] photo. I found quite a few of these predators a month ago, but this is a late one.

File.Name: da18c_da18-016.jpg
Notes: 10353238775 These are pretty new flowers of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family in the canyon. They keep the hummingbirds going during late summer and autumn. Very beautiful, especially the large pollen grains. I reckon these flowers have been thoroughly self-pollinated! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2013)

File.Name: da18d_da18-020.jpg
Notes:
10353003606 These are late-season male staminate flowers of the dioecious native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers look a lot like their cousin Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis), but the foliage is quite different. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2013)

I found this plant in the canyon today, but it's more common higher in the mountains. See this photo from July. It's curious that in spring the flowers start low and work their way up the mountains, while in the summer flowers start in the mountains and work their way down.
CommonNames: Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Coyote Bush

File.Name: da18e_da18-023.jpg
Notes: 10352921874 I believe this is the integral stem gall of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae (Lepidoptera). The moth causes an abrupt swelling on the stems of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family - a female pistillate plant in this case. This gall is still young. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2013)
CommonNames: Baccharis Stem Gall Moth; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: da18f_da18-028.jpg
Notes: 10352993813 These are elegant seed pods of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriatus - in the Liliaceae plant family. The flowers are long gone, but the seed pods are quite beautiful. There are still some of the flat seeds in there. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2013)
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriatus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriatus; Liliaceae

File.Name: da19a_da19-011.jpg
Notes:
10372637274 This is a tattered Behr's Metalmark (Apodemia virgulti aka Apodemia mormo virgulti) butterfly working one of the last flowers of native composite Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. I guess when the flowers run out, so do the butterflies - but both are still going. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2013)

Today was yet another beautiful in paradise, not too hot or breezy. We need some weather!
ScientificName: Apodemia virgulti; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apodemia virgulti; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: da19b_da19-038.jpg
Notes: 10372557246 These are different leaf galls on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure what critter these galls are the galls of. They make these neat little folds onto the upper surface of the leaves that look like something tasty from the bakery. I know nothing about them. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2013)

File.Name: da19c_da19-022.jpg
Notes: 10372440013 This is another "integral stem gall" on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family - with a twist! I'm not sure what critter these galls are the galls of. They cause a right-angle bend in the stem, and sometimes kill the leaves beyond. I suspect they are the galls of wasps in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. I don't know what they are, but I call them "Lumpy Galls" because of their shape. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2013)

File.Name: da19d_da19-039.jpg
Notes: 10372237743 The swelling in the stem is an "integral stem gall" on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe it's the gall of the Split Twig Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus asymmetricus) or the Tapered Stem Gall Wasp (Andricus spectabilis), both the galls of wasps in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2013)
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Split Twig Gall Wasp; Tapered Stem Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae; Dryocosmus asymmetricus; Andricus spectabilis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae; Dryocosmus asymmetricus; Andricus spectabilis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: da19e_da19-021.jpg
Notes:
10371562526 This is an interesting "woolly gall" on a stem of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe it's the gall of the Woolly Gall Wasp (Heteroecus dasydactyli) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera? The web page by Joyce Gross has photos but no info. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2013)

Remember - this gall is part of the oak shrub. It's also a wasp nest. The wasp induces the growth and there are wasp larvae inside, but it all comes from the oak.
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Woolly Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae; Heteroecus dasydactyli; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae; Heteroecus dasydactyli; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: da19f_da19-035.jpg
Notes: 10371629793 Here is another gall mass of the remarkable Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see that they are growing on the underside of an oak leaf. The mass is made up of individual capsules which each house a single wasp larva. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da20a_da20-010.jpg
Notes:
10393085675 This is a stunning California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly in the garden. This one must be newly emerged with those fantastic blue colors on its forewing. They are odd butterflies that never seem to visit flowers, but are with us all summer. I guess they are multi-brooded and emerge with a full belly and fly until they are empty. They don't feed, but they do come down to "puddle" on the damp soil and leaves in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2013)

Today was 10 degrees cooler than yesterday - low 70s rather than 80s. There's a rumor of rain next weekend, but who knows?

File.Name: da20b_da20-036.jpg
Notes: 10393117673 This is a lovely Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on late flowers of Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. These butterflies have five eyespots above and below their hindwings. I've been seeing them lately here and in the garden at home. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 October 2013)
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: da20c_da20-026.jpg
Notes: 10392747293 Here is a whole plant of Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family - still covered with flowers for the end of a very dry year. It's growing along the side of East Camino Cielo Road near the top of our mountains. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 October 2013)
ScientificName: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: da20d_da20-014.jpg
Notes: 10392425584 There are still new flowers of late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family in the woods. The long pistils and sleeve-like stamens are remarkable. See my [Previous] photo for something similar, but unwanted. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 October 2013)

File.Name: da20e_da20-019.jpg
Notes:
10392188036 Non-native Skeleton Weed (Chondrilla juncea) in the Asteraceae plant family is an unwanted weed, but it does have rather pretty flowers. They remind me of genus Stephanomeria with those long pistils and sleeve-like stamens. Make no mistake. This is a noxious weed that is just getting started in our mountains. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 October 2013)

*Sigh* There was once a time when even non-native Smilo Grass (Stipa miliacea) was just getting established in the mountains.
ScientificName: Chondrilla juncea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria; Stipa miliacea
associatedTaxa: Chondrilla juncea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria; Stipa miliacea

File.Name: da20f_da20-017.jpg
Notes:
10391994466 This is a new weed to reckon with in the Santa Barbara area. It is non-native Skeleton Weed (Chondrilla juncea) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are growing along East Camino Cielo Road in the mountains, but I've also found them near the second crossing of Paradise. They have tough stems that are very hard to pull. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 October 2013)

This is considered a type "A" noxious weed in California - "A pest of known economic or environmental detriment and is either not known to be established in California or it is present in a limited distribution that allows for the possibility of eradication or successful containment." (CDFA Encycloweedia)

File.Name: da21a_da21-025.jpg
Notes:
10413397665 This is a female (with darker wings) Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis or P. albescens) butterfly. It's working still-flowering yellow composite flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). Their host plant is the native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family, which grows nearby. I show it a few photos back. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 October 2013)

Today was another beautiful autumn day, not too hot or breezy. Our chance of rain next weekend seems to have evaporated like that last little bit of rain we had.
CommonNames: Common Checkered-Skipper; Goldenbush; Coastal Goldenbush; Desert Isocoma; Chaparral Mallow
ScientificName: Pyrgus communis; P. albescens; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia; Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae
