File.Name: da21b_da21-018.jpg
Notes: 10413245246 This is most of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on still-flowering yellow composite flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). Some bird has gotten a chunk of its hindwings, but you can see a bit of the dark gray color above. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 October 2013)
CommonNames: Gray Hairstreak; Goldenbush; Coastal Goldenbush; Desert Isocoma
ScientificName: Strymon melinus; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia

File.Name: da21c_da21-004.jpg
Notes: 10413037355 This is another tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on still-flowering native perennial Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. latifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. Well, it's still flowering in our garden - where it gets some summer water and does very well. The deer don't seem to like it much. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2013)

File.Name: da21d_da20-039.jpg
Notes: 10412855055 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working pretty new late-season flowers of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. The unopened flowers on the right look like little egg yolks. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 October 2013)

File.Name: da21e_da21-036.jpg
Notes:
10412660424 The last crossing of Paradise...

This is all that's left of the swimming hole at the last river crossing of Paradise Road, just before the parking area before the walk to Red Rock. There is still a bit of water, Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii, Salicaceae) trees showing some fall color, and dark Camuesa Peak in the background. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 October 2013)

File.Name: da21f_da21-016.jpg
Notes: 10412505046 There are still new flowers on the native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family growing along the river. You can see a tiny ant (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera) on the flower on the left. This is the host plant for the White Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis or P. albescens) butterfly that I show in my first photo today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 October 2013)
ScientificName: Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Pyrgus communis; P. albescens
associatedTaxa: Malacothamnus fasciculatus; Malvaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Pyrgus communis; P. albescens

File.Name: da22a_da21-001.jpg
Notes:
10431694975 Here is a tiny spider on cactus spines in our garden this morning. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens, as close as I can get. The body of the spider is about 400 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the spider is 23.4x400/4800 = 1.95 mm, a bit over 1/16 inch. Like I said, this spider is tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2013)

Today was another beautiful autumn day, with some real clouds in the sky. I thought I saw virga - raindrops that don't make it to the ground - falling like thin veils of mist from the clouds. I'm glad it's raining somewhere!

File.Name: da22b_da22-015.jpg
Notes:
10431374556 Fall color is where you find it!

This is fall foliage of the unusual deciduous native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family, showing some color in the woods today. Our woods are mostly evergreen in the mountains, but there are a few plants which give us a taste of fall. This is a remarkable shrub that has wonderful spring flowers. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2013)

File.Name: da22c_da22-004.jpg
Notes:
10431100664 This is a female pistillate shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family - going to seed. We'll soon find the dandelion-type seeds everywhere. It's kind of a weedy shrub, but also a California native. It deserves some respect for flowering at the end of summer and growing in waste places - as well as our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2013)

There's some debate about whether the flowers are pollinated by insects or wind. I have no doubt they are insect pollinated because they produce nectar. And the seeds are definitely dispersed by the wind!

File.Name: da22d_da22-009.jpg
Notes: 10430998974 This is the dandelion-type seed head of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a weed along our road, but also a California native. It deserves some respect for flowering at the end of summer and growing on roadsides and waste places. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2013)

File.Name: da22e_da21-008.jpg
Notes: 10430904955 These are strange "flowers" of non-native Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. They look like little paper parasols. It's a weed, though I don't notice it very often in our area. These plants are usually the host for tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterflies, but for some reason I haven't seen the butterflies in this drought year. This place was lightly burned in the White Fire last May, which may have something to do with it. This is a new plant, which has come up and flowered since the fire. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 October 2013)
CommonNames: Russian Thistle; Tumbleweed; Western Pygmy Blue

File.Name: da22f_da06-034.jpg
Notes:
10430462834 I believe this is non-native Lanceleaf Nightshade (Solanum lanceolatum) in the Solanaceae plant family. They have small red chili-like fruit. (They look like chilis, I don't know about taste. It has poisonous relatives.) It's listed as a "list B" noxious weed in California - "Control required in nurseries, control elsewhere at the discretion of local County Agricultural Commissioner." There's a colony of these weeds growing along Gibraltor Road in the mountains that's been here for some time. They don't seem to be spreading much. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 6 October 2013)

I've been sitting on this photo for a while. I figure I'd better post it or forget it.

File.Name: da23a_da23-014.jpg
Notes:
10449691074 I believe this is a goofy-looking Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera. I found it today on foliage of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. They are quite small, maybe 1/2 inch long or a bit more. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2013)

They vary remarkably in color. Compare this photo and this one. I rarely find them, but that's no sign of how common they are. They are small and easy to overlook.

This is the hardest part of the year to get now photos every day - after most of the insects, and before the mushrooms appear. I'm taking it one day at a time, and still finding surprises like this critter!
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: da23b_da23-015.jpg
Notes:
10449347144 I believe this is a goofy-looking Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera. I found it today on foliage of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. They are quite small, maybe 1/2 inch long or a bit more. This one stuck its head in the shade, but you can see its hind parts. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2013)

They vary remarkably in color. Compare this photo and this one. I rarely find them, but that's no sign of how common they are. They are small and easy to overlook.

File.Name: da23c_da23-006.jpg
Notes:
10449243783 This is a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae. I think it's too dark to be a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) - but it sure has a cat face on it's abdomen! This one was resting on the eyelet of the hammock on our upstairs porch this morning, in the shade. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2013)

What's scarier than spiders for Halloween? Having no spiders! It feels like the silent spring that Rachel Carson wrote about many years ago. These large orb-weavers should be everywhere right now, but this is the first and only one I've seen in this drought year. I'm not surprised, as there are few insects to serve as prey.

File.Name: da23d_da23-022.jpg
Notes: 10448965674 Here are beautiful yellow flowers and seed pods of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family, with burnt twigs in the background from the Gap Fire a few years ago. I sometimes half-joke that there are always flowers on Bush Poppy *somewhere* in the mountains, but this drought year will be a test. So far, so good. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2013)

File.Name: da23e_da23-017.jpg
Notes: 10448825216 This is a mass of new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. They are the first new flowers of any native plant in this rain year. Most of the shrubs are still dormant after our long dry summer, but this one is going full-on. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2013)
